detail: Driven ashore and broken up by storm. The tug CAROLINE WILLIAMS stripped her in mid-Nov after several failed attempts to pull her off the beach. Homeport:Cheboygan, MI

name: ADELAIDE HORTON
detail: She struck bottom while entering the harbor, broached to and was washed up on the beach. She was reported a total loss. Out of Kincardine or Goderich. Master: Capt. Duncan Rowan. She reportedly pounded to pieces quickly, but was actually raised in 1873, her engine going into the new propeller VANDERBILT (qv). Her hull was reportedly converted into a towbarge at Chatham.

name: LEWIS HOTCHKISS
build_info: 1870, Morrill, Collingwood (or Goderich or Owen Sound)
detail: Downbound in tow of the tug WALES, she was cut loose and then wrecked in a gale. Her crewmen were able to escape in her boat and made it to shore near Port Blake, Ont. A large section of her deck washed ashore near Goderich. Out of Toronto, owned by A. H. Campbell *also given as Sep 5, but first reports of her loss were on Sep. 2. She was the largest lumber vessel on the lakes from the time she was built ’til 1886, when the WAHNIPITAE(qv) was built

name: H. HOUGHTEN
other_names: often seen spelled H. HOUGHTON in error
detail: She burned to the waterline in the early morning, a total loss. Sunk in a collision at the S end of L. St. Clair Sep 9, 1902 with a cargo of stone – two lives were lost. Converted to a sandsucker in 1920.

name: GEORGE G. HOUGHTON
place_of_loss: NNW of Bar Pt., SE of Detroit
detail: Opened her seams and sank in a storm. Lay in relatively shallow water, obstructing the edge of the channel, so she was removed the next year.

name: HOWARD
other_names: built as ADMIRAL D.D. PORTER renamed 1889
place_of_loss: Victoria Isl off Cloud Bay [S of Thunder Bay]
detail: Bound Thunder Bay for Cloud Bay, Ont., when she stranded in fog, then burned to a total loss. Sold Canadian in 1877 [C#74376], returned to US reg in 1889 after a fire at Port Huron. One source: she was the oldest vessel on L Sup. when lost. *other reliable sources say she was built at Philadelphia Rebuilt 1878, major repairs, 1882, 1904. Former navy gunboat and transport

name: CHARLES HOWARD
detail: Went ashore in a gale, along with a number of other vessels. A quickly assembled group of rescuers were able to remove her crew. The rescuers included 3 ship captains and the famous shippers N.K. Fairbanks and Charley Bissell. Master: Capt. Mahoney. Ashore at Racine and declared a total loss in Sep, 1850

name: FLORENCE HOWARD
detail: Bound Wellington for West Point, Ont., she sprung a leak shortly after leaving port. While her crew worked the pumps to keep her afloat, she made for Weller’s Bay. Before she made it, the inflow of water overwhelmed her pump’s capacity and she sank. Owned by Savage of Belleville, Ont. Thoroughly rebuilt, 1880

name: HATTIE HOWARD
place_of_loss: near the lighthouse at Oswego, NY
detail: Inbound from Port Hope in a gale, she broached to, struck the W pier, stranded and broke up. Her crew was rescued by the Lifesaving Service. Owned by Henry Howard of Port Huron.

name: HENRY HOWARD
place_of_loss: Harsen’s Isl. mouth of St. Clair R.
detail: Bound for St. Ignace with barge GEORGE WORTHINGTON in tow. Caught fire in transit, beached on the island, where she burned herself out. Out of Port Huron, owned by J. Miner [wife of skipper] and B. Hoose

name: R.L. HOWARD
detail: Bound Toronto fro Port Dalhousie, she sprung a leak and sank about 15 miles from Port D. She was reportedly in poor condition. The scows of her tow were rescued by the small tug YOUNG LION. *year also reported in error as 1868, 69

name: A. HOWES
other_names: also seen as ABNER HOWES and just HOWES
detail: She was trying to ride out a storm off Ahnapee when she dragged her anchor and came in, wrecing on the north side of the north pier. She was owned at Racine. Capsized on Lake Erie in 1866.

name: THOMAS H. HOWLAND
detail: Tow of the tug DANFORTH, she had made it less than four miles out of Buffalo when she was discovered to have been cut through by ice. Her crew safely escaped in her yawl and the boat went down fifteen minutes later. Master: Capt. Jacob Jacobson. Though in fairly deep water, she was later raised and towed into Buffalo but it was found that the wreckers who raised her had damaged her considerably in removing her cargo. Court cases followed and it was months before it was decided that she could be repaired. Abandoned in 1921. She sprung a leak after leaving Manitowoc and made a run for St. James Bay, Beaver Island in a storm in November, 1872. She began to sink and her skipper (Capt Griffin) and mate went ashore for help. The local lightkeeper set out with the mate in a small boat to rescue the crew, but both were drowned in the attempt. Both the HOWLAND and the remainder of her crew survived. Major repairs, 1881 & 83
sources: nsp,nl,hr, lt —-——————————— JESSE HOYT – See MARY D. AYER

name: HUBBARD
place_of_loss: off Little Black Lake, near Muskegon, Mich
detail: Bound Buffalo for Chicago, she was driven ashore by a very destructive gale. Three local men rescued her crew by rowboat, despite great difficulties. By Nov 12 the schooner was declared a total loss and was being stripped of what little of value was left. Out of Chicago, owned by Andrew McGraw.
sources: nsp,mv,wl,wmhs,wmn,cbt —-——————————— BELA HUBBARD – former entry eliminated in favor of HENRY HUBBARD, below

name: HENRY HUBBARD
other_names: some sources say her name was BELA HUBBARD
detail: Capsized and foundered, no detail. Master: Capt. Pearson(d). Future iron magnate Peter White was part of her crew according to some later accounts. No further registrations of the vessel have been located, but there is a report that she was wintering at Detroit in 1845-6. Maybe the vessel built 1842 by D.M. Hagedon at Port Huron, MI., 59x17x6, 53 t., but this vessel was still extant in 1846.

name: J.C. HUBBARD
other_names: none sometimes seen as J.G. HUBBARD
detail: This little ferry was sunk in the river. There is no record of her use or recovery afterwards.

name: NEHEMIAH HUBBARD
detail: Arrived in the offing at Conneaut, but was unable to enter the harbor and sank in a storm outside. When the wreck was examined, her boat was missing, so her crew may have survived, but it was not reported so in the papers. Her regular captain was absent, and the vessel was run by the mate as master and a saltwater skipper as mate. Master: acting Capt. Olney Hatch(d) [regular master ashore].

name: NEWELL HUBBARD
place_of_loss: between Bar Point and Bois Blanc (Bob-Lo) Island
detail: Bound Pelee Island for Detroit, she suddenly sprung a leak and foundered in 25 feet of water. Her crew escaped. She was stripped and towed close to the Canadian bank by Oct 7 and was blown up four days later. Owner and master: Capt John Denstead. Out of Detroit.

name: H.S. HUBBELL
place_of_loss: 20 mi ENE of Poverty Isl, Little Bay de Noc
detail: An exploding galley lantern caught her afire and she burned quickly to a total loss. She was abandoned by her crew, who were picked up by the steamer NEW ORLEANS. Owned by John Spry, et.al. of Chicago.

name: L.M. HUBBY
carrying: in ballast [listed as 80 tons of sand]
detail: She capsized in a gale and sank in about 30 minutes. Four men launched her boat and tried to save the others, but the boat was pushed away by heavy wind. One from the boat was the only survivor, having made a 3-day trek from the shore opposite the wreck site to a settlement in Sleeping Bear Bay. Master: Capt. Roberts(d).

name: HUDSON
detail: A ship was seen to have foundered 3 miles off shore on the date above, but it was three days before the loss was confirmed and the ship identified as the HUDSON. Her cargo probably shifted in big seas [as flaxseed especially was prone to do] and she capsized and sank in a teriffic gale. She had fore & aft tandem stacks and a green & grey paint job which made her one of the most recognizable boats on the Lakes. Sister of steamer HARLEM. Outbound from Duluth. Master: Capt. A. J. McDonald(d).

name: BRUCE HUDSON
type_at_loss: propeller, steel, tanker built as tanker-barge
build_info: 1935, Horton Steel Works, Fort Erie, Ont.
detail: She caught fire while loading gasoline and exploded. Her skipper, Capt. Ross Hindman, his 16-year old son and two crewmen were killed when the vessel exploded. Owned by Lloyd Tankers, Ltd., Toronto. She was later recovered towed to Muir Shipyard, Port Dalhousie, and rebuilt . Capsized July 13, 1935 on Lake Ontario and towed to Toronto upside-down. Lost from tow and abandoned on Lake Ont. in Nov of 1935. Recovered by the steamer BRULIN. Converted to a steamer in 1939. Lengthened in 1946 and 1957. Size in 1967:246x30x15 1319g 933n

name: HENDRICK HUDSON
build_info: 1846, G.W. Jones, Cleveland or Black R., OH
place_of_loss: at Cleveland at Otis & Co. Rolling Mill Dock*
detail: She was totally destroyed by fire and sank after being struck by lightning. Vessel had been lying idle at her dock for several years and was of low value. Owner: F. G. Stone, Cleveland. Wreck removed in August of 1869. *also seen as Buffalo.

name: SARAH E. HUDSON
other_names: also seen as S. E. HUDSON, E. K. HUDSON
detail: Bound Milwaukee for Buffalo, she came in collision with the propeller ECLIPSE at 4 or 5 am and sank quickly in 18 fathoms of water. Preparations were made to raise her, but they were foiled by a storm, and she was left for the winter. The wreck, reported as a new vessel, was visited by the Coast Wrecking Co. tug RESCUE in the summer if 1871 for possible recovery, but there is no record that she was ever recovered. Owned by Elon W. Hudson, Detroit Master: Capt. James Harvie

name: JAMES HUGHES
detail: Ashore and total loss near Muskegon. She was stranded with heavy damage just a couple weeks before at Pentwater. Owner: Charles Mears, Chicago.

name: MARY ANN HULBERT
place_of_loss: off St. Ignace Isl., 40 mi NE of Thunder Bay
detail: In tow of steamer KINCARDINE (qv), she was overwhelmed and foundered in a heavy gale. She had been bound for Michipicoten Isl. from Port Arthur, Ont. In terms of loss of life, this is the worst sailing ship disaster in Lake Superior history. Master: Capt. Martin O’Malley. *Crew of 5 plus 15 railroad workers as passengers.

name: HUMBOLDT
type_at_loss: scow-schooner, wood [reportedly had a flat bottom, but a sharp bow]
detail: Sank, no detail. May have been recovered. Out of Chicago

name: THOMAS HUME
other_names: built as schooner H.C. ALBRECHT, renamed in 1883
detail: Bound Chicago for Muskegon, she disappeared with all hands in heavy weather. Though rewards were offered for information about her loss, no trace of the vessel or crew was ever found. A vessel thought to be her was discovered off New Buffalo in 1905. Owned by Hackley & Hume of Muskegon. Master: Capt. Harry Albrightson(d). Major repair in 1884

name: HUMKO
place_of_loss: 6 mi off Two Rivers Point, WI
detail: Caught fire, destroyed and sank. Date also given as 1856 in error.

name: GEORGE M. HUMPHREY
other_names: renamed CAPTAIN JOHN ROEN (1945), ADAM E CORNELIUS (1948), CONSUMERS POWER (1958)
detail: She collided with 5,500 ton steamer D.M. CLEMSON in fog and sank. Her recovery was the most expensive salvage job up to that time. Included on lists of U. S. World War II war losses. Converted to a self-unloader in 1948, reengined in 1955

name: HUNGARIAN
build_info: 1853, Asa Wilcox, Three Mile Bay, NY as a bark
detail: She was driven on a rocky shore by a gale and became a total loss. Her outfit was later recovered, and the vessel was abandoned Nov 1, her document being surrendered at Chicago, Nov 15. Owned by David Dall of Chicago. Master: P. W. Costello. Rebuilt in 1871

name: S. D. HUNGERFORD
place_of_loss: E of Hungerford Point, Manitoulin Isl
detail: She was cut loose from the tug GLADIATOR after the tug developed engine trouble in a gale. She dropped her anchors in a futile effort to remain in deep water. She finally struck W of Owen Island and sank, but her crew was able to make it ashore, where they survived the rugged coast for 19 days. Wrecked with the loss of three lives at Port Maitland in 1874.

name: HUNTER
build_info: 1857, Bidwell & Banta, Buffalo as a propeller
place_of_loss: near Greenbush, S of Harrisville, MI
detail: Upbound, she was driven ashore and broke up after being lost from tow of prop DETROIT (qv). Rebuilt from a propeller to a barge in 1870 after sinking in a collision with the prop COMET(qv) in 1869 on the Detroit R. The engine went to convert the sidewheel steamer DETROIT (US# 6189) to a propeller. This is the same DETROIT that was towing her at the time of her loss. Major repair in 1862 after a major fire at Chicago. Two lives were lost.

name: HUNTER
detail: She capsized at a dock while unloading and sank, along with a steampump and boiler that were on deck. She was later raised, but never used again. Owned by Milwaukee Tug Co.

name: HUNTER
detail: Stranded in a storm and became a total loss while bound Whitehall, Mich., for Michigan City. Value of vessel and cargo only $278. Out of Grand Haven or Michigan City

name: HUNTER
place_of_loss: in harbor at Grand Marais, MI
detail: Stranded in the harbor, caught fire and destroyed before she could be released. Owned by Booth Packing Co.

name: ELVIRA HUNTER
detail: foundered – no detail

name: MAGGIE HUNTER
other_names: built as JOHN S. CLARK, renamed, 1876
detail: Bound Oswego for Toronto, she sailed into a gale and foundered. Wreckage which washed ashore the 11th, including an oval cabin window, was unidentified for quite some time, but was finally attributed to her. She was reportedly very deep in the water when she left Oswego, many said she was overloaded. Owned by Samuel Hunter of Toronto. Master: Capt. Frank Nixon(d).

name: WILLIAM HUNTER
place_of_loss: Niagara Reef, near Middle Sister Isl.
detail: Bound Toledo for Buffalo, this small schooner went ashore on the reef and jammed tight. Though tugs tried to save her, by the 31st she had broken in two and was abandoned as a total loss. Out of Cleveland, owned by Doville Bros. Master: Capt. Doville. Still listed in some later sources later (mv), but probably in error.

name: HUNTING BOY
place_of_loss: one end of Sturgeon Bay canal
detail: She was driveen ashore between the piers and wrecked, a total loss. She was destroyed with dynamite in July, 1880. Owned by E. B. Graham of Fish Creek.

name: CHARLES H. HURD
other_names: also seen as C.H. HURD (also reported as J.L. HURD)
type_at_loss: schooner, wood, 3-mast (mv shows her as a steamer)
detail: She left Chicago the 21st and sprang a leak and foundered rapidly in a gale the next day. The only survivor was Capt. W.O. Harrison; his wife, children & brother died on the wreck. Owned out of Detroit by her master, Capt. W. O. Harrison, and John Hosmer.

name: JOSEPH L. HURD
build_info: 1869, Campbell & Owen, Detroit as a package freighter hull# 10
place_of_loss: 9 mi NW of Sturgeon Bay, WI
detail: Bound for Manistee from Sturgeon Bay in tow of a tug when she broke loose and stranded. At first thought to be repairable, but finally declared a constructive total loss and scuttled for a dock facing later Owned by Leatham & Smith, Sturgeon Bay. Heavily damaged in a fire June 6 of the same year. She sank following a collision with the prop CAYUGA(qv) near Skillagallee, May 10, 1895. 1 life lost. Sold off the beach by auction. Her hog braces were removed during the rebuild. Sad story: jettisoned a deck cargo of 100 kegs of beer during a gale in the Straits in Nov, 1879. Rebuilt in 1882, 1895. Rebuilt to a bulker from a passenger/package freighter in 1891
media_notes: Image as package freighter from Walter Lewis website

name: CHAUNCY HURLBUT
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, bulk and package freight
detail: Began to leak badly, she was run ashore to prevent her sinking. Her crew was taken off by lifesavers before she pounded to pieces. Also heavily damaged in a fire in 1889 at W. Superior, WI. Rebuilt at Detroit in 1900
sources: lssc,lss,is,gwgl,ns1,h,mv,mpl,nsp —-——————————— HURON – See also COLONIAL, HURONTON

name: HURON
place_of_loss: E shore of Bruce Peninsula, Georgian Bay
detail: Stranded and lost. Homeport: Saugeen

name: HURON
detail: Wrecked and declared total loss. No detail. Owned by Dallas of Owen Sound. Recovered in 1886 and rebuilt almost completely (assigned new Official no. – C90569 – but it was later cancelled), broken up in 1905.

name: HURON
detail: Tow of the steamer LOTHAIR and bound McKellar’s Island for Buffalo, she foundered in a summer storm. She was owned by Thompson & Co., Windsor, Ont.

name: HURON CITY
place_of_loss: at Sandwich, Ont. (part of Windsor now)
detail: She was totally destroyed in a fire. Lay on the riverbank until she was stripped and abandoned the following year.

name: HURONTON
other_names: built as propeller HURON, renamed in 1920
build_info: 1898, Lorain, OH (also seen as Cleveland Shipbldg, Cleve.) US# 96425
detail: Rammed by the 4700 t. prop CETUS (US#200374) in fog. CETUS skipper kept his engine running full ahead and kept the steamer’s nose in the hole caused by the collision long enough for the HURONTON’s crew to scramble aboard. When CETUS was backed away, the other sank quickly, in 800′ of water. Sold Canadian, 1920.

name: HURRICANE
place_of_loss: 8 mi N of St. Joseph, MI
detail: The HURRICANE was driven ashore and wrecked in a gale that destroyed or damaged over 60 vessels across the breadth of the lakes. She had been bound Chicago for Buffalo. Owned by Sears & Clark of Buffalo. Master: Capt. Wm. Webb or Welsh(d).

name: HUSTLER
build_info: 1846, Samuel Farmin, Milwaukee as a brig
detail: Bound Green Bay for Chicago, she tried to put in to Kenosha to pick up hay. She struck the pier and and was spun about, breaking her back in the process. The tug UNION from Chicago was ordered to her assisitance but was delayed by weather, and by the time shemade Kenosha the schooner was already breaking up. Out of Chicago, owned by Nelson Ludington. Converted from brig to schooner after 1865.

name: CHARLES L. HUTCHINSON
other_names: built as BUTCHER BOY[US#2149], name changed in 1881, also seen as C.L. HUTCHINSON
detail: She broke up and foundered in a SW gale while anchored to ride out a storm. Her crew made it ashore in her boat. Owner: Nelson Luddington. Other accounts saying she was lost near Port Colborne are in error. Rebuilt, 1881, renamed and renumbered.
sources: mv,nsp,sol,h,wb,hgl,sb,mpl,es3-1,hr —-——————————— CHARLES L. HUTCHINSON – See also KINSMAN INDEPENDENT, WILLIAM C. MORELAND —-——————————— GENE C. HUTCHINSON – See WILLIAM C. MORELAND

name: JOHN M. HUTCHINSON
build_info: 1873, Quayle & Martin, Cleveland as a schooner
detail: She opened her seams and sank in a heavy weather, going down in deep water. The crew were saved by her tow vessel, the steamer CALEDONIA. After the accident members of her crew claimed she was overloaded. Part of the Corrigan Fleet, Cleveland. Built for Capt. Frank Perew at a cost of $80,000 and was once the wonder of the lakes.

name: H.S. HYDE
detail: She sank off the point in a gale, but her crew was rescued by the local USLS lifesavers. Document surrendered 12/15/83, shown in 1884 Merchant Vessels as “lost or otherwise out of service.” Owned by Mannell Bros. of Port Huron

name: HYDRUS
other_names: built as steamer R.E. SCHUCK or SHUCK, renamed after 1910
detail: Overwhelmed and sunk by the “Big Storm” of 1913 while making for the St Clair R. & shelter. Winds in the area were reported gusting over 90 mph, with waves over 35 feet high. Master: Capt. James Lowe(d). Wreck located in Fall, 2015.

name: HYPHEN
detail: She went ashore on Pt. Pelee about the 23 rd in a storm. The tug B. B. JONES succeeded in pulling her off the reef, but she became unmanageable, capsized and foundered when she had only been pulled a mile toward Detroit. Three of her temporary crew, including the engineer manning her pumps, were drowned when she went down. Owned by Valentine Fries, Milan, OH
sources: nsp,rsl,mv,hgl,ec Share this: Share on X (Opens in new window) Share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Facebook Like Loading...

name: ICEBERG
detail: Bound Cleveland for Kingston, she went missing in a summer storm. She left the Welland Canal the 12 th and never arrived at Kingston. The vessel was built by Dr.John Rae for arctic exploration purposes [she was supposedly exceedingly strongly built] and was supposed to be used on the lakes for the balance of the season, then sailed to England where she would be prepared for her arctic voyage in the spring of 1858. She was slated to look for remains of the ill-fated Franklin Arctic Expedition, with which Rae had been associated. She lasted slightly more than a month. Some of her wreckage drifted ashore in September.

name: ICEBIRD
detail: Sank following a collision. May refer to loss of ICEBERG.

name: IDA
detail: She was wrecked on Catawba, colliding with a dock and holing herself in a storm. She was soon stripped of her engines and gear and was towed away and abandoned, but later washed back ashore on Catawba. In July, 1900, the little steamer was struck by a squall and was not powerful enough to oppose the winds. As she dragged ashore, her crew was rescued by the Marblehead Lifesaving crew and the tug MONK. Out of Mt. Clemens, MI

name: IDA
detail: Bound for Chicago, she capsized and sank in a gale. Her crew were rescued by a passing vessel. Major repairs in 1880

name: IDA CAROLINE
build_info: 1902, Charles F. Williams, Milwaukee as a 2-mast schooner
detail: No detail. Homeport: Milwaukee

name: IDA & MARY
place_of_loss: near Sturgeon Point, W of Buffalo, NY
detail: She was driven ashore and went to pieces in a gale, a total loss. Document not surrendered until 1879. Owned by Andrew Bates of Cleveland Also sank on Lake Ontario with 2 lives lost in 1859.

name: IDAHO
detail: She foundered in a gale. Two crewmen clambered up to the tip of her tall mast as she went down and clung there for 12 hours. In what must be one of the great feats of shiphandling of all time, the skipper of the bulker MARIPOSA brought his big ship right up to the wooden mast and plucked them off in mountinous waves. Owned by Western Transit Co. Master: Capt. Alexander Gillies(d) – his body came ashore near Port Maitland 355 days later. She had just come out October 22, after having been out of commision for several years. Ashore at 14-mile Point, near Ontonagon, Mich and expected to become a total loss, June 8, 1890. major repairs in 1874,75 & 80

name: IDAHO
detail: Sunk in the lower lock of Milan Canal and became a total loss. Location of loss also given as Welland Canal

name: IDLEHOUR
detail: She burned at 2 am at her winter layup dock – a total loss. Owned by W. J. Ziegele, Buffalo, she had been in the excursion business in that area for a number of years.

name: IDLER
other_names: built as O. B. REED, renamed in 1893
detail: Burned to a total loss. Owned by E. L. Kingsbury, Sandusky, Oh Built to run on the Wolf River, Wisconsin, moved to Toledo in 1894.

name: IDLER
detail: The large yacht of millionaire fleet owner James Corrigan was caught in a squall with her sails out full and capsized. The six drowned passengers consisted of Capt. Corrigan’s wife, his 3 daughters, a neice and an infant grandchild. The captain of the yacht was later arrested for manslaughter in mishandling the vessel. All but one of the drowned were in the cabin. All of the crew and one passenger survived. Master: Capt. Chas. J. Holmes. The yacht was recovered soon after. Owner Corrigan subsequently equipped a scow with a glass bottom and strong electric lights with which to search the bottom in the area for his daughters’ bodies. Rebuilt 1873, 1876 and 1890. Came to the lakes in 1879. The yacht once competed in the America’s Cup race {1870}.

name: IDLER
detail: She caught fire from an explosion in the engine room and was soon engulfed in flame. She was about a mile and a half north of the lighthouse. The crew had to abandon her in her boat and made it to shore safely. A tug towed the boat in but she was found to be a total loss. Owned by McCann Bros., Beaver Island and registered out of Grand Haven.

name: ILLINOIS
detail: Bound Cleveland for Detroit, she was struck by a heavy squall and began leaking. Her crew worked the pumps while the vessel was run for shore. She sank five miles short, crew abandoning in her yawl. Reportedly the oldest working vessel on the lakes at the time. Also wrecked on Lake Erie in 1851.

name: ILLINOIS
build_info: 1849, Bidwell & Banta, Buffalo [also shown as Detroit]
detail: Bound Detroit for Buffalo, she was rammed by the big package freighter DEAN RICHMOND and sank quickly in a passing error. RICHMOND ran full ahead and pushed her into 15 feet of water as she was going down. At first it was thought that she could be recovered, but she broke up in July. Machinery and cargo were later salvaged by the wrecking tug MAGNET. Master: Capt. T. Fupell. Owned by the Buffalo and Detroit Navigation Line. Stranded and thought a total loss of N. Fox Island, L. Michigan, late Nov., 1851. Major repairs in 1861,

name: ILLINOIS
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, bulk freight “propeller barge” [i.e. steam barge]
detail: She was not able to maintain steam in a terrific storm, reportedly due to a bunker full of inferior quality coal. She became unmanageable and finally foundered, overwhelmed by the storm. Her crew made it to shore in her smallboats. Reportedly owned by Ives, Windsor, Ont., but still shows on US lists. Master: Capt. Ives. She was built for Oliver Newberry of Detroit as a sidewheeler. Eventually she was stripped of her engine and abandoned near Springwells, but was raised about 1863 and re-engined as a propeller.

name: ILLINOIS
detail: Tow of the prop IRON CITY, she broke loose in s torm, drove ashore and was wrecked. Owned by M. B. Kean of Detroit and home ported out of Marine City. As a passenger steamer, she struck a reef off Eagle River, Michigan, Lake Superior, and quickly sank in July of 1864. Thought to be a total loss, but was recovered easily a week later. Her passengers, crew and freight were rescued by the steamer CLEVELAND. Built as a sidewheeler, converted to a steambarge in 1863 at the Stupinsky yard, Detroit, and may have been converted to an unpowered barge in 1868 or 9 after wrecking at Marine City.

name: ILLINOIS
detail: Drove ashore at Smith’s Pier in a gale and broke up. Also sunk in a collision with the bark NEWSBOY on a line between Grand Haven and Muskegon on May 29, 1868. Crew rescued by NEWSBOY. Major repair in 1871, probably after cutting in front of and being run down by the Canadian bark BRITISH LION on Lake Mich in May, 1870.

name: IMPERIAL
detail: Capsized and sank, “total loss”

name: IMPERIAL
detail: Bound Chicago for Midland, Ont., she struck a rock near Western Islands and began to leak heavily. She ran for Hope Island but sank 4 miles out in 200 feet of water. She was going 10.5 mph in a stiff breeze when she struck. Her crew abandoned in the yawl and made it to Hope Island lighthouse. Out of Manitowoc, owned by Johnson. Master: Capt. A. Warden. Rebuilt in 1875-6

name: INDEPENDENCE
place_of_loss: off Black River [site of Lorain], OH
detail: Dismasted and capsized in a storm, going down with all hands. Master: Capt. J. Brooks (d). Out of Sandusky, Oh

name: INDEPENDENCE
build_info: 1843, R.C. Bristol, Chicago (builder also given as J. Banta, J. Averill)*
detail: Her boiler exploded violently and she sank in the river. One crewman was blown 2-300 feet into the air and lived. Parts of her wreck have been recovered and made into souvenirs at various times. The remains of her boiler were fished up in April of 1899. Master: Capt. John McKay, who owned her with Sheldon McKnight of Detroit.. She was the first prop built on L. Mich and first steam craft on L. Superior. She was portaged around St.Mary’s Rapids in 7 weeks in the winter of 1845-6. She used a tremendous amount of fuel in going very slowly and had to stop for fuel every few leagues, until re-engined in 1844. After that she was much faster, making a trip from Soo to LaPointe and back in less than a week in 1847. Ran ashore near LaPointe, WI in the fall of 1852 and was not recovered until October of 1853. *Year of build often given as 1846, but she is mentioned in newspapers before 1844 and 43 is on her official documents.

name: INDIA
place_of_loss: Georgian Bay, near Little Current, Ont.
detail: Caught fire under her boiler, burned to the waterline and sank off West Mary I., 7.9 mi E of Little Current. She had been loading pulpwood and repairing damage from an earlier storm when the fire was discovered. Her crew was rescued by a local boat which was attending the loading process. Owned by P. & W. Ramsey, Soo, Ont. Wreck is visible on Google Earth. Link: India wreck Also sank with heavy damage in the Welland Canal in 1922.

name: INDIAN
other_names: built as CINCINNATI, later CITY OF HAMILTON, renamed ALPS in 1857 and INDIAN in 1858
build_info: 1853, Thomas Wicks or Geo. S. Weeks, Buffalo
detail: Destroyed by fire at her layup dock, a few days after tying up for the winter. She was a total loss of about $5,000. Owned by Fraser; homeport at Montreal. Sold Canadian in 1855 or 8 Ran ashore on E. Sister reef, Lake Erie in fine weather while bound for Toledo with pig iron, May, 1865. Heavy damage. As CINCINNATI, she went ashore near Forestville, Mich, in December, 1854 and was expected to be a total loss. She was recovered the following May and extensively rebuilt at Detroit. Major repairs in 1861, 63. Rebuilt 1880 and reduced from 141×26, 452 t.

name: INDIAN
detail: Foundered in the great “Armistice Day Storm.” One of two sturdy fish tugs lost in that storm in the area [see also RICHARD H.] The two had just left South Haven on the 1 st day of trout season when they were struck by a violent storm. Neither were ever seen again. Originally steam-powered, but re-engined with a Kahlenberg oil engine in 1936. Link to photo and info Link to online article

name: INDIAN QUEEN
loss_of_life_original: 20 or none [newspapers of the time don’t mention lives lost, later sources do]
detail: Driven ashore and broke in two the same night as the steamer HELEN STRONG(qv). Total loss.

name: INDIANA
detail: She went ashore and reported a total loss of $3,000..

name: INDIANA
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: Struck an obstruction, then caught fire and burned to a total loss of about $20,000. This is probably the vessel that was reported to have struck a snag and sunk in Maumee Harbor in 1845. Engraving from Stanton, American Steam Vessels(1895), Walter Lewis website

name: INDIANA
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, bulk and package freight
carrying: iron ore. This was called at the time the first cargo of Lake Superior iron ore ever lost.
detail: Downbound, she blew a propeller seal, split her sternpost, filled and foundered quickly. Her cabin was blown off by the pressure and floated about for awhile with its lights still burning. Crew and 4 passengers, including Frank Perew, the ship’s owner, made it to shore in her boats, camped out overnight and then sailed one of her boats to Whitefish Point, where the schooner ST. PAUL brought them to the Soo. Her machinery and some gear was recovered in 1978-83 and now rests in the Smithsonian. It is reputedly the oldest marine engine existing that was built in North America. Owned by the People’s Line [Frank Perew] of Cleveland. Master: Capt. Wm. H. McNelley Rebuilt in the winter of 1857-8

name: INDIANA
detail: Bound Buffalo for Cleveland, she was struck by a squall and began to leak when off Grand River, Ohio. Despite the efforts of her crew to pump her out, she was overwhemed by inflowing water and sank. Owned out of Cleveland by Palmer & Pennington. Master: Capt. McAdams. She was built in only 45 days. Major repairs in 1862

name: INDIANOLA
detail: Wrecked. Document surrendered at Port Huron 1/14/78, annotated “wrecked at Toledo, 1876.” Rebuilt and reregistered with a new official number in (US44088) 1879. Gone from registry by 1883.

name: INDUSTRY
other_names: in earlier editions of mv as INDUSTRIE
detail: Reported ashore and a total loss. Out of Manitowoc; owned by J. M. Halvorsen and Christian Olsen. Valued at $2,500. *Previously numbered 44114, number changed between 1869 and 1872 As a Canadian vessel her document was surrendered when she was wrecked on Long Point, Lake Erie, probably in an incident in November, 1856, when she was reported a total loss. She probably came into U.S. registry at that time.

name: INDUSTRY
place_of_loss: half to 2/3 mi off South Haven, MI, piers
detail: In transit St. Joseph for Muskegon, she capsized and sank near shore in a gale. Her crew clung to the wreck for a short time, but efforts from shore were unable to affect a rescue. The wreck drifted to the beach about five miles south of town and went to pieces. Out of St. Joseph, Mich., owned by her skipper, Capt. John King(d). King’s son was a crew member, also lost.

name: INDUSTRY
other_names: built as steam lighter C. H. CONOVER, renamed in 1924
detail: no detail. Owned by Roen Salvage Co., Sturgeon Bay, Wis. Converted from steamer to barge in 1944. Normally operated on the Chicago River.

name: ROBERT G. INGERSOLL
other_names: also seen as R.G. INGERSOLL and BOB INGERSOLL
detail: Bound Manistee for Chicago (also stated as Manistee for Waukegan), she caught fire offshore and was abandoned to burn to the waterline. Her crew was taken off by the propeller GEO. C. MARKHAM. Out of Chicago or Waukegan, owned by Fay.

name: B. INGWERSEN
place_of_loss: near Turtle Light, off Toledo, OH
detail: Caught fire and quickly burned to a total loss. Her crew barely had time to escape in her boat. Owned by J. E. and J. H. Davis, Toledo

name: INKERMANN
build_info: 1918, Canada Car & Foundry, Ft. William, Ont.
place_of_loss: between Ft. William and the Soo
detail: Built for the French navy, she left Thunder Bay on her delivery trip in company with CERISOLES(qv) and SEBASTOPOL. Only the last named made the Soo. The other two probably foundered in a storm the 24th, though no evidence was ever found. Master: Capt. Mezou – the crew consisted of French Navy men. Later speculation was that she struck Superior Shoal, a pinnacle in the center of the lake that was uncharted until the 1940’s but many other sceneria are possible. *although the shipbuilder generally constructed steel vessels, minesweepers were normally constructed of non-magnetic wood. Articles on the wreck are mute on the point. The ship was equipped with 10-centimetre deck guns fore and aft.

name: INTERLAKEN
place_of_loss: 4 mi N of of White Lake Channel, MI
detail: The barge and a small scow tied along time were bound North Manitou for White Lake in tow of the tug FRED C. GERTLING. When approaching their destination in heavy seas, the tug ran short on fuel and abandoned the INTERLAKEN. Several salvage/rescue attempts from shore were ineffectual, and the crew was finally taken off by the Coast Guard. Out of Milwaukee; owned by Lyons Construction Co. Master: Capt. Matthew Overogg. Wreck discovered from the air by 16-year-old student pilot Jonathon Freye on Sep, 2003.

name: INTERNATIONAL
build_info: 1854, Bidwell & Banta, Buffalo (also seen as Chippawa, Ont.)
detail: Was docked on the American side to have some minor repair done to her machinery. She caught fire from a small unattended stove the workmen had lit to warm themselves and burned to a total loss, along with a nearby flour mill. Owned by the Buffalo, Brantford and Goderich Railroad and used as a break-bulk and passenger ferry. Part of her hull was used in a new vessel of the same name launched in 1856.

name: INTERNATIONAL
detail: Bound Chicago for Kingston, she drove ashore in a storm, a total loss. The crew lashed themselves to the rigging and waited 36 hours for rescue before the vessel began to break up, then they attempted to drift ashore on wreckage. Out of Chicago, owned and sailed by Capt. Mark McGuinne

name: INTERNATIONAL
detail: Carried RR cars across the Niagara R. and Lake Erie, she was one of the earliest purpose-built carferries. Destroyed by fire, possibly arson. She had been idle since the previous November. Detroit R. also given as location

name: INTERNATIONAL
place_of_loss: White City, MI, Portage Ship Canal
detail: She burned at her dock, total loss. The remains of the wreck are still located just off the dock site. Owned out of Houghton, MI by M. Carroll

name: INTEROCEAN
detail: Destroyed by fire in the St. Clair R opposite Dunford and Alvorson Drydock. Owner: F. B. Hackett.

name: INVINCIBLE
detail: Bound for Fort William in a gale, she was driven ashore by big seas and wrecked.

name: INVINCIBLE
build_info: 1857, B.B. Jones, Buffalo (also given as 1852, Clayton, NY)
place_of_loss: 3 mi S of Presque Isle, near Thunder Bay, MI
detail: Dragged her anchors and drove ashore in the great gale of November, 1869 and reported a total loss. Out of Buffalo and bound Milwaukee for that port. Stripped and abandoned in May of the following year. Owned by McDonald and Griffin of Buffalo. Master: Capt. Mellen. Ashore and thought lost near Tawas, Michigan, Lake Huron, in Nov of 1858

name: IONA
date_of_loss: Also reported scuttled on Lake Ontario in 1902.
place_of_loss: off Stony Point, NY, near Oswego
detail: Bound Sodus, NY for Montreal, caught fire offshore, burned to the waterline and sank. She was a total loss of $17,000. Owned out of Montreal

name: IONIA
detail: Burned to a total loss at the foot of Wolfe Island. The accident reportedly resulted in $1750 in damage. Owned by C. Kelsey, Cape Vincent. Hailed from Kingston, where her registry had been transfered less than a month before. Ashore and reported wrecked at Presqu’ile, Ont, in October, 1874. Recovered by tug WATERTOWN.

name: IOSCO
build_info: 1891, F. Wheeler, W. Bay City hull #80
detail: Towing ore-laden schooner OLIVE JEANETTE(qv), struck by a terrific gale and foundered, exact position unknown, but probably between Portage Entry and Huron Isls. OLIVE JEANETTE was also lost. Master: Capt. Nelson Gonyaw(d).

name: IOWA
detail: Bound Buffalo for Huron, Ohio, she collided with the steamer ERIE and sank, a total loss. Owned by Colvin . Master: Capt. Patterson.

name: IOWA
detail: Wrecked in a gale and a total loss of $3,500.

name: IOWA
detail: Left Chicago in company with the bark J. V. AYER and lost in the same storm. She went missing in a huge gale with 9 to 11 people aboard. No evidence of her has yet been found. Out of Chicago. Damaged on St. Clair Flats in a towing accident in July, 1855

name: IOWA
build_info: 1852, F.N. Jones, Buffalo, as a sidewheel steamer
specs: [242x31x13, 981 t. om as a propeller steamer.]
detail: Lost from the tow of the iron tug ADMIRAL D.D. PORTER and went ashore. PORTER and tug KITTY SMOKE(qv) tried for days to get her off, but she broke up in a storm the 18th. Remains were reportedly towed in to Kewaunee and finally burned Dec 18. Owned by Peshtigo Lumber Co. Also burned at Manitowoc in Jul, 1864. Converted to a prop in 1855 and became a barge in 1867.

name: IOWA
detail: Bound Alpena for Buffalo, she broke her towline from the steamer OREGON near Pte Aux Barques and was left to her own devices in a gale. She became overwhelmed with ice and then the deckhouse was washed overboard taking the skipper’s wife and 2 children to their deaths. She floated awash for several days until her crew was finally taken off by the Canadian tug JOHN McKAY on the 17 th . The vessel was left to sink on her own. Owned out of Buffalo. Master: Capt. John Taylor.

name: IOWA
other_names: built on hull of old steamer MENOMINEE [US#90720] built 1872, by Rand, Manitowoc
detail: Trying to take advantage of a midwinter thaw to haul a valuable cargo, she was caught in the ice in a refreeze and crushed. Her single passenger and her crew were able to walk ashore. She had a reputation as a jinx because of her many mishaps. As MENOMINEE she was originally built for the Goodrich Line. When she was sent to be dismantled in 1896, her hull was found to be so sound that IOWA was built on it by the same company.

name: IRELAND
place_of_loss: near Windmill Point, N of Buffalo
detail: Bound Toledo for Buffalo, she went on the reef in wind and fog. Her skipper had feared she would strike the sunken steamer ALABAMA(qv) and had veered too wide. She dropped her anchors and her crew abandoned the vessel, which later went to pieces.

name: R.L. IRELAND
detail: Heavily damaged in a stranding earlier in the fall, but pulled off the rocks before the waves destroyed her. Struck by a gale while in tow to Duluth, she broke her hawser and was driven ashore in the Apostles, where waves wrecked her. Declared a total loss at the time, but recovered and lasted (as SIRIUS and ONTADOC) until 1970.

name: IRENE
place_of_loss: 1 mi S of Chicago Coast Guard Station
detail: Exploded and sank. *[Perhaps US#226136, built Leland, MI, 1928]

name: IRIS
build_info: 1862, Bertier or Bertha, Prince Edward County, Ont.
detail: She struck the west crib while trying to enter the harbor in a gale. Most sources report that she sunk and was a total loss, but others state that she was recovered, lost in the big storm of 1869. Out of Hamilton, Ont. (one source says Detroit)

name: IRIS
detail: She stranded in heavy weather and was abandoned. Owner; T. Thompson, Milwaukee.

name: IRON AGE
build_info: 1880, Detroit Dry Dock, Wyandotte, MI hull# 37
detail: Caught fire, burned to the waterline and sank in the shipping channel. Later dynamited as a hazard.

name: IRON CHIEF
build_info: 1881, Detroit Dry Dock, Wyandotte, MI hull# 51
place_of_loss: 6 mi off Pte Aux Barques, MI
detail: Blew her propeller stuffing, filled and sank in heavy seas. Crew rescued by steamer ANDREW CARNEGIE. Bound for Ft. William with coal. Owned by Corrigan fleet. Master: Capt. U. S. Cody. Her consort IRON CLIFF made it to Alpena under sail. *launched as a schooner-barge, but engined soon after

name: IRON CITY
place_of_loss: near Sturgeon Point, 18 mi W of Buffalo
detail: Tow of tug M.I. MILLS, she broke loose in gale near Point Abino and was driven into the shallows, a total wreck. Owned by M. Nean of Detroit, and chartered to carry oil between Buffalo and Cleveland for the season. Master: Capt. Richard L. Manning [who was lost a month later on the schooner W. O. BROWN (qv)]. Rebuilt from a prop to barge in July of the same year as her loss. As a propeller she was 185x29x12, 612 t., and had been property of the Lake Superior Line.

name: IRON CITY
other_names: built as 3-mast schooner DANIEL E. BAILEY renamed in 1886
build_info: 1874, Bailey Bros., Toledo as a 3-mast schooner
detail: Collided with the steel steamer THOMAS F. COLE and sank in the shallows near the island. Converted to barge in 1912
media_notes: Image with an Inman Line tug:

name: IRONSIDES
place_of_loss: Grand Haven Bar, near Grand Haven
detail: She was driven into the bar and destroyed by wave action. She had become disabled after she sprang a leak, flooded stern first and her fires were put out. She sank about 5 miles out within a few moments of the leaving of the last lifeboat. Two lifeboats were capsized in the storm, drowning most of their occupants, while other boast were swamped in the surf near shore. Owned by the Englemann Line, connected with the Detroit & Milwaukee RR. Master: Capt. Harry Sweetman(d). Englemann of Milwaukee unsuccessfully attempted to raise her in 1876-7. *number lost also given as 28

name: IRONTON
detail: Bound Cleveland for Marquette in tow of the steamer C. J. KERSHAW, she collided with steamer OHIO(qv) and sank quickly after her towline became entangled in the KERSHAW’s propeller. Two of her crew were rescued by the KERSHAW. Owned by W. S. Mack of Cleveland. Master: Capt. Peter Girard(d).

name: IROQUOIS
build_info: 1846, Sprague, Sackett’s Harbor, NY as a brig*
detail: This aged schooner went out of control while trying to make the Manistee harbor for shelter from a gale, and beached north of the piers. By the end of the month she was reported stripped and gone to pieces, a total loss of $5,000. Out of Detroit, owned by W. H. Tefft. Also reported ashore in the North Channel in 1864. Major repairs in 1862. Converted from brig to schooner after 1872. *builder also given as Wilcox, location as 3-mile Bay or Pt. Peninsula, NY

name: IROQUOIS
other_names: none; may have been built on the burned-out hull of the steamer JOHN J. LONG(qv)
place_of_loss: 10 mi NE of Spanish Mills, Ont, North Channel
detail: While on a routine trip from Little Current to Cutler, Ontario, IROQUOIS was attempting to run a short cut in order to enter the McBean Channel ahead of the tug B. M. FRASER, which had a tow of logs. Visibility was poor at the time due to a forest fire that was burning in the area, resulting in the IROQUOIS running up on a rock while out of the main channel. The collision either upset a lantern or caused the boiler doors to open spilling fire on the deck, and the ship was consumed. Two bags of mail were the main casualties. Hailed from Goderich, owned by T. C. Sims, Little Current (Eastern Manitoulin Royal Mail Steamship Line). Master: Capt. Ed Mackie

name: CLARA IRR
detail: No detail. Document surrendered Mar 31, 1891, annotated “wrecked, total loss.” Out of Bay City. Also reported built at Depere, WI, 1871, by Sorenson. Not in ’69 mv. *reported to be first steam fish boat on Lake Michigan

name: GEORGE IRVING
detail: Bound Alpena for Marine City, she waterlogged and foundered in a gale. Two brothers from W. Bay City were her entire crew after her regular crew refused the trip. They crawled to the top of the cabin as the boat sank and were saved by the big D & C passenger steamer CITY OF MACKINAC. The derelict hull floated about the lake for a few days and was finally located and towed into port by a tug. She was deemed unrepairable and declared a total loss. Owned by Fred and Olan Cook, Bay City. Master: Capt. Fred Cook.

name: WASHINGTON IRVING
detail: Bound Erie for Buffalo, she went missing on her regular route with all hands lost. It was not known that she was lost until the tug J.B. WHITE picked up her provision box from the lake on the 11th. Owned by Marshall and Walker of Erie, PA. Master: Steven H. Vanatta(d).

name: JOHN IRWIN
carrying: hardware, merchandise [also seen as railroad iron]
detail: Bound Cleveland for Milwaukee, she went ashore in a gale and was later reported broken up, a total loss. Master: Capt. James Conoly who owned her with J. M. Isaacs, Chicago.

name: LEWIS C. IRWIN
other_names: also seen as L. C. IRWIN
detail: Reported ashore and lost in the “Big Gale” of 1869. Still on some 1871 and 72 lists and may have been lost at Cleveland with a cargo of stone in September, 1871. Homeport: Chicago. Master: Capt. McGregor. Capsized and sunk by a white squall with one life lost in July 1855, and ashore near Michigan City in Dec of the same year. Ashore and wrecked near Chicago in Nov, 1857. Major repairs in 1862, ‘65. *May have been built at Kenosha, Wis.

name: ISABEL
detail: She was bound Owen Sound for Detroit when she was hit by heavy weather. She made for shelter behind Chantry Island, but capsized when about two miles out. The crew and at least two passengers – one a little girl – were able to right her yawl and make it to shore somehow. The schooner drifted into shoal water and broke up. Owned and sailed by Capt. John Chisholm, Owen Sound.

name: ISABELL H.
detail: She was lying peacefully at her dock when she was crushed by the 250-foot steel tanker COMET. The tanker had miscalulated a turn in the turning basin and had wiped out the boat and her dock. Owned by John Mollhagen, St. Joseph.

name: ISABELLA
place_of_loss: between Port Maitland and Gravelly Bay, 5-6 miles from shore
detail: She went on Long Point in a gale on August 29. The next day the tug HARRISON put pumps on board and hauled her off and began to tow her to Buffalo for repairs. Enroute the pumps failed and she foundered in deep water, a total loss to the vessel and the valuable wrecking pumps. The wrecking crew aboard her barely escaped with their lives. Owned by Darragh & Co, Toronto, in 1864 Repairs in 1861

name: ISABELLA
place_of_loss: near the entrance to harbor at Charlotte, NY
detail: Bound Whitby, Ont. for Sodus Point, she foundered in a storm near the end of the pier. Her crew was rescued by a volunteer crew consisting of the captains of a number of nearby vessels. Master: Capt. Ewart. Usls report shows her as a total loss. N.A.C. had indefinite date for date of her loss – 1921 document surrender is annoted “lost about 10 years ago.”

name: ISABELLA H.
other_names: built as the steamer McCORMICK, renamed in 1915
detail: Bound Cape Vincent for Sodus, NY in company of her fleetmate HINCKLEY when her old hull failed in a moderate blow and she went down. HINCKLEY tried to nurse her in to Oswego, but she foundered just outside the harbor. Lifesaving Service picked her crew out of the water just after she disappeared. Owner: Capt. A. R. Hinckley. The Coast Guard flattened the wreck with dynamite the next year, and the remains were mostly obliterated during the building of the west breakwater in 1931. Built as passenger & package freight prop Rebuilt in 1915 at Chaumont, NY – may have been renumbered at that time to US#213102.

name: WAYNE ISBELL
other_names: built as the tug T.R. MERRILL, renamed in 1883
detail: Burned to a total loss. No detail. Struck the breakwater at Sand Beach, Michigan in a storm in May, 1884, and heavily damaged. She was a fish tug at that time. Sold Canadian at Marquette in 1910. Last reported owned by N. Trotter, Little Current, Ont.

name: ISHPEMING
build_info: 1872, J.M. Jones, Detroit, as a 3-mast schooner
place_of_loss: Black River Isl, S of Thunder Bay, MI
detail: She and her tow steamer fought a storm until she was cast off, went ashore and was wrecked. Owned by Hiram Henderson of Cleveland. Master: Capt. Sylvester Ray.
media_notes: Image as schooner with full outfit:

name: ISLAND BELLE
detail: Caught fire at park dock and burned through her hawsers. Shorebound firemen could only watch as she floated down the river, a mass of flame. After a short chase, the Detroit tug SHOOFLY got a line on her before she ran among the wharfside schooners downtown. She was beached and allowed to burn out at the foot of 24 th St., Detroit. Her engine was removed from the remains by the steambarge FLORENCE in October. Owned by Blanchard Whipple, Detroit Master: Capt. Whipple [father of owner]. Her engine and boiler went into the barge NELLIE, which was to be turned into an excursion steamer herself. *This is not the current city of Hamtramck, but a village along the river north of Detroit, since annexed.

name: ISLAND BELLE
other_names: built as ISLAND WANDERER, renamed in 1894
detail: Burned to total loss. Out of Buffalo, owned by W. F. Voetsch. Rebuilds: 1889, 94; 1910, 15

name: ISLAND CITY
build_info: 1859, Peter Perry, St Clair R.[Harson’s Island], MI
detail: She foundered in a storm while bound Ludington for Milwaukee. Her skipper made it to shore in her nine-foot yawlboat, which had drifted away from the sinking vessel uncontrolled, and was found on the beach north of Milwaukee, unconscious. Master and owner: Capt. William B. Wood, Grand Haven, age 26. Rebuilt and enlarged in 1866, major repairs 1878, 79.

name: ISLAND QUEEN
place_of_loss: Waugoschance Pt., western Straits of Mackinac
detail: She struck Skillagalee in heavy seas and was run to the beach at this point to save her. Apparently she later pounded to pieces.

name: ISLAND QUEEN
build_info: 1855, Kelley or Hackett, Kelley’s Island, Ohio as a sidewheel steamer
detail: Beached north of the North pier in a gale the 9 th of October, and was pounded to pieces on the date shown above. Owned by Squires & White. This is the vessel which was seized by Confederate raiders in Sep. 1864, in an abortive attempt to capture the U. S. gunboat MICHIGAN [see also PHILO PARSONS]. Rerigged as a barge in 1875. *number also given as 12250

name: ISLAND QUEEN
other_names: built as L. GOLDISH, renamed in 1926
type_at_loss: propeller, diesel, wood*, pass & frt packet
detail: Destroyed by fire in harbor. Homeport: Chicago – owned by G. R. Sweeney. Over the years she had rescued 29 sailors in distress in lake storms, as her skipper, L. Goldish, would rush to the rescue in any weather. *hull sheathed with 14 ga. iron plates against ice

name: ISLAND QUEEN
detail: Burned to a total loss at her wharf at Hanlan’s Point, along with the prop KATHLEEN(qv). Owned by Toronto Ferry Co., Toronto.

name: ISLANDER
build_info: 1860, Mackinac, MI as a steam ferry
detail: No detail. Date shown may be wrong: her listing in 1874 Bureau of Navigation copy of MVUS has the handwritten notation “wrecked.” *Shown as a sidewheeler in 1st enrollment, propeller later

name: ISLE ROYALE
other_names: built as barge AGNES* renamed between ’80 and ’82
detail: Sprang a leak and sank. Her passengers and crew made it to the island. Owned by Cooley & Lavague, Duluth. *rebuilt to propeller in 1880, originally 55 gt. **also shown as built by S. Langell, or at Port Huron and Algonac (by McElroy)

name: ISLET PRINCE
other_names: built as MARIPOSA, renamed BON AMI in 1908, last name in 1926
build_info: 1902, Burger, Manitowoc, Wis. as a fish tug [US#93330]
detail: She was retuning to Owen Sound after a short stint as a ferry between Kingsville and Pelee Island when she was caught by a storm and put into Chantry Island dock for shelter. During the storm the vessel was struck by lightning, which apparently started a fire which later burst out and destroyed the vessel. Remains of machinery were later recovered for salvage. Owned by John Tackaberry of Lion’s Head, Ont.and sailed by him. Sold Canadian in 1907.
media_notes: Image as MARIPOSA (top) and ISLET PRINCE

name: ISOLDE
other_names: built as CITY OF PARIS, renamed C.W. JACOB in 1914, renamed ISOLDE in 1928
build_info: 1891, J. Davidson, W. Bay City, MI
place_of_loss: E of Erie, PA, at 6-mile Creek, 400 yds offshore
detail: In tow of the propeller TRISTAN, her hawser parted and she went ashore and was wrecked. Owner: Hammermill Paper of Erie Sunk in a serious collision with the steamer LINDEN off Sans Souci, St. Clair River, in 1905. Rebuilt 1919, converted to barge, 1928

name: ITASCA
detail: Tow of Canadian tug K. HAIGHT with schooner MARY (qv), collided with the steamer PARKS FOSTER in a passing error. She may later have been raised, scuttled off Lexington, Mich. Owned by C. E. Chilson of Lorain, OH.

name: ITHACA
place_of_loss: near the north pier of St. Joseph, MI
detail: Bound Muskegon for Michigan City, IN she struck a reef and sank in a gale. Her crew was saved by the Lifesaving Service. The vessel was a total loss of about $2,000. Homeport: Chicago Rebuilt in 1863. *builder also given as Kelley.

name: EDNA IVAN
detail: No detail.

name: IVANHOE
detail: Bound Cleveland for Mackinac, she collided at about midnight with the schooner ARAB and sank soon after. Her crew escaped in her yawl before she went down, and were later picked up by the propeller OHIO. Out of Buffalo. Master: Capt. Dennis Gallighan.

name: IVANHOE
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, iron, passenger & package freight
detail: Destroyed by fire at the VanBuren Street wharf, Chicago, along with the steamer R. J. GORDON. Both vessels owned by Ben Newman, Chicago. Burned to a shell at Houghton, Michigan in August, 1877, and was completely rebuilt afterwards.
sources: hcgl,nsp,polk,mpl,wmn,jm Share this: Share on X (Opens in new window) Share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Facebook Like Loading...

name: ANDREW JACKSON
place_of_loss: 2 ¼ mi E of USLS station, on Pte Aux Barques, MI
detail: Stranded and wrecked in a big gale. The U.S. Lifesaving Service was able to rescue her crew. Owned by Haynes Lumber Co. *Location also given as Grand Haven, in error.

name: CECILIA JAFFRAY
other_names: also seen as CECELIA JEFFREY, CELIA JAFFRAY, C. JEFFRIES, CELIA JEFFREY, etc.
detail: The JAFFRAY was overtaken by a snowstorm and sank at about 10 pm. All of her crew escaped safely. Owned out of St. Catharines by D. D. Harnett and sailed by Capt. J. Malloy. Sunk at Kingston, Ont., in October, 1873 and near Cleveland in 1874 Rebuilt in 1876.

name: JAMAICA
loss_of_life_original: none of 10 [7 crew plus Captain’s wife, niece and son]
detail: In a crippled condition, she was run ashore to save her from sinking. Those aboard were rescued by Lifesaving Service. Bound Oswego for Chicago and owned by Thomas Norton of Oswego. She had had a similar accident near the same spot the previous year. Usls report has long description of her loss. Also knocked down by a white squall Jun 18, 1872 on Lake Huron. Crew rescued by the passing schooner STARLIGHT. Master: Capt. David Bouthwell. Owner: M. M. Wheeler, Oswego. One source reports her as a scow-schooner. Major repair in 1882.

name: WILLIAM JAMIESON
place_of_loss: off N side of Amherst Island
detail: She began to leak heavily in a storm, was abandoned by her crew and sank. Bound from Oswego. One of the last working schooners on the Great Lakes when lost. Owned by Wm. Savage of Kingston. Wreckage located by divers about 1970.
sources: csqw,mmgl,ns3,is(2-90),do —-——————————— JAPAN – See ROY K. RUSSELL

name: JAPAN
build_info: 1853, Ruggles, Milan, OH [also given: T. Hamilton, Milan]
place_of_loss: in Garret Bay of Green Bay
carrying: wood (liquor reported by hopefuls, but no mention of it in the press)
detail: Driven on the beach and wrecked near Hedgehog Harbor just after taking on a cargo of wood. Owned out of Chicago by Sirhorn and Gunderson. Rebuilt in 1865, ‘85, major repair in 1861

name: MARY JARECKI
place_of_loss: Au Sable Point, W of Grand Marais, MI
detail: She went ashore on the W side of the point in a heavy fog. Despite the efforts of the tug MYSTIC with a 14-inch steam pump, the vessel remained in place and was later pounded to pieces by a storm. Owned out of Chicago by Barker. Master: Capt. Tony Everett. Major repairs in 1880.

name: T.J. JARMIN
other_names: built as fire tug GEYSER, renamed in 1906
build_info: 1889, J. Davidson, W. Bay City hull #27 US#86036
detail: Foundered. No detail. Owned by W. J. Bell, Sudbury, Ont. Built as a fire and harbor tug. Sold Canadian in 1904. Rebuilt in 1912.

name: JAVA
detail: Bound Detroit for Dunkirk, she was riding out a storm behind Long Point when her anchor chains parted. Her skipper ran for Dunkirk, but she crashed on the breakwater while trying to enter the harbor. Her crew was rescued by boats from shore. Owned by W.H. and James Craig of Detroit Master: Capt. William Been.

name: JAVA
carrying: valuable mixed merchandise including 300 t. of fine household goods, parlor stoves, salt,etc.
detail: She was a twin-screw, and the main theory of her loss in good weather was that her starboard shaft coupling came loose and shaft slid out the stern, allowing water to flood through the sleeve. She sank quickly in more than 300 ft. of water. Crew escaped in lifeboats, were picked up by J.P.MARSH. Bound Bay City for Milwaukee & Chicago. Owned by Ensign Line [Ensign & Holt], Buffalo. Master: Capt. F. L. Pope. Sister of RUSSIA, CUBA, SCOTIA.

name: JAY BIRD
detail: Document surrendered Nov 12, 1896 at Port Huron, annotated only “wrecked and abandoned.” Rebuilt and enlarged in 1877

name: JEAN
detail: Destroyed by fire. Owned by H. H. Boyd, Port Burwell. Sold Canadian, 1906.

name: JEANIE
detail: Caught fire from unreported causes in her winter berth at the Craig shipyard, Toledo, and burned to a total loss. Owned by John Craig & Sons, Toledo.

name: JEANETTE
detail: Stuck a pier while entering the harbor in a heavy gale and pounded to pieces. Owned by her skipper, Capt. James King(d) of Scriba, NY and J Lyon & Co. of Oswego.

name: JEFFERSON
detail: Bound for Chicago, she was driven ashore and demolished. Eight passengers [including an entire family of 7] & 1 crewman were lost. The remainder of the crew made it to the shelter of the brig OLIVE RICHMOND, which had gone ashore nearby. One young woman passenger was later found in the cabin, frozen solid in an upright position, with arms outstretched. She was later recovered and is the same vessel as the 1844 wreck (below). Owned out of Chicago by J. Ransom. Master: Capt. William Dougall. This vessel is mentioned many times in early Great Lakes lore. A schr THOMAS JEFFERSON was reported wrecked at Chicago with the loss of 2 lives in November, 1839. Owned by Hugunin and sailed by Capt. Keeler.

name: JEFFERSON
detail: Bound Chicago for St. Joseph, she was overwhelmed by a storm when approaching the latter port and sank up to her decks. Master: Capt. Dougall. Her crew clung to her ice-coated rigging for eight hours until rescued from shore by a hastily-formed group of lifesavers in a rowboat. The rescue crew was led by Capt. Nelson Napier, who was skipper of the steamer ALPENA(qv) when that vessel was lost in 1880. Capt. J. Prindiville,at the time an 18-year-old crewman on the JEFFERSON, related this story in detail after the ALPENA was lost.

name: JOHN H. JEFFERY, Jr
other_names: also seen as J.H. JEFFERY. See below
detail: Tied up at her dock on Grassy Point , she was destroyed when the great Cloquet forest fire swept the dock area. Owned by Northern Dock & Dredge Co., Duluth. The fire destroyed all of NDDs floating stock including the dredge DULUTH, the tugs ELLA G. STONE, MENTOR and this tug as well as five or six scows. Nearly destroyed by fire Nov 3, 1910 at Duluth. Reported destroyed by fire Oct 1, 1892, but recovered and rebuilt (brand new at the time or maybe the fire was to AGATE). Built on the hull of the 1868 tug AGATE (US#1713).

name: L. JENISON
other_names: none also seen as L. JEMISON
detail: Burned to a total loss. Document surrendered in March of 1877. Owned by Capt. Ganoe.

name: ISAAC G. JENKINS
other_names: also seen as J.G. JENKINS, F. G. JENKONS
detail: She left the Welland Canal bound for Oswego and was caught in a terrific westerly storm. Foundered somewhere enroute. A few small pieces of wreckage and the body of the Captain’s dog were all that was ever found. Out of Oswego, owned by E. Mitchell and others. Master: Capt. John Brown(d). *It has long been stated in modern sources that her position is unknown, but vessels reported striking submerged masts thought to be hers 4-5 miles off Lewis’ Bluff, E of Oswego, NY* in 1876. Wreck probably located in nearly 700 ft of water off Sodus Bay and idenfied remotely.

name: LOUISA JENKINS
detail: She lost her rudder and became unmanageable while coming into the harbor in a gale. She drove against the piers and was pounded to pieces in a short time. Her crew was rescued by the steamer CHAUTAUQUE. Master: Capt. Traverse. Owner: John Vail, ey. Al, Silver Ck., NY. Ashore with damage under Point Abino in 1833 *first known registry 1834

name: EDWARD H. JENKS
other_names: built as prop E.M. FOSTER(qv), later FAUSTIN(qv)
build_info: 1882, D. Foster, Port Dover, Ont. as a passenger steamer C# 85471
place_of_loss: Ballard Reef, 1 mi above Lime-Kiln Crossing
detail: She collided with the 1000 t. steamer GEORGE W. MORLEY in a misunderstanding of passing signals and beached on the reef with just her cabin above water. The captain’s wife was among those lost. Master: Capt. Ekbert. Recovered in October; rebuilt and enlarged at the Oades yard, Detroit, in 1900. See FAUSTIN for details of her final demise. Sold U.S. 1888 after capsizing in Saginaw Bay and then drifting ashore near Port Hope, Mich. She was then purchased by Jenks, rebuilt and renamed.

name: J.M. JENKS
place_of_loss: a few mi N of Midland, Ont., Georgian Bay
detail: Driven ashore with heavy damage in the “Big Storm” of 1913. Recovered though thought to be a total loss, but the cost of repairs was near her value before the accident. Later rebuilt as a bulker. Scrapped in 1963

name: JENNIBEL
detail: Outbound from Egg Harbor, she was struck by a southerly squall and capsized. The tug GREGORY rescued her crew shortly before the vessel went down, her cargo being unable to sustain bouyancy. Owned by Capt. Christianson, Milwaukee. An attempt was made to raise her in the 1960’s, but she broke up in the process.

name: JENNIE AND ANNIE
place_of_loss: under Empire Bluffs, 9 mi S of Sleeping Bear
detail: Driven into shallows by a gale and pounded to pieces. Also damaged in great gale of Nov 17-18, 1869, near Racine. Homeport in 1869: Buffalo

name: A.H. JENNIE
detail: She was swamped and sunk by a storm. Bound Hamilton for Cobourg, Ont. Out of Port Rowan, owned by J.J. Deadarch. Rebuilt and lengthened 12 ft in 1899. Orig. 108gc

name: JENNIFER
other_names: built as the steamer CACOUNA from halves of sister ships, renamed LORNA P in 1973, last name in 1974
detail: This small freighter capsized and sank in a storm when her cargo shifted. A USCG helicopter and the British freighter FORTUNA rescued her crew. Owned by Matthew Shipping Co., Great Britain.

name: F.A. JENNINGS
detail: She burned to a total loss at 3 am. At the time arson was suspected. The tug had been laid up in the canal since the fall of 1877, and had just been raised preparatory to putting her back in service. She was towed down and her machinery was removed at Amherstburg in late July. Owned by Mrs. F. A. Jennings of Detroit NOTE: Vessel name may be in error. A fish tug WALTER SCOTT (#80064), owned by the same party, was begun to be totally rebuilt at the same time as this accident – reported relaunched in October, 1879.

name: W.G. JENNINGS
detail: No detail. Document surrendered Jun 3, 1896, annotated “wrecked.”

name: JEROME
detail: This small local tug exploded her boiler at her dock, injuring one and throwing hot boiler pieces in all directions. Out of Grand Haven. Master: Capt. Moses Gerard.

name: JEROME
build_info: 1872, J. Jerome & A. Hemminger , Carrollton, MI (Saginaw) as a sidewheeler [US#75704]
detail: Burned to a total loss in the River Sydenham. Owned by J. A. Smith or Dr. Mitchell of Wallaceburg. Hulk later sold to C. J. Johnson, Wallaceburg, but no evidence that she ever ran again. Also sunk in a collision with the steamer WILLIAM J. MACY near the same spot [i.e. Baby’s Point], in 1881. Canadian records say she went into that registry in 1873, but she is still in 1877 mv. Rebuilt from a sidewheeler to a prop in about 1883.

name: GORDON JERRY
build_info: 1884, J Duprey, River Rascom, Ont. as a schooner (or Port Burwell, 1874)
detail: She stranded and was abandoned as not worth salvaging. The hulk caught fire and burned to the waterline Oct 10, 1906. Converted to a prop at Toronto, 1892 or 1889.

name: JERSEY CITY
place_of_loss: near the tip of Long Point, opposite Dunkirk, NY
detail: Bound Toledo for Dunkirk, NY, she was driven ashore and wrecked in a very powerful storm. Owned by the New York and Erie RR Line.
sources: sagl,lhl,hgl,nsp,rnc —-——————————— JERSEY CITY – former entry (1890) deleted – no supporting information

name: JESKA
detail: Filled and foundered in 300 feet of water. Registered out of Kingston Stranded on Main Duck Isl., L. Ont., the previous year.

name: JESSIE
place_of_loss: E end Bois Blanc Isl. Straits.
detail: Stranded and went to pieces while bound for a Les Cheneaux Islands lumber camp. Document not surrendered until 1893. Rebuilt 1880

name: JESSIE
detail: Bound Toronto to Kingston, she drove ashore after dragging her anchors in a gale, and became a total loss of $34,000, vessel & cargo. She had come under the point for the night and anchored to ride out the gale, but the wind shifted during the night, driving her ashore. A desperate effort by locals on shore failed to save her crew, who had clung to the rigging until they dropped off one by one and perished. Out of Port Stanley. Master: Capt. John Shevlin(d). The loss of this vessel helped stimulate the government in to suveying the adjoining bay for conversion to a harbor of refuge. Major repair in 1862.

name: JESSIE MAGGIE
detail: This small schooner was caught by a storm, and during the buffeting her anchor came loose and stove a large hole in her hull. The crew barely had time to escape before she went down. Owned by W. J. Terrill, Cleveland. She was built on a farm near Sebewaing, Michigan and hauled 4 1/2 miles to Saginaw Bay over frozen ground by 16 spans of oxen. Built as a small 3-master, she was converted to a 2-master the next year.

name: JOHN JEWETT
place_of_loss: Grace Harbor, Hammond Bay, eastern Straits of Mackinac
detail: While loading lumber at the Grace Harbor Lumber Co. dock, the area was struck by a sudden heavy storm. Huge seas drove her right through the dock and ashore, where she pounded to pieces. Usls report gives the location of this wreck as Grace Harbor, Isle Royale. Location above is near Nine Mile Point, Michigan, east of Cheboygan. rebuilt, 1886. Out of Detroit

name: H.N. JEX
other_names: built as passenger & package freight steamer LAWRENCE; renamed FRONTENAC, 1901; and H.N. JEX in 1909
place_of_loss: 10 mi SE of Pt. Petre, Ont.
detail: She sprang a leak in a storm and foundered. Her crew abandoned in her boats and were picked up by the prop LEHIGH. She was a total loss of $16,000. Wreck is sometimes reported under her previous name, FRONTENAC. Sold Canadian, 1908, as a hulk, after a spectacular fire at Lorain, OH Jul 17 of that year. Her document was surrendered at that time and the vessel declared a total loss. Lengthened 37′ in 1901, converted to bulker in 1909

name: JOE
build_info: 1889, T.W. Kirby, Grand Haven as a steam yacht
detail: Her engine failed in storm and she was pushed ashore , where she broke up. Her crew was rescued by the lifesaving service. Bound Saugatuck for Holland, MI. Owned by Upham & Weed, Saugatuck. Master: Capt. Upham

name: JOHN & ALEX
detail: This family-owned fishing tug caught fire at her dock in the middle of the night and was totally destroyed. The tug was pushed away from her moorings to save the dock and it’s ice house and twine sheds. She sank in Big Tub, where her remains are a regular dive target.. Master; Capt. J. W. Ransbury. Owned by W. W. Ransbury & Sons, Tobermorey, she was a $15,000 loss.

name: WILLIAM JOHN
build_info: 1865, Quick, Presq’ile, ONt. on the hull of the schooner BOB WILSON [blt. 1844]
detail: Bound Trenton, Ont., for Oswego, she foundered shortly after beginning to take on water from an inexplicable leak. Her crew abandoned in her yawl and were picked up by the schooner CENTURION. Registered out of Montreal, owned by Capt. James Savage of Wellington, Ont. Also reported as wrecked in Oct. 1870 at the water works, Kingston. She struck a pier and did $6,000 damage to hull and cargo. Also stranded at Bay of Quinte May 2, 1871.

name: JOHNSON
other_names: built as French naval vessel L’OUTAOUAISE
build_info: 1759, Pte Au Baril, [St. Lawrence R.] as a 10-gun bark
place_of_loss: near site of Oswego, NY (then called Ontario)
detail: Part of Col. Bradstreet’s small fleet, she was lost while enroute Niagara for Oswego during war with the French. Taken by the British at Isle Royale, St. Lawrence R., in 1760

name: C.H. JOHNSON
other_names: also seen as CHARLES H. JOHNSON
place_of_loss: near Gros Cap, MI, Straits of Mackinac
detail: Loaded with feldspar blocks at Jacobsville, MI and bound for Chicago, she was sheltering from SW gale behind St. Helena Island. Her anchor chains broke and she was driven onto the rocks, where she broke up quickly. The crew was rescued by a local fisherman who answered her distress signal. Owned by her skipper, Capt. Hiram Henderson of Cleveland major repairs in 1881

name: C.N. JOHNSON
detail: Bound Buffalo for Toledo in tow of steamer OSCODA, she sprang a leak and was put on a bar to save her, but slid off into deeper water while her skipper was looking for a tug. Raised and towed to Amherstburg in late Nov, but not rebuilt. Owned by Chas. Holland of Marine City major repair in 1882

name: HATTIE JOHNSON
other_names: none also seen as HATTIE JOHNSTON
place_of_loss: Goose Isl Shoal, Les Cheneaux Isls., Straits (or Hammond Bay)
detail: Struck bottom on the shoal and sank. Reported in several sources as a total loss, but she was off the shoal and in port for rebuilding by Nov. 12. Out of Port Huron, owned by John Hibbard or John Johnson

name: HATTIE L. JOHNSON
place_of_loss: off harbor entrance at Oswego, NY
detail: She broke adrift from the tug PROCTOR while trying to make shelter at Oswego and drove ashore near the lifesaving station. Her crew made it to shore on their own. She was later stripped of everything of value and abandoned. Hailed from Ogdensburg, NY. See also TUSCARORA. Also wrecked on L. Michigan in 1879 and in 1880. Major repairs, 1881

name: HENRY J. JOHNSON
detail: Groping through the Straits in a dense fog, she collided with the steel steamer FRED PABST and sank quickly, in deep water. Her crew abandoned and was adrift for several hours before being picked up by the tug PARKER. Bound Escanaba for Cleveland. Owned by Capt. Henry J. Johnson, Green Springs, Ohio, hailed from Cleveland

name: HORACE JOHNSON
place_of_loss: Saginaw River just down from Saginaw
detail: Destroyed by explosion and fire only six weeks after her launch. Master: Capt. Alex Lafferty or Rafferty(d). Owned by Lapham & Johnson, who were two of those killed.

name: I.A. JOHNSON
detail: This small schooner sank in 100 feet of water following a collision with the much larger schooner LINCOLN DALL (qv). She was an unrecoverable loss, despite the efforts of the tugs SHEBOYGAN and NELSON. Out of Milwaukee. *also given as 12090

name: J.H. JOHNSON
other_names: also seen as JAMES F. JOHNSTON, JAMES H. JOHNSON
type_at_loss: paddlewheeler, wood, package freight (also reported as a propeller)*
place_of_loss: Green Bay, midway bet. Sister Bay and Eagle Harbor
detail: Bound for Manistique, Michigan, she drove up on a rock and stuck fast. She broke up over the next few weeks. Out of Milwaukee. Driven ashore in NW gale near St Joseph, MIch, Nov 25, 1891, and expected to break up in place. Reportedly valued at $65,000 at this time. Rebuilt and enlarged after recovery. *built as a paddlewheeler and still listed as such in ‘95mv, but newspapers call her a steambarge [i.e. propeller].

name: JOHN T. JOHNSON
place_of_loss: North Point of Thunder Bay, MI
detail: Tow of steamer B.W. BLANCHARD(qv) with the schooner-barge JOHN KELDERHOUSE, she was torn loose and driven ashore in a gale. Bound for Detroit and owned by Capt. W.L. Martin of Cheboygan Sunk by a gale in Milwaukee harbor in September, 1893, and expected to break up and become a total loss. Heavily damaged [broke in two] in a stranding near Cleveland in Oct, 1883. Declared a total loss. Major repairs in 1878
sources: nsp,stb,slh,ns1,h,mv,polk,usls,ade —-——————————— LENA JOHNSON – See EVRA FULLER

name: L.B. JOHNSON
detail: Sprang a leak and abandoned in storm, she later washed ashore and went to pieces. Her crew spent the night on a small life raft. Not purposely beached as sometimes stated. Owner: Great Lakes Towing. Capsized twice at Chicago in 1896.

name: LAURA JOHNSON
detail: Driven ashore by a storm and wrecked, a total loss. She had been loading at a Racine dock.

name: LEVI JOHNSON
detail: Exploded and burned to a total loss on her trials trip after a big repair job. Abandoned, but recovered in the spring of 1869 and rebuilt at Portsmouth. Still operating in 1881

name: MARIA F. JOHNSON
place_of_loss: off the tip of the peninsula surrounding Misery Bay, Erie, PA
detail: She had just left Erie, when she sprung a leak and was turned for shore, she foundered on the way in. Her crew took to her boat and made it to shore safely. Owner: J. L. Miner, Detroit. A news clipping from the next year reports that there was 20 feet of water over the deck of the JOHNSON. She was run ashore to prevent her sinking after colliding with the schooner LIVELY off Sheboygan, WI, in Sep, 1866. Damage heavy.

name: R. JOHNSON
other_names: also seen as R. R. JOHNSON, K.R. JOHNSON
detail: Bound Chicago for Buffalo she beat all the way down L. Huron during a succession of several storms, but was finally blown ashore 2 mi W of Fairport and sunk. Her crew climbed her rigging, but she came broadside to and dipped them in the icy water with every roll. She finally washed them to their deaths one by one before help could arrive. The farm on the bluff above her, and which had sent to the town for help to the unknown schooner below, was actually the home of the JOHNSON’s lost mate, who had been put in charge of the boat by his brother, Capt. Solomon Snell, the skipper and owner.

name: SOLON H. JOHNSON
detail: Her machinery was disabled by a storm and she blew ashore and broke up. L.S.S saved her desperate crew in an heroic effort. The tug PETER DALTON (qv) removed her boiler in the spring of 1896, but was burned and sunk on the way back to port. Homeport: Chicago. Owner: Mears. *location also given in error as Washington Isl. major repair 1876, 78. Rebuilt in 1879.

name: WILLARD JOHNSON
detail: Upbound for Chicago, she ran on a reef in fog and was stranded tightly. There was every prospect of getting her off, but foul weather caused her to be stripped and abandoned for the fall. By spring there was nothing left. Owned by Charles H. Smythe of Oswego.

name: JOLIET
detail: While lying at a dock on the Canadian side of the river, she was rammed and sunk by the steel steamer HENRY PHIPPS. Shortly after she was sunk her owner offered to give her to anyone who would guarantee to remove her. No takers. Dynamited later in the fall to flatten her. The remains were removed by the Corps of Engineers in 1963.

name: JOLLY INEZ
other_names: built as TURRET CHIEF renamed VICKERSTOWN, ’15, renamed JOLLY INEZ the same year
detail: She stranded and was heavily damaged in a gale, declared a total loss. However, she was recovered the next spring, her engines removed and her hull repaired & returned to service as the barge SALVOR(qv), wrecked L. Mich, 1930. See TURRET CHIEF for an earlier accident 1st Canadian registry in 1913, but a long-time laker as a Britisher before that [Br#106605]
media_notes: Image as TURRET CHIEF from GLMD Image as SALVOR, same source

name: JONAS
detail: no detail Seems an odd name for a vessel – personification of nautical bad luck.

name: B.B. JONES
detail: Her boiler exploded at her dock. The crew were at dinner on the deck just over the boiler when it went off, and most of the dead were never found. A 100-pound piece of her boiler went through a building 400 yds away. She was recovered by tug RESCUE in June, but not worth rebuilding. Hull finally towed out and abandoned at the head of Stag Island. Owned by Trowbridge & Wilcox of Detroit. Master: Capt. S. H. Burnham. Had just been rebuilt the previous winter. She was reportedly fastened with trenails [wooden pegs] when built

name: B.F. JONES
build_info: 1906, Great Lakes Engineering, Ecorse, MI hull# 15
place_of_loss: just above Lime Isl., St. Mary’s R.
detail: Suffered a collision with the steamer CASON J. CALLAWAY and sunk. Recovered, but declared a constructive total loss and scrapped the next year at Superior, WI. Part of her lower hull was used as the shipyard crane lighter SSC-1 at Superior Shipbuilding.

name: ELIZABETH JONES
type_at_loss: schooner, wood, 3-mast (some sources say she was a jackass-rigged 2-master )
build_info: 1867, Mason & Bidwell, Buffalo as a bark
place_of_loss: Racine Reef 2 mi S of Racine, WI
detail: She was bound Chicago for Buffalo in one of the heaviest gales of the century. Her sails blew out and she was at the storm’s mercy. She drove on the reef and wrecked, but her crew were rescued by the tug KNAPP and the Lifesaving Service. She was later stripped and broke up in another big storm on the 24th. Owned by W. H.
