detail: She foundered in a gale, a total loss of $6000. Owned by William Anglin or Anglian of Kingston.

name: MARY
detail: Caught fire from a burning grain elevator while she was tied to a dock. The dock, the elevator and the MARY were destroyed.

name: MARY
other_names: often seen as MARY OF DETROIT
date_of_loss: 1870, Oct 17 (1860 also given, but ‘70 is correct)
detail: Bound Kelley’s Island for Cleveland, she struck a pier in a gale, broke up and sank. Her skipper had sailed from Kelley’s Island into the storm despite entrreaties by his brother, who had had a premonition that something dreadful was about to happen to the scow. The vessel was later raised and MAY have been rebuilt. Homeport: Detroit. Owned by Martin L. Peterson, who had just paid the last installment of her purchase price of $600. Noted in 1872 Merchant Vessels as “lost.”

name: MARY
detail: Bound Port Stanley for Oswego with a heavy cargo of stone, she went ashore near Charlotte during a stormy night. By the next day she was breaking up. Registered out of Cape Vincent, NY, sailing out of Chaumont and also shown as owned by Shepard or Sheppard, Port Stanley. Rebuilt at St. Catharines in 1859. Apparently went between US and Canadian registry several times. *Also given as 220 t. schr b. 1868, Shickluna, St. Catharines This ves. was still operating in 1883

name: MARY
detail: When she became waterlogged she was abandoned by the crew in her yawl, but neither boat nor crewmen ever made it to shore. Out of Chicago. Master: Capt. Frank Bartlett.

name: MARY
detail: In tow of the tug KITTY HAIGHT with the barge ITASCA, she collided with the steamer PARKS FOSTER, capsized in Northeast Bend and sank. A misunderstanding of signals was blamed for the accident. In January the Corps of Engineers advertised to have the wreck removed as an obstruction to navigation, and she may have been raised and put back into service, as a tug was working on bringing her up in May of ’96. Out of Sarnia, owned by Harriett Millard

name: MARY
detail: Bound Fair Haven, NY, for Napanee under command of her owner Andy Baird, she was caught in a terrific storm. She was last seen off Oswego driving eastward under bare poles. Some wreckage of her was later found floating near Galloo Isl. Out of Napanee. Master: Capt. Andrew Baird, Jr.(d).

name: MARY
build_info: 1876, N Brooks, Milwaukee as a yacht
detail: Foundered in a gale. Her crew evacuated in her boat and made it to shore. Owned by Eddie Johnson.

name: MARY ALICE
detail: This little carrier was capsized by a squall. The tug HERCULES went to her assistance, but was unable to rescue her. Most of her cargo was recovered, however, and some sources say the vessel was, too. Out of Windsor, Ont., owned by Edw. Walsh Ordered off the registry in 1902.

name: MARY ALICE B
other_names: built as QUINTUS, renamed BONANKA in 1962, renamed LOMAX in 1972, last name in 1975
build_info: 1931, Marine Iron & Shipbuilding, Duluth as a U.S. Army C of E tug
detail: She had been working on the salvage of the sunken steamer MONROVIA (qv) for most of the summer and was returning to Detroit in tow of the tug DOLPHIN I when she was found to be leaking, and two pumps were placed aboard. As she reached the point shown she began to founder. Her crew was picked up and she was cut loose from the tow vessel. She foundered soon after. Owned by Peter Bill and Associates, Detroit. Wreck discovered in 1992, upright and intact, in 92 feet of water. Built for the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and served on the Great Lakes for her entire career until sold private in 1962.
media_notes: Image as BONANKA from my collection

name: MARY AMELIA
place_of_loss: halfway between Bar Pt. & Det. R. mouth, near Bob-Lo Island
detail: Foundered as a result of a collision with the schooner SELKIRK, which happened due to a passing error by the MARY’s wheelsman, and became a total loss. Master and owner: Capt. Geo. Van Camp. A few weeks later the Canadian government took steps to have her removed, and she was towed around Bar Pt. and out of the channel.

name: MARY AND LUCY
other_names: also seen as MARY ANN LUCY
place_of_loss: shore of Chantry Isl. near Southampton, Ont.
detail: She was driven into the shallows and wrecked by the action of waves. Four crewmen from the steamer MANITOBA attempted to take her crew off, but their yawl capsized and 3 of them were lost. Out of Goderich, Ont. Owned by Sims, et. al. Sold Canadian, 1879, after wrecking near Goderich. *Original measurements – rebuilt and enlarged at Algonac, MI, in 1866. **also given as Nov.

name: MARY ANN
detail: She arrived at Marblehead, her destination, and came to anchor off shore. A sudden squall tore her from her moorings and she went ashore, where she was quickly pounded to pieces. A few days later her outfit was salvaged, but the vessel was a total loss, financially ruining her owner and skipper, Capt. Henderson. Rebuilt in 1869. Sold U.S. before 1860.

name: MARY ANNA
detail: This little rafting tug foundered while attempting to make Tobermorey harbor for shelter from a Lake Huron storm. Her skipper and engineer drowned. The vessel was recovered the following year and totally rebuilt at Wiarton, becoming the tug MINNIE KIDD. Owned by Joseph Kidd, Dublin, Ont. Sold Canadian in 1877.

name: MARY D
place_of_loss: 17 mi N of Sand Beach USLS station
detail: She sprang a leak in a NW gale and was abandoned by her crew after she filled. The Pte. Aux Barques Lifesaving Service crew pulled out to her and were able to get her into Sand Beach with the help of the tug JONES, but she was apparently stripped and abandoned. Document surrendered at Port Huron Sep 30, 1901, annotated “Total loss off Pte Aux Barques, 9-1-01.” Rebuilt and lengthened in about 1887.

name: MARY ELIZABETH
other_names: none – sometimes seen as MARY AND ELIZABETH
detail: She went ashore in a storm and was reported a month later as broken up. Probably out of Detroit.

name: MARY FRANCES
build_info: 1848, L. Shickluna, Prescott, Ont. (1851 also given)
place_of_loss: near “Chervell” in the vicinity of Rondeau, Ont.
detail: “Wrecked,” and a total loss of about $2,600. Major repairs several times.

name: MARY HATTIE
place_of_loss: near Whitestone Point, MI, lower Saginaw Bay
detail: She lost her sails and began to sink in a storm. Her crew took to the rigging as she went down and were rescued by the Tawas Point Lifesaving Service crew. Owned by Capt. McKay of Port Huron Went ashore and declared a total loss near Forestville, MI, in 1888. *place of build also given as New Liverpool, MI Major repairs in 1881 and 82

name: MARY JANE
detail: Driven back by a gale while attempting to make Chicago harbor, she was eventually abandoned by her crew and went ashore, where she broke up. Another MARY JANE, a schooner, was reported wrecked and a total loss at Chicago, Dec 22, 1857. She capsized off the harbor entrance after being denied entrance by a heavy northeaster. Out of Manitowoc. [nsp only] May be the vessel of this name reported sunk at Toronto in 1855.

name: MARY JANE
detail: Left Pinconning, Michigan, for Toledo Sep 2 and was seen off Port Austin two days later. October papers reported that she had gone down of Kettle Point. Besides the captain and two crew, the captain’s wife, four children and sister-in-law were aboard. Listed in 1884 Merchant Vessels as “lost or otherwise out of service” Out of Port Clinton, Oh *Official number changed from 17298 to 90077 between 1869 and 1872.

name: MARY JANE
place_of_loss: Long Point Cut, near Port Rowan, Ont.
detail: Bound Port Hope. Ont. for Erie, PA., she was driven ashore by a storm and broke up. The first known of her fate was when wreckage washed ashore near Dunkirk, NY, on the 20 th . Owned and sailed by Capt. Flanagan and others of Toronto. Ashore on Long Point in the fall of 1863 and not recovered until the following spring. Another MARY JANE (US#90077) is shown in 1884 Merchant Vessels as “lost or otherwise out of service.”

name: MARY KAY
detail: Burned to a total loss. Out of Milwaukee

name: MARY M
detail: She struck the Chicago harbor breakwater and sank off the city. All of those aboard made their way to shore safely.

name: MARY MARGARET
detail: Bound for Chicago, she was caught in a gale, filled and capsized. The crew clung to the heaving wreck for four hours until rescued by the schooner MAGIC. On May 30, 1853 she capsized between Muskegon and Grand Haven in heavy weather. Four crewmen were trapped below and drowned, while the captain and mate held on for 60 hours until picked up by a passing ship. The hulk was towed in two weeks later. Master: Capt. Bergh.

name: MARYLAND
detail: She was driven ashore in a big, general gale, along with a number of other vessels. She pounded to pieces and became a toal loss of $7,000. Owned by H. N. Gates, Cleveland. Master: probably Capt. John Pomeroy. Sunk at Fairport in 1845; ashore near Long Point Cut in 1848; aground in Sandusky Bay in 1853.

name: MARYSBURG
other_names: built on the hull of the 1854 schooner JAMES LESLIE also seen as MARYSBURGH
detail: She went ashore and wrecked, a total loss. Also in trouble on Pt.Pelee in 1871, and damaged in a collision off Braddock’s Point, Lake Ontario, in the spring of 1876. Master: Capt. Sullivan.

name: MARYSVILLE
other_names: built as propeller NORMANDIE, renamed in 1920
place_of_loss: mouth of Belle River, Marine City, MI
detail: She burned to the waterline and sank at the mouth of the river. She had been tied up at a dock when she caught fire. Owned by Service Gravel Co., Marysville, MI.
sources: sol,ns3,mv,mpl,eas —-——————————— MASON – see also GOVERNOR MASON

name: JANE MASON
detail: Coming into the harbor in a storm, she veered to avoid the inbound tug BARRETT, towing the disabled schooner SEA BIRD. She went on a sandbar and was soon pounded to pieces. Owned by Betz of Tawas City and upbound from New Baltimore, Mich.. She broke in two quickly and was purchased for pennies on the dollar by a local salvager. Document surrendered at Port Huron 11/20/1890 annotated “wrecked, total loss.”

name: L.G. MASON
detail: Burned to a total loss and sank at her dock at the Middle Grounds, where she was laid up for the night. Remains are still visible near Lafayette Bridge, Bay City. Out of E. Saginaw, owned by the “Saginaw Line” [English & Mahar] Rebuilt, 1879

name: L. M. MASON
detail: She was driven ashore by a gale, along with at least 8 other schooners in the same vicinity. She was stripped and abandoned the following week, and presumably broke up.

name: MAGGIE MASON
detail: Went ashore and broke up.

name: NELLIE MASON
detail: Outbound for Alpena in tow of the steamer LANSING with the barge B.W. PALMER, she struck the breakwall and stranded. Not too badly damaged initially, but destroyed by a series of storms before she could be released. Master: Capt. J. B. Mitchell. Owner: F. W. Gilchrist, Alpena. Stranded and burned on Observation Point, near Alpena in Oct, 1887, declared total loss and documents surrendered, but later refloated and rebuilt. Also had major repairs in 1883, 1895

name: R.P. MASON
build_info: 1867, H. C. Pearsons, Ferrysburg (Grand Haven), MI
loss_of_life_original: 2 [lost in the dock fire, not on the ship]
detail: She was loading a cargo of sawmill equipment when she caught fire. She burned through her lines and drifted into the bay, where she was consumed down to the waterline. The dock and sheds were also destroyed. Some remains were reportedly still visible at low water as late as 1975. Owned by Dan Seavey, Goulais Bay. Capsized and sank, drowning 5, in the Straits, Oct 15, 1871, during a salvage operation. The following summer she was raised and towed to St. Helena inverted by stuffing her hull with cedar posts to make her bouyant. Ashore with heavy damage near Chicago in May of 1883. Driven ashore and broke her back near Marinette, WI, May 2, 1905. June 30, 1917 is also given as the date of her loss.

name: MASSASAUGA
detail: Burned to a total loss. Owner: H. S. Dale, St Clair, Mich Also shown as burned in 1888

name: MASSASOIT
other_names: built as schooner JESSE H. LINN, renamed in 1888
detail: Stranded on the waterworks intake crib and sank. She was considered uneconomical to salvage, and was abandoned, then dynamited as a hazard to navigation in the spring of 1905. Her stranding and the subsequent blasting caused expensive damage to the water works and its crib. Master: Capt. Chas. Keenan. Spent the winter of 1874-5 ashore, thought to be a total loss, but recovered in June of the following year.

name: MASSILON
type_at_loss: schooner, wood, 3-mast with foretop & topgallant sails*
build_info: 1857, Roderick Calkins, Cleveland as a bark
place_of_loss: 20 mi above Pte Aux Barques, MI
detail: Bound Kelley’s Island, on Lake Erie, for Chicago, she sprang a leak – probably from her heavy cargo – and went down in about 20 minutes. Her crew quickly abandoned her in her yawl and drifted for five hours until picked up by the tug VULCAN. Owned by James Corrigan, Cleveland Master: Capt. N. L. Sherwood. *officially a schooner, but usually described as a bark. Probably same vessel as wrecked in Sleeping Bear Bay in Dec, 1858.

name: JOSEPH G. MASTEN
build_info: 1867, Quayle & Martin, Cleveland as a bark
detail: She went ashore and wrecked just south of the lighthouse. She had been bound Lorain for Milwaukee and was dragged ashore when her tow steamer KEYSTONE was driven aground. At first it was thought she could be recovered, but she sank down in the sand until she was unsalvageable. Owner: Frank C. Goodyear or George L. Colwell. Ashore with heavy damage on South Fox Is., Lake Michigan, in June, 1885.
media_notes: Image as a bark from GLMD

name: MATAAFA
detail: Outbound from Duluth with the barge JAMES NASMYTH, she was unable to breast a terrific gale. She turned back, dropped the barge some miles out and tried to make the harbor. Her machinery failed and she was driven on the breakwater, where she broke in two. Heroic effort by Lifesaving Service saved the forward crew but those stranded on the after end froze to death within sight of a crowd gathered on the breakwater. Part of the giant Pittsburgh Fleet. Master: Capt. R. F. Humble. Recovered, converted to auto carrier in ’46, scrapped in ’65

name: MATERIAL SERVICE
type_at_loss: motor vessel, steel, bulk freight, self-unloading
build_info: 1929, L. Smith, Sturgeon Bay, WI hull# 253
detail: Foundered in a heavy summer gale. Master: Capt. Charles Brown(d). She was a canal motor barge not designed for open-lake use. She had been designed for Chicago canal use and was low enough to go under most bridges without the raising of them. MATERIAL SERVICE is the official name of the vessel. She was not officially removed from documentation until 1957. Wreck lies in 31 ft of water, 2100 ft, 12 deg from Calumet harbor light. Virtual image of the wreck from Indiana Dept of Natural Resources

name: SAMUEL MATHER
detail: She collided with the upbound package freighter BRAZIL in drizzly rain and sank in the early a.m. BRAZIL picked up her crew. She was a major loss of $176,000, going down in 300 feet of water 8 miles offshore. Home port: Cleveland – owned by Pickands, Mather & Co. Wreckage, in well-preserved state, was located by divers in 1978.

name: SAMUEL L. MATHER
other_names: also seen as S. L. MATHER
place_of_loss: 4 miles off Cat Head Point, near Leelanau, MI
detail: She was bound Escanaba for Cleveland with a heavy load. She collided on a dark, rainy night with the schooner MARY COPLEY, which was bound from Chicago on the opposite tack. She sank quickly in deep water, while COPLEY stood by and picked up her crew. Owned by C.P. Minch and Capt. Treuter of Cleveland. Treuter was also her master.

name: MATILDA
detail: This little schooner went ashore in a gale near the wreck of the schooner NEW YORK(qv) from the same storm. Her master: Capt. Cameron(d), and all of her crew perished.

name: MATILDA
place_of_loss: near Pine River, lower Saginaw Bay
detail: Tow of the tug PETER SMITH, she was cut by ice and went down. in 35 ft of water off Pte. Au Gres. SMITH saved her crew. Bound Kelley’s I., Lake Erie, for Saginaw. Owner: A. A. Pinet, Saginaw. Master: Capt. Crippen. Former St. Lawrence R. river barge sold U.S. in 1866, and owned out of E. Saginaw.

name: MATOA
place_of_loss: Port Austin Reef, mouth of Saginaw Bay
detail: A powerful vessel, she was driven aground by the “Big Storm” of 1913. Declared a total loss, she was later recovered & rebuilt at great cost. Cut in two and reassembled on saltwater, 1915. Cut in two again and returned to Lakes as a Canadian in 1923. Scrapped in 1937 after a period of idleness. Engraving from MHGL

name: MATTAWAN
detail: Stranded in a gale near Richmondville, Mich, with her consort GIBRALTAR and wrecked, a total loss. Owned by H & A Allan, Montreal. Her machinery later was salvaged. Laid up 1884-6, Rebuilt, 1887

name: WILLIAM MATTHEW
detail: The steamer OWEGO encountered her as a floating derelict and attempted to tow her in to Cleveland. Due to heavy weather OWEGO was forced to abandon her. It was not reported if her crew was located nor if the vessel was ever seen again, but she disappears from the record at this time. Probably out of Sackett’s Harbor, Lake Ontario. Other sources say she was lost in 1861. At Smith & Hall’s Mill.

name: MAUD L
build_info: 1907, C. W. Menten, Parry Sd., Ont.
detail: Burned to a total loss. Owned be C.E and E.S Pratt, Parry Sound, Ont. Inage from GLMD

name: MAUDE
detail: Burned to a total loss at her dock. Sunk in a collision with the U. S. steamer SURVEY in 1904. Sunk in a collision with the prop HILTON in 1915.

name: MAUDE
other_names: built as GRACE DARLING[C#85706], renamed in 1902
place_of_loss: in the lake – no exact location given
detail: No detail. Owner: W. H. Biden, Chatham. Originally 51x13x4, 26 tons, rebuilt and enlarged in 1902 – renumbered at that time.

name: MAUMEE VALLEY
detail: After she was driven aground on the middleground near Kingsville by a gale, her crew clung to her rigging for three days while several rescues were attempted, particularly by the Canadian tug HOME RULE, Capt. Hackett. Finally all died before help could reach them. Hackett complained bitterly that the men would have been saved had not the Kingsville lifesaving crew’s boat been unusable. Master: Capt. Henry Scanlan(d). She went ashore near Port Colborne a fortnight earlier. Ashore and abandoned on Lake Superior in fall, 1869.

name: THEODORE W. MAURICE
other_names: also seen as T. W. MAURICE
type_at_loss: schooner, wood, 2-mast [also referred to as a brig]
detail: She passed Milwaukee in a waterlogged and out-of-control condition, then went on the rocks and capsized near Wind Pt. Her crew made it to shore, but the vessel broke up, a total loss. Out of Cleveland In 1874 it was reported that she had carried the smallest cargo of grain that had ever left Lake Michigan for an Eastern port. Rebuilt in 1841

name: MAUTENEE
build_info: 1873, A. Turner, Trenton, MI as a 3-mast schooner
detail: She broke loose from her tow steamer and was driven ashore. Her crew was rescued by the Lifesaving Service from the beach with a breeches’ buoy. The vessel was torn to pieces by storms that followed. Registered out of Bay City; owner and master: Capt. Thomas Madden. Official records say she was lost this date near Pictou, Ont rebuilt in 1882
media_notes: Image of her ashore from GLMD

name: MAXWELL A
build_info: 1891, J. Davidson, W. Bay City US#92302
detail: Reportedly burned to total loss, but still listed on “Canadian List of Shipping” in 1920 – dropped in 1923. Built and worked for Gilchrist Transp. Co. for several yrs. Fishing tug on and off over the years. Sold Canadian in 1909.

name: A.C. MAXWELL
place_of_loss: Woolworth’s Dock, Sault Ste. Marie, MI
detail: Twenty feet of her bow were sheared off by the big steel steamer R. W. ENGLAND, which went out of control and struck her. She was a total loss and was abandoned in place. Owner: W. E. Donley, East Saginaw, Mich. Also sunk in 1883 and 1885. In the 1885 incident, near Goderich, Ont., a group of Canadian fishermen rescued her crew and were later awarded lifesaving medals by the U.S. Lifesaving Service.

name: EMILY B. MAXWELL
build_info: 1881, Hanson & Scove*, Manitowoc, WI as a schooner US#135536
detail: Driven on the breakwater at Cleveland and torn to pieces. *builder also shown as Rand & Burger Sold Canadian, 1903
media_notes: Image of her ashore from GLMD

name: WILLIAM MAXWELL
place_of_loss: Thunder Bay Reef, Thunder Bay, MI
detail: Stranded and demolished in a storm. Owned by H. J. Knight, Erie, Pa

name: MAY FLOWER
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: She struck an ice floe in fog and sank in Pelee Passage. The steamer PEARL remained with her for several days to try to save her, but finally stripped and abandoned her on Dec 2. Owned by Michigan Central Line. Master: Capt. Hobson. Her cargo was salvaged in 1889. Ashore near Conneaut, Ohio in Dec 1852. Released the following spring and rebuilt. She was a very large vessel for her day.

name: MAY FLOWER
detail: Stranded and wrecked.

name: MAY FLOWER
detail: This steamer caught fire at night while tied to her dock and burned to the waterline, her crew having only enough time to escape in their nightclothes. She was a small supply steamer running the eastern shore of Georgian Bay. Owned by F. Morrow, Waubaushene, Ont. Thought to be a total loss, but later recovered and rebuilt – lasted to 1912.

name: MAY QUEEN
detail: Bound Milwaukee for Oswego in a blinding blizzard, she got off course and struck the bar south of Tawas Point. Her crew took to the rigging as she went down, wrapping themselves in the sails for warmth, until they were rescued by local fishermen the next day. Recently sold to Oswego parties, formerly out of Milwaukee. Her wreckage probably discovered off Tawas Point in low-water year of 2000.

name: MAY QUEEN
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
place_of_loss: near Sheboygan, WI (also given as Cheboygan, MI)
detail: She stranded tightly; tugs that were sent to her aid were unable to release her that fall and she was left in place. During the winter she was released and towed to Milwaukee, where she caught fire and burned to the waterline Jan 15, 1866. Her hull was reportedly later resurrected and rebuilt to a towbarge, which foundered on Lake Michigan while under tow, Oct 31, 1868. Owned by Goodrich Line in 1865. Another MAY QUEEN, a steamer, was reported ashore and total loss at Port Colborne in November of 1866. Cargo grain. Value $2000. (globe)
sources: is,rsh,less,lhl,hgl,mpl,rsl,wl,hr,wls —-——————————— MAYERS – See POLLY M. ROGERS —-——————————— MAYFLOWER – See also MAY FLOWER, COMET (1861 loss)

name: MAYFLOWER
detail: Bound Port Elgin, Ont., for Buffalo, she was dismasted off Point aux Barques by a gale, then was driven southeastward to founder off Port Sanilac, Mich. Her crew were taken off by local fishermen, who risked their lives in the rescue. Owned by M. J. Grogan of Chicago and her master, Capt. Dillon. Probably also the vessel that capsized off Black River, OH, in 1864, killing 8. Rebuilt following this accident.

name: MAYFLOWER
detail: In good weather, she began to leak heavily and went down in about 30 feet of water, after her crew had heaved most of her cargo overboard to try to save her. She was raised a few days later and towed in to a point 30 rods off Huntington’s Dock, Kelley’s Island and there abandoned as a total loss. Probably out of Cleveland

name: MAYFLOWER
detail: She drove on the reef in a gale and wrecked. By Sep 17 she had gone on her beam ends andpounded to pieces. Master: Capt. William O’Neil. Her machinery was struck by and sunk the prop ANTELOPE on the reef 10 days later, and a great salvage effort was made to recover MAYFLOWER’s machinery in November. This was finally accomplished by Odette & Wherry of Windsor in May, 1882.

name: MAYFLOWER
place_of_loss: Point Abino, near Buffalo [also given as Long Point]
detail: Stranded broadside to the beach and pounded to pieces in a gale. Her crew made it to shore in her yawl. Owned by Harry Blanchard of Detroit Master: Capt. Cramer. Built as a very fine passenger steamer, rebuilt at Detroit to this steam barge in 1867-68.

name: MAYFLOWER
place_of_loss: S end of Madeline Isl., Apostles group
detail: Bound Portage Entry for Duluth in tow of the tug CORA A. SHELDON, she capsized after her deck cargo shifted and sank near LaPorte, WI in a NW gale. 3 crew were saved by the tug. Owned by Leathem & Smith, Sturgeon Bay, WI. Master: Capt. Zeerbst(d). This or another scow of the same name was capsized off Cleveland in Aug, 1871, and left to go to shore to break up.

name: MAYFLOWER
detail: This steamer caught fire at night while tied to her dock and burned to the waterline, her crew having only enough time to escape in their nightclothes. She was a small supply steamer running the easter shore of Georgian Bay. Owned by F. Morrow, Waubaushene, Ont. Thought to be a total loss, but later recovered and rebuilt – lasted to 1912.

name: EMMA A. MAYS
other_names: also seen as E.A. MAYES, EMMA A. MAYES, EMMA ELIDA MAYES [from launch article]
build_info: 1871, P. Lester, Marine City, MI as a schooner
place_of_loss: off Grand Isl., near Munising, MI
detail: Bound Cleveland for Thunder Bay, Ont in tow of the tug MUSIC, she struck an ice floe and sank quickly in 90 fathoms of water. Her crew abandoned. She had just come out of Grand Island, Michigan, where she had lain all winter, to complete her trip. Document surrendered Jun 24, 1884. Owned by F. Morrell of Detroit Major repairs in 1880

name: NELLIE MAYS
detail: Destroyed by fire at Saginaw, according to her document surrendered in 1878. One source says she was still in service afterward, destroyed by fire in 1887. Above is official. Not in ’84 mvus

name: MAZEPPA
other_names: built as steamer FARMER, name changed in 1851
date_of_loss: 1856, Nov [probably the great storm of the 4 th ]
detail: While sheltering from a gale under the lee of Chantry Island, she dragged her anchor and went ashore, where she was pounded to pieces. Her usual route was Southampton-Detroit. Reported as ashore on the 12 th . A schooner of this name was built 1854, Montreal (77x17x7, 78gc), seized by U.S. Govt at Detroit, 1856. Rebuilt, 1851 at Bath, Ont.

name: MAZEPPA
place_of_loss: near Norwood, MI, Grand Traverse Bay
detail: Just as she pulled away from the pier, she went out of control, went ashore and was wrecked. A few days later she was regarded as a total loss, abandoned. Out of Chicago.

name: McARTHUR
place_of_loss: near Collins Bay, Ont., 8 mi W of Kingston
detail: Caught fire while working on raising the propeller ARMSTRONG and burned to a total loss. She was worth about $17,000. Owned by Collins Bay Rafting Co., homeport: Kingston.

name: JAMES McBRIDE
detail: Bound from Manitou Islands to Detroit, she was forced ashore and wrecked. Her gear and rigging were salvaged in November. Out of Chicago, owned by Stafford. Rammed by schooner C.R. WILLIAMS and sunk near Milwaukee, Oct. 6, 1855. 1st vessel to have carried a cargo directly from W Indies to Chicago [1848]. Also made other trips to the Atlantic.

name: A.J. McBRIER
detail: Had just finished unloading household goods for the establishment of a lumber camp when she caught fire and burned to a total loss. Out of Saginaw. Master: Capt. C. B. Moiles. Converted from schooner to steamer after 1 900

name: FRED McBRIER
build_info: 1881, F. Wheeler, W Bay City hull #12
place_of_loss: 7 mi E of Waugoschance Pt., western Straits
detail: She collided with the propeller PROGRESS in fog and sank in 7 minutes, having been struck squarely amidships. She had been downbound towing the barges A.STEWART and J.B. LOZEN, and the two steamers had misunderstood signals while passing. Owned out of Detroit by N. S. Whipple and Capt. John Andrews [her master] Located by divers in 1966.

name: D.W. McCALL
detail: Sprang a leak 20-25 miles out and made for the harbor. When she came in she became unmanageable and struck a pier, going ashore. At the time she was seen to be breaking up and there is no further mention of her in the papers. Canadian listing shows her registration closed in 1927 as “wrecked, Port Burwell” but doesn’t give a date of the actual wreck, only the closing of the document.

name: W. J. McCARTER
detail: Burned to a total loss with no one aboard. Probably at her dock at Erie, Pa. Fired on by the Canadian fisheries cruiser VIGILANT on Lake Erie in September, 1905 over a fishing rights dispute. The tug limped back to port with a large hole near her waterline.

name: D. McCLELLAN
other_names: none also seen as D.M. McCLELLAN
place_of_loss: off Pt. Lookout, lower Saginaw bay
detail: She was reported lost after she failed to arrive at Bay City after a short trip there from Grindstone City. Bodies of her crew began washing ashore on Pt. Lookout about 10 days later. Master: Capt. Trudo(d). Documents not surrendered until Sept, 1885. Wreck removed in 1888. * also given as Mud Lake, St. Mary’s R.

name: GEORGE B. McCLELLAN
detail: Burned to a total loss. Also reported to have burned at Michigan City in 1898. Out of Chicago for her entire long career. Major repair in 1871.

name: B.B. McCOLL
other_names: built as H.M.S.SERVITOR, renamed PULOE BRANI in 1923, B.B. McCOLL in 1926 , A.J. PATMORE in 1929, ROTARY in 1942, A.J. PATMORE again in 1945, PEGGY REINAUER in 1946 and DETROIT in 1955
place_of_loss: in harbor at Buffalo, NY [McColl Bros Dock]
detail: She was lying at her dock when she was approached by the burning tank barge JAS. F. CAHILL (qv), which had burned through her mooring lines and drifted away. Though followed by the fireboat WILLIAM S. GRATTAN, the barge set the McCOLL ablaze and soon the fumes in her empty tanks caused a tremendous explosion and fire, enveloping all three vessels. McCOLL was declared a total loss, but later recovered & converted to a tank barge. Repowered in 1930. Built as a British naval tanker, sold American 1928 after this accident. She was assigned to the U.S. Navy as ROTARY (YO-148), a fuel oil tanker, from 1942 to 45.

name: J. H.
detail: No detail Shows in hgl but not Polk or other reference sources. Merchant vessels of 1901 includes her, but not after that. Some sources says she may have been operating in 1900.

name: ANTHONY McCUE
detail: Broke away from the tug BARRYTON and stranded, a total loss. Owned by Anthony O’Boyle, New York, NY

name: J. McCULLOUGH
detail: Wrecked. Document surrendered at Port Huron in 1879.

name: J.G. McCULLOUGH
place_of_loss: 20 miles from Bailey’s Harbor, WI [Clay Banks]
detail: She was bound Escanaba for Kenosha early in the morning fog when she collided with the bark PENSAUKEE out of Pensaukee, WI, for Chicago. One of her masts fell and smashed her lifeboat, but her crew was able to clamber aboard the PENSAUKEE before McCOLLOUGH sank about 20 minutes later. Owner: Capt. A. Miller & Co., Kenosha. Out of Kenosha, WI

name: CHARLES C. McDONALD
detail: Bound for Saginaw, she caught fire and was fully ablaze so quickly that her crew had to dive overboard and swim for shore. Owned by Geo. C. Sherbert.

name: J.H. McDONALD
detail: Burned to a total loss. Owned by R. Thompson, Southampton, Ont. Reg. out of Collingwood

name: JOHN A. McDONALD
other_names: also seen as J.A. McDONALD – built on hull of J.A. TORRANCE
place_of_loss: on Long Point, about 5 miles “above the point”
detail: She was loading her cargo from a dock when a sudden squall blew up, destroyed the dock, and drove the scow ashore. A tug worked on her, but she was not expected to be recovered as it was thought she would not hold together under strong pulling. She was owned by her captain and her engineer. Ashore with heavy damage at Port Burwell, Ont, Oct, 1864. * built on hull of J. A. TORRANCE, which was built at Wellington Sq. Ont. in 1841

name: JOHN A. McDONALD
type_at_loss: propeller, wood sometimes listed as a large tug
detail: She was driven into shallows, struck bottom and sank. Declared a total loss, but reportedly later recovered. Shows on official lists only sporadically from here on, but hgl shows her still in use as late as 1898. Some sources show her as a paddle steamer, others a schooner
sources: clu,h,do,wl,nsp not in mmgl —-——————————— LOUISA McDONALD – See LILY E. —-——————————— R. McDONALD – See PINE LAKE

name: ROBERT McDONALD
other_names: none (may have been former schooner PARTHENON)
build_info: 1890, Hepburn, Picton, Ont. as a schooner-barge
detail: Sank in the harbor and was abandoned. Heavily damaged by fire on a trip from Peninsular Point to Stoney Point, Lake Ontario in July 1908. Converted from 2-mast schooner to steam in 1907.

name: DUNCAN ELLIOT McFARLAND
place_of_loss: near Morgan Point, 10 mi W of Port Colborne, Ont.
detail: Foundered off the point after springing a leak. It was speculated that the tug’s seams opened because she had been in ordinary for a long period and her topside planking had dried out and shrunk, allowing water washing onto the decks to pour into her hull. Sold Canadian, 1876

name: JOHN A. McGEAN
detail: Considered unsinkable, she foundered in the “Big Storm” of 1913. Her exact position when she went down was a mystery until her wreckage was located in 1985 near where she was last sighted. Master: Capt. C. R. Ney(d).

name: NELLIE McGILVRAY
other_names: also seen as N. McGILVRAY, NELLIE McGILVERAY, NELLIE McGILVERY , NELLIE McGILVRA
detail: She struck the end of the pier at Portage entrance to the canal, sunk and became a total loss. Blown up by dynamiting as a hazard, Oct 26, 1882. Out of Cleveland. Also wrecked on Lake Erie in 1874. *A barge NELLIE McGILORA of Buffalo is reported as receiving registration no. 93835 in Jun, 1873. She was also 427 t.

name: J.G. McGRATH
detail: Foundered in 150 ft. of water. Her crew was at first reported as lost, but later were reported at Port Colborne, 16 miles distant, where they had rowed in the small yawlboat. Hailed from St. Catharines, owned by H. C. Dunlap. Master: Capt. McAuley. Also ashore on Long Point with heavy damage in Nov, 1873.

name: MARY A. McGREGOR
detail: Grounded, she caught fire and burned to the waterline before she could be released.

name: JAMES McKAY
place_of_loss: near the piers in Chicago harbor
detail: Foundered in a gale in the harbor. Out of Detroit, owned by F. J. Parks, St. Joseph, Mich.

name: JOHN M. McKERCHEY
detail: She capsized and sank in 30 feet of water after springing a leak. Her skipper went down with the ship, probably becoming wedged in the pilothouse door as the boat went over. The rest of crew went into the water or into a lifeboat and were rescued by the Coast Guard. She went down 1.2 miles NW of W breakwater light. Wreck was removed in 1954. Master: Capt. Horace Johnson(d).

name: P.E. McKERRAL
detail: Burned to the waterline at her home port. Possibly recovered. Not in clu after 1878.

name: MARY E. McLACHLAN
build_info: 1893, F. Wheeler, W. Bay City US#92503
loss_of_life_original: some sources show as many as 23
loss_of_life: some sources show as many as 23
detail: Foundered in a gale. Also stranded with heavy damage at Black Bay, Ont., Oct 20, 1913. Document surrendered, but recovered and rebuilt. Sold Canadian in 1916

name: MARY McLANE
place_of_loss: Niagara River at Sour Spring Grove
detail: She caught fire while running from Tonawanda to Buffalo. She was beached to save her crew and eventually burned out. The hulk was later towed to Buffalo, where the engine was removed and the hull abandoned. Master: Capt. T. W. O’Connor. Owner: Independent Towing Co. Rebuilt, 1880

name: J. LOOMIS McLAREN
place_of_loss: off 27th St. N of downtown Chicago
detail: Lost from tow of a tug in a terrific gale when her towpost ripped out, spraying flying gear which killed her mate. She went into the shallows where police and bystanders rescued the balance of her crew. Broke up in place. Master: Capt. K. Johnson; out of Sturgeon Bay.

name: ANDREW A. McLEAN
detail: Foundered in a storm. Document surrendered at Port Huron Jul 9, 1917.

name: R.
other_names: built as ALICE M CAMPBELL, US105237
detail: She caught fire and burned to a total loss at the south end of St. Joseph Island. The cause of the fire is unknown. Owned by M. McLean, Sault Ste Marie, Ont. *1907 also given Sold Canadian 1901.

name: D. McLEOD
place_of_loss: Finlay Bay near Silver Islet, Ont.
detail: Burned to a total loss. Out of Port Arthur, Ont.

name: JANE McLEOD
other_names: none also seen as JAMES McLEOD
detail: She stranded and became a total loss in Georgian Bay, while anchored for the night off the island. She left her name behind on McLeod Island, where she went ashore. A vessel identified as the McLEOD is a frequent dive target in the area. BUT one source reports her sunk in Niagara Bay, Lake Erie in 1891! Rebuilt, 1880

name: SYDNEY C. McLOUTH
other_names: built as steamer ROCHESTER renamed in 1906
detail: She burned to a total loss and sank. Wreck located 4.75 mi 175deg 35′ from Oconto South Pierhead light in 18 feet of water.

name: McMULLEN & PITZ DREDGE
detail: Enroute to Sheboygan in tow to dredge the river, she sank in a storm in 75 ft. of water.
sources: nsp —-——————————— P.B. McNAUGHTON – See JOSEPH H.

name: JOHN A. McPHAIL
place_of_loss: several miles north of Pte Aux Barques [18 mi N also given]
detail: Chained in tandem behind the tug G.W. ROGERS with the similar ferry JAMES W. CURRAN (qv). CURRAN was struck by tornado and sank, McPHAIL dragged down behind, into 200 ft deep water. Both vessels had been decommissioned and were being towed to Kingston, Ont. for refitting before going into service to Wolfe Isl., Ont. Owned by Ontario Dept. of Highways. Link to clipping , another (p.6) Link to photo

name: BELLE McPHEE
place_of_loss: near Thornbury, Ont., W of Collingwood
detail: Caught in a spring “ice shove” crushed and sank in 50 fathoms of water, 8 mi N of Nottawasaga light. Her crew barely had time to escape her sinking. Also reported as having stranded. Master: Capt. A. McPhee. Owned by A. McPhee, Sr., Owen Sound, father of Capt. Reg. out of Owen Sound.

name: PATRICIA McQUEEN
other_names: built as BALTIMORE, renamed n 1936; renamed MARACAIBO II in 1970, JIGGS in 1982
place_of_loss: W of Livingstone Channel Light, off Belle Isl
detail: Sank in the channel. Later recovered. Registered out of Amherstburg. Ashore on Manitoulin Isl. with the dredge ELLA M, in November, 1948. The crews were rescued by two men from Tobermory, Ont., at great risk. Rebuilt to a yacht in 1970.

name: MAGGIE McRAE
date_of_loss: 1888, May 30 (Jun 4 also given, but wreck reported in some papers Jun 2)
detail: Tow of tug BRUNO(qv) after dark, she stove in her bow planking in collision with an ice sheet and sank. Bound for Kingston. Hailed from St. Catharines. Owned by J Graham.

name: MARY McVEA
other_names: also seen as MARY McVIE, M. McVEY
type_at_loss: schooner, wood (some sources say small steamer.)
place_of_loss: 1/4 mi SW of Walker’s Pt., Manitoulin Isl.
detail: Stranded and wrecked. Due to an ongoing dispute as to whether she could be salvaged by an American tug in Canadian waters, she went to pieces before she could be pulled off. Out of Grand Haven, MI, owned by Jacobsen & McVea. Master: Capt. Edward Mullins.
sources: win,slh,nsp,mv,wmn —-——————————— JOHN J. McWILLIAMS – See M.J. BARTELME

name: MEARS
build_info: 1869, McDole, Lincoln, MI as a 3-mast schooner
detail: She was driven ashore and broke up in a gale. She had been the tow of the steamer S.S. WILHELM with the barge MIDNIGHT (qv), broke loose and drifted around for quite some time. Crew of MIDNIGHT had abandoned their boat and come aboard her earlier. A MEARS crewman died of exposure just as the Lifesaving Service pulled alongside in their rescue boat. Master: Capt. Hill. Rebuilt, 1881

name: CHARLES MEARS
other_names: none sometimes seen as CHARLES MEERS
detail: Destroyed by fire. Lengthened in 1862, major repairs in 1863

name: A. MEDBURY
place_of_loss: 100 yds N of Four Mile Point, near Niagara
detail: Bound for Chicago, she collided near Snake Island with the schooner HERCULES and was cut nearly in two. She sank very quickly, her crew having time only to save themselves. *Also given in error as Lake Michigan Major repairs in 1861,68

name: MEDIATOR
other_names: none built on the bottom of the schooner SINBAD or SINDBAD
detail: Tow of the steamer KALKASKA and bound Duluth for Chicago, she was torn loose in a gale and driven ashore. Her towmate J.H. MEAD was also cast ashore, but was recovered. Crews made it to the beach on their own. Owned by Shoemann, Chicago. Later removed from the beach and towed to Houghton, but too badly damaged to be returned to service. Her remains were later abandoned off the grounds of Michigan Technological University. Sunk in a storm near Racine, WI, in 1888. Thought to be beyond repair, but later towed to Chicago in a waterlogged condition with her decks awash. Major repair in 1883

name: MEDITERRANEAN
place_of_loss: 2 miles S of harbor at Sheboygan, WI*
detail: Bound Alabaster, MI for S. Chicago, she was overwhelmed and foundered in gale. Her crew was rescued, picked up by the schooner JOHN MEE. She was owned by Capt. E.G. Kohnert, her skipper *Also given as 25 miles out Rebuilt, 1881 and 1891

name: MEDORA
place_of_loss: near Little Sandy Creek [25 mi E of Oswego]
detail: She was driven ashore and wrecked in a gale. None of the bodies were ever found. It was speculated that her crew abandoned her in her boat before she came ashore. She had been bound Cleveland for Oswego. Owned by T. Wyman, Oswego. Master: Capt. Archibald Morse(d).

name: MEDORA
build_info: 1861, N. Wheeler or John Hayes, Clayton, NY
place_of_loss: Grim’s Pier, S. of Wolf River
detail: Loading at Grim’s pier when a storm blew up and swung the big schooner H. B. STEELE into her, crushing her hull and sinking her. The storm subsequently pounded her to pieces. Owned by Edw. Readey, Detroit.

name: ADA MEDORA
detail: Drove onto the breakwater in a storm while trying to avoid the steamer UGANDA, which was coming out of the harbor. Her crew made it to the breakwater and were rescued by Lifesaving Service . Sandsucker SANDY HOOK lightered her cargo and was then lost when sank in Buffalo Harbor (see ARGONAUT). MEDORA later broke up. Owned by F. Baldwin of Petoskey, Mich. Master: Capt. Martin Abrams.

name: LOUIS MEEKER
other_names: also seen as LOUISE MEEKER, LOUIE MEEKER, LEWIS MEEKER
detail: Capsized and foundered. Owned out of Chicago by Charles Lindgreen. Master: Capt. West(d). *some sources say she foundered with all hands off Middle Island, near Thunder Bay, Lake Huron. – newspapers report Lake Michigan

name: C.G. MEISEL
other_names: none also seen as C. J. MEISEL
detail: Tow of the tug J. P. CLARK with 3 other barges, she broke loose in a gale and went ashore. While the CLARK was able to save her crew, the vessel broke up. Out of Port Huron, Mich. Probably still owned by Capt. C. G. Meisel. Rammed amidships by the propeller FREE STATE and sank off Bob-Lo Isl, Detroit R, Aug. 10, 1870. The wife and child of the captain were lost.

name: MELBOURNE
other_names: built as ALMA MUNRO, renamed in 1893. Renamed JOHN R in 1910
detail: Bound Montreal to Hamilton, she burned to an almost total loss. Owned by Melbourne Steamship Co., Montreal. She lay abandoned for four years until resurrected and rebuilt to a 225 ton vessel in 1909. Broken up or converted to a barge in 1914, her records show her as “abandoned” that date. The wreck of a barge identified as JOHN R lies off Crescent Beach, Hamilton. **at time of this loss. Numbered C71239 in 1885. Numbered C126461 in 1909 after the major rebuild. Major rebuilds in 1885, 1893, 1909.
media_notes: Image as ALMA MUNRO from GLMD

name: MELROSE
build_info: 1852, Asa Wilcox, Three Mile Bay, NY
detail: Upbound with salt taken on at Bay City, she went ashore in a storm. By Oct 5 she was considered a total wreck, having been driven over a sandbar to a rocky area, where her bottom was stove in. Only a portion of her rigging was saved. Probably owned by J. B. Scott, Detroit. Master: Capt. John C. Brown. Her skipper was reassigned to her fleetmate WILLIAM SANDERSON after the wreck, and was drowned when that vessel was lost the following month. Rebuilt in 1859 and 1869

name: MARIA MELVIN
detail: She rushed to the rescue of the schooner NORWEGIAN(qv), in distress off Oswego and was herself driven ashore by the storm. Out of Oswego. She was later recovered. Damaged by a rupture in her boiler at Oswego on May 19, 1873. Stripped in 1881, her boiler going into the new tug ELIZA J. REDFORD(qv) and abandoned in 1883.

name: ADDIE MEMBERY
other_names: none also seen as ADA MEMBERY, ADDIE MEMBRAY
detail: Trying to enter harbor in NE gale, she struck the breakwater went out of control and was dashed to pieces on shore. The crew were taken off by the USLS. Bound Oswego for Brockville, Ont. She was pulled off a few days later, but was considered to be too badly damaged to rebuild. Out of Sacket’s Harbor, NY, owned by Barney Eveleigh. Master: Capt. John Baley.

name: MENDOTA
detail: She was towing the sister schooner-barges MORNING STAR(qv) & EVENING STAR(qv) when struck by a gale. She lost the tow and began to leak. She was turned and run for shore, but soon foundered, despite heroic efforts to save her. Only one boat was launched, and that one drifted for 29 hours until being picked up near Manitowoc, nearly a hundred miles from the wreck site. The wife and father of the captain were among the lost. Bound Buffalo for Chicago and owned out of E. Saginaw by W. Crosthwaite and his “silent partner,” John Kelderhouse of Buffalo. Master: Capt. A. Fairbanks. EVENING STAR also went down.

name: MENDOTA
detail: The skipper of a downbound schooner saw a vessel capsize in a blow off Milwaukee and it took a couple weeks to account for all of the possible vessels it might have been. It was finally determined that the MENDOTA, bound Milwaukee for Muskegon, Michigan, was the craft, and that all aboard her had perished. Owned by her skipper, Capt. Tobias Christianson(d), Of Milwaukee, and his mate, Otto Hansen.

name: MENEKAUNEE
detail: She broke away from tow of the steamer MANISTIQUE off the Manitous and was driven ashore near Frankfort after a long fight against the elements. Frankfort Lifesaving Service struggled to get their apparatus to the beach but were too late and arrived to find the a mass of lumber, gear and bodies on the beach, being protected by the ship’s dog which died soon after. Owned by Loud & Co. of Oscoda. Master: Capt. Cox(d).

name: MENTOR
other_names: built as HATTIE A. FOX, renamed in 1884
detail: no detail. Also reported as sunk rather than burned.

name: MENTOR
detail: Tied up at her dock on Grassy Point , she was destroyed when the great Cloquet forest fire swept the dock area. Owned by Northern Dock & Dredge Co., Duluth. The fire destroyed all of NDDs floating stock including the dredge DULUTH, the tugs ELLA G. STONE, this tug and JOHN H JEFFREY, JR as well as five or six scows.

name: MERCER
detail: ashore and total loss. homeport: Algonac Rebuilt at Algonac, 1867 At the time of her loss a newspaper claimed she was a former U.S. revenue cutter.

name: MERCHANT
loss_of_life_original: 14 [some estimates as high as 40, though newspaper is specific about who was lost] [all]
detail: Bound Soo for L’Anse, she sailed out into a gale and was never seen again. She was said to be overloaded when she left port. Some of those aboard were mining VIPs, carrying a lot of cash. Owned by Coe & Colt, Detroit Master: Capt. Robert Moore(d), who had broken his leg the day before and tried to talk his friend Capt Robert Brown into taking over the run. In October, 1852, the tips of her masts were discovered 30 feet below the surface, but the locality was not given. Hauled around the Soo Rapids in the winter of 1845-6, to engage in the copper trade.

name: MERCHANT
detail: no detail

name: MERCHANT
detail: Stranded on the reef when the second mate, who was in charge of her on his watch, miscalculated her position and drove her onto the center of the reef at a high rate of speed. Her bottom was totally ripped out. Part of her cargo was salvaged. Owner: Anchor Line. Later reports state that she caught fire after stranding, but contemporary newspaper accounts don’t mention this. Wreck located in the 1990’s. She had stranded on the same reef twice previously. Much of her hull was salvaged later, but some parts still remain on the site. Sunk near Malden, Ont., in 1868. 1st iron cargo ship built on the Great Lakes and 1st one lost. Rebuilt and lengthened in 1872-3.

name: MERCHANT
detail: Stranded and wrecked, little detail. Document surrendered at Port Huron, 6/30/1905, annotated “wrecked near Sandusky, Ohio.”

name: MERCURY
detail: She was smashed to matchwood by a powerful NE gale while trying to anchor in Chicago harbor. Her crew was rescued by bystanders. The vessel broke up soon after. Master: Capt. M. Shumer; out of Grand Haven. Hauling pine planking when driven ashore near Pentwater, MI, Nov 20, 1879 and declared a total loss. Re-covered the following summer.

name: MERIDA
build_info: 1893, F. Wheeler, W. Bay City hull# 95
place_of_loss: off Wheatley, Ont., 40 mi SE of Long Point
detail: Heavily loaded and bound Ft. William, Ont., for Buffalo, she foundered in the “Black Friday Storm” with all hands. Owned by Valley Camp Steamship Co., Cleveland. Built as an unusual bulker with her engine amidships, but it was moved aft in 1904. Wreck located in 1975.
media_notes: Image as built from GLMD Image as she appeared a time of loss, same source

name: E.G. MERRICK
detail: Stranded and lost.

name: M.F. MERRICK
detail: She was run down in a heavy fog by the steamer R.P. RANNEY and dove under bow first in less than a minute. She was well offshore at the time. Bound Port Austin for Portage, MI. Owned at this time by Taylor & Maitland of Detroit. Master: Capt. Alexander Rusho. Built for E.G. Merrick’s “Reindeer Line” out of Detroit Rebuilt, 1877. Also probably ashore in Georgian Bay, 1865.

name: JOHN B. MERRILL
detail: Tow of the steamer F. E. SPINNER, she broke up near Holdridge Shoal in a gale and sank. Her crew abandoned just before she went down and were rescued by local fishermen. Hailed from Buffalo, owned by Connelly Bros. Ashore near Cleveland in Nov, 1883. Crew saved in a textbook “breeches’ buoy” rescue Major repair in 1884

name: MERRIMAC
build_info: 1852, G. S. Weeks, Oswego as a 3-mast schooner
detail: Driven ashore in a storm after she broke loose from tow of the prop MAYFLOWER, along with the barges E. F. GOULD, SOPHIA SMITH and THOMAS P. SHELDON (qv, all). By early November she was gone completely to pieces. SHELDON and GOULD were later recovered, SMITH also lost. Foundered and reported a total loss on Long Point in early May, 1867. Thought to be a total loss, but later recovered and converted to this barge. Rebuilt in 1866

name: C.H. MERRITT
detail: No detail Owned by A. M. Clark, Marysburgh, Ont Also burned to the waterline at Chatham dock, Dec 5, 1887. Registration closed in 1921, reported as having been lost some years previously.

name: THOMAS R. MERRIT
other_names: also seen as T.R. MERRIT and just MERRIT
place_of_loss: 5 mi E of Fair Haven, NY, light
detail: Driven ashore – so high out of the water that crew were able to step off onto the beach from her decks. Her back was found to be broken and she became a total loss of about $5,000. Bound Hamilton for Oswego in the same storm as the ALBACORE(qv) loss. Registered out of Hamilton, Ont. and owned by Capt. McKay. Master: D. W. Carson. Rebuilt, 1883

name: C. H. MERRITT
detail: Bound Chicago for her home port of Goderich, she was driven ashore and wrecked, a total loss. Owned by Shickluna, Goderich

name: H.F. MERRY
place_of_loss: near Silver Creek, NY, 16 mi above Buffalo
detail: Blown ashore by a storm, she hogged and broke up by the 16th. The crew came ashore in her yawl. The swelling of her cargo split her seams after she went ashore, destroying her hull. Her soggy cargo was later sold for whiskey distilling purposes. Bound Detroit for Buffalo, master: Capt. Nichols. Rebuilt in 1869 [to barge] and 1881 [back to schooner]

name: MESQUITE
detail: Coast Guard vessel struck a rocky bar due to a navigational error and sank. USCG thought she would weather over the winter [though few vessels stranded in that area ever had] but she was damaged beyond repair by winter storms and ice. Partially stripped in the spring and sunk [actually lowered by a huge floating crane] to become part of an underwater preserve in 1990. Served in the Pacific in WWII.

name: MESSENGER
detail: Bound Gosfield, Ont., for Detroit, she went ashore and was wrecked. By the 14th she had broken up.

name: MESSENGER
build_info: 1866, E.M. Peck, Cleveland as a passenger vessel
detail: Caught fire in the harbor, but cut loose and allowed to drift out into the lake. About five miles out she burned to the waterline and sank. Owned by Stephenson of Detroit and Hoeft of Rogers City.
media_notes: Image as passenger vessel from GLMD

name: METAMORA
detail: This aged tug, while working as a passenger and freight vessel, caught fire and burned to total loss. Sold Canadian in 1865. *51688 also given Major repair in 1875

name: METEOR
detail: Destroyed in a dock fire, along with warehouses and docks. Thought to be beyond repair, but later recovered Collided with her sister PEWABIC and sank her in 1865. Later in the same month she burned at Sault Ste. Marie, MI, with heavy damage. Struck a boulder and sank near Put-In-Bay in 1871, major repairs followed. Rebuilt in 1882 after a wreck, became schooner-barge NELSON BLOOM. Abandoned in 1925.

name: METEOR
place_of_loss: off Ford Shoal, near Oswego, NY
detail: Having lost some sail and been overladen with ice the previous night, she was trying to make Oswego when she began to founder after her pumps froze. The steamer MILWAUKEE tried to take her in tow, but ended up just taking her crew off before the vessel foundered. She had been bound Oakville, Ont., Ogdensburg, NY. Owned by F. J. Brown, Oakville. Sold Canadian, 1862

name: METEOR
other_names: built as steamer FRANK ROCKEFELLER,renamed SOUTH PARK in 1928, given this name in 1943
build_info: 1896, W Superior Shipbuilding, W Superior, WI hull# 136
detail: Went aground and damaged, ending her career. Later salvaged and converted to a museum ship located at Superior, WI. Rebuilt from bulk freighter to a tanker in 1943

name: METROPOLE
other_names: built as steamer SAKIE SHEPARD*, renamed in 1900
place_of_loss: 10 mi N of Port Austin, W of Pte Aux Barques
detail: She began to leak profusly after her propeller-shaft seal failed and soon foundered. Her crew abandoned after pumps began to lose ground against the water. Owned by John Stevenson, Detroit. Foundered near Turtle Isl., Maumee R., in April of 1895, with the loss of one life. Heavily damaged in a fire in 1898. * Merchant Vessels shows her as SAKIR SHEPARD.
media_notes: Image from GLMD by Bob McGreevy

name: METROPOLIS
place_of_loss: 3 mi S of Old Mission, MI, Gd Traverse Bay
detail: She was driven ashore in a storm and scuttled to keep her from pounding to pieces, but was later wrecked by wave action. Bound for Chicago, her homeport, and owned by the Goit Estate. Master: Capt. Corbett. Maybe recovered and later stranded and wrecked on Canada [Cana?] Island, near Sturgeon Bay, WI, in Sep, 1893. *date also shown in error as 1867 Major repairs in 1874-5 and 80-84

name: METROPOLIS
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: She caught fire and burned to a total loss at her dock. She had spent much of her recent career lying at her dock – she was only used for occasional excursions. Homeport: Toledo. She was once used as a mail steamer between Alpena and Bay City.

name: LIZZIE METZNER
detail: Caught in a 60mph NW gale, she was blown free of her dock at Oswego, dragged her anchors and went into the shallows on opposite side of the harbor. The Lifesaving Service took her crew off, but the vessel was a total loss. Owned and sailed by Capt Chauncey Daryaw of Kingston, Ont.

name: F.A. MEYER
other_names: built as J. EMORY OWEN, renamed in 1905.
detail: Her hull crushed by ice in an early freeze, she sank, a total loss. Owned by Adam Hartman, Tonawanda, NY. Sister of steamer CHAS. STUART PARNELL. At the beginning of her career she was known as a very tender boat and had many strandings her first few seasons. Stranded and burned near Sturgeon Bay, WI, in Dec., 1903 and declared total loss

name: MIAMI
detail: Out of Chicago and bound for Buffalo, she was stranded and lost. True to maritime traditions, her skipper reported afterwards that the rats had deserted her at Chicago before she sailed on her final voyage. During her attempted salvage, the Buffalo marine inspector and twelve others were almost lost when a storm rose and she broke up while they were on board. Out of Chicago, owned and sailed by Capt. Robert Clydesdale, Buffalo Rebuilt in 1871

name: MIAMI
place_of_loss: about mid-lake, 48 mi off Thunder Bay Island, Mich
detail: Bound for Spragge, Ont., she caught fire, burned and sank on a line between Thunder Bay, MI, and Burnt Isl. Ont. Her crew was rescued by the tug EDNA A. Worked for a time as a USLHS lighthouse tender.

name: C. MICHELSON
place_of_loss: a few miles S of Escanaba, MI
detail: She was overwhelmed by storm and driven ashore. Her crew reached the shore safely.This was the fifth loss for her skipper and owner Capt. Sam Martin of Racine: RAINBOW(1894), PERSIA, LAURINA, SILVER LAKE. Early MVUS’s show her as CHRISTINE MICHELSON. Major repairs in 1881, 83

name: MICHIGAN
detail: Destroyed by fire near midnight while lying at her dock. Her crew barely escaped with their lives. The vessel was allowed to float to Island No. 2, where she burned out.

name: MICHIGAN
build_info: 1847, B. S. Goodsell, Detroit as a sidewheel steamer
detail: Bound Saginaw River for Buffalo In tow with three other barges, all former steamers, she was caught in a storm. The tow broke up and her crew abandoned her – she became a total wreck. Converted to a barge from a steamer after heavy damage in a wreck near Green Bay in November of 1863. MAY have been recovered, 1869 is also given as the year of her loss. Owned by Ballantyne, Crawford & Co., East Saginaw.

name: MICHIGAN
detail: Driven on a reef and wrecked. homeport: Cleveland

name: MICHIGAN
detail: Went to aid of the icebound steamer ONEIDA Feb. 9 , and became caught in the ice herself, 20 miles off South Haven. Her hull was crushed and she foundered after being jammed in for more than a month. During that time she drifted quite a distance to the north with the ice. Owned by Grand Haven and Milwaukee Transportation Co . Master: Capt. Prindiville. An unsuccessful expedition was raised to salvage her in 1906. The vessel was located off Holland in June, 2005. She was built for the famous Goodrich Line, designed by Frank Kirby. Sister of WISCONSIN/NAOMI(qv) and CITY OF MILWAUKEE/MUSKEGON(qv).

name: MICHIGAN
build_info: 1852, A.C. Keating, Ohio City, OH [builder also given as Stevens & Presley or Moses & Quayle]
place_of_loss: 10 mi off Sandusky, near Kelley’s Isl.
detail: She caught fire in her hold and burned to a total loss. She had been headed Cleveland for Detroit to lay up for repairs. Out of Detroit in 1885 major repair, 1864, 1881. Rebuilt, 1883

name: MICHIGAN
build_info: 1873, Jenking, Walkerville or Sandwich, Ont. as a carferry
place_of_loss: just E of Pt. Sable, off Grand Sable Dunes
detail: Bound Marquette for Ashtabula, in tow of the steamer CITY OF NAPLES, she sprang a leak in heavy seas and sank before she could be towed to shore. Her crew was taken off 1 hour before she sank, by NAPLES. Owned by J. C. Gilchrist, Cleveland. Broke in two in a loading accident at Chicago in 1890. Built as an 18-car sidewheel river carferry. Sold American and reduced to a barge in 1884, her engine going into the steamer W. H. PRINGLE (qv).

name: MICHIGAN
detail: Tow of the steamer M.M. DRAKE(qv), she began to leak in vile weather. DRAKE came alongside to take crew off but collided with her in heavy seas and both sank. Both vessels were members of the James Corrigan fleet.

name: MICHIGAN
build_info: 1890, F. Wheeler, W. Bay City hull# 76 as a carferry
place_of_loss: NW of Hope Island, upper Georgian Bay
detail: While removing grain from the damaged Canadian steamer RIVERTON, she was driven on the shoal, broke in two and sank. Retired as a 16-car carferry in 1916, converted to barge, 1926

name: MICHIGAN CENTRAL
build_info: 1884, Detroit Dry Dock, Wyandotte hull# 65 as sw carferry
place_of_loss: False Detour Channel entrance, Cockburn Isl.
detail: She foundered from unknown causes. Her crew taken off by the Durocher tug GENERAL (qv) which was towing her. Owned by T.L. Durocher of Detour at time of loss. Master: in charge of Joseph Beatty, her chief engineer Reduced from a 21-car carferry to a barge in 1923. *76 ft over guards Remains were reportedly still visible in 1994

name: MICHIGAN CITY
detail: Foundered.

name: MICHIPICOTEN
other_names: built as large tug E.K. ROBERTS, renamed CITY OF WINDSOR(1890), last name in 1910.
build_info: 1883, Detroit Dry Dock, Wyandotte, MI hull# 64 as a fish tug
place_of_loss: Cooks Dock, W of Bayfield Sd., Manitoulin Isl
detail: Destroyed by a fire. Dimensions as a tug: 125(oa)x25x20 Rebuilt, 1890, 1912

name: MIDDLESEX
detail: She had just arrived at Hebard & Thurber’s Lumber Co. dock with her barge MELBOURNE and was unloading when a fire broke out in her cabin. A gale which was blowing at the time prevented effective fire fighting or even scuttling, and she burned to a total loss. She later drifted ashore nearby and broke in two. She was a loss of nearly $50,000. Owned by David Whitney, Jr., Detroit Master: Capt. R. Curson. Hull later recovered, converted to a schooner-barge of the same name at Algonac, Mich. and lasted until 1929. Sold Canadian 1918 and renamed WOODLANDS – C#138504. A schooner MIDDLESEX (probably US#16437), outbound from Milwaukee for England, was lost in the north Atlantic in 1876, crew saved.

name: MIDGE
detail: She came to anchor in a storm while trying to make Milwaukee. Unble to hold ground, she dragged ashore 8 miles south of the harbor mouth and became a total loss. Bound White Lake, Mich., for Milwaukee. Owned by J. Claussen. She had been damaged by a fire at Green Bay just a month earlier. Last known registry, Aug 1882

name: MIDNIGHT
place_of_loss: on AuSable [Fish] Point, near Oscoda, MI
detail: In tow of steamer S.S. WILHELM with schooner-barge MEARS, she was caught in a violent gale. Her crew abandoned to MEARS, which they deemed to be the better boat. MIDNIGHT was finally driven ashore by the unusual SE gale and torn apart after drifting, abandoned, for quite some time. MEARS drifted ashore also, but the two crews were rescued by the Lifesaving Service. Listed in ’84 mvus as an unrigged barge

name: MILAN
detail: Upbound Oswego for Cleveland, she sprung a leak and went down in 150 fathonms of water, about 15 miles offshore. Her crew took to the smallboat and made it to shore safely. Value of vessel and cargo $5000. Out of Oswego, owned by Capt. Richardson.

name: MILDRED
detail: Burned to a total loss. Document surrendered at Port Huron Jun 30, 1893. Sunk in a storm in Thunder Bay, MI, in Nov, 1872 *also given as 1891 **also seen as Buffalo major repair in 1880

name: “Millecoquins wreck” – identity of vessel unknown
detail: Wreck was discovered against the riverbank by a young boy in 1990 and excavated in 1991 and 1994. Though thought to be a shipwreck loss my many, I believe that the vessel was hauled up to that spot and abandoned. At one time she was thought to be the schooner FORESTER (qv)

name: ALBERT MILLER
place_of_loss: near Big Sable Point [also given erroneously as Au Sable Point, L. Huron]
detail: Bound Manistee for Chicago she caught fire and burned offshore. The Lifesaving Service helped take her crew off. The vessel later drifted ashore on the point, but was beyond salvage.

name: E.H. MILLER
detail: She exploded her boiler and sank, killing two crewmen, off Alpena. At first expected to be a total loss, she was raised almost exactly a year later. Master: Capt. Edwin H. Miller (d). The brand-new tug burned at her dock on the Saginaw River at Saginaw in April, 1874. Though thought to be a total wreck, she was soon rebuilt. Abandoned in fall of 1920 at Alpena, where she had spent most of her 46-year career.

name: JANE MILLER
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, pass. & pkg frt “coaster” and ferry
place_of_loss: probably near the mouth of Colpoy’s Bay, Georgian Bay
detail: Departed Meaford, Ont., bound for Wiarton into teeth of a gale and was never seen again. Those aboard consisted for her crew of about 10 and around 20 passengers. Master: Capt. Andrew Port(d). She had serviced the number of small ports on the inside coast of the Bruce Pen. A plaque was erected in her honor by the Ontario Government in 1968.

name: JOHN A. MILLER
detail: A bursting lantern caused a n inferno in the engine room of this tug. The skipper aimed the tug for port, gave her full steam and locked the steering, then went to fight the fire with the rest of the crew. The fire, however, burned through the steering apparatus, causing the tug to go in a large circle while the crew tried to decide whether to burn or drown. The smoke drew the tug WELCOME – mastered by the brother of the MILLER’s skipper – from Chicago. With courage and skill, the WELCOME drew up alongside the circling MILLER and took off the crew, even though she briefly took fire herself. The MILLER burned to the waterline and sank. Owned by Barry Bros., Chicago and sailed by Capt. Peter Barry. Thomas Barry skippered the WELCOME and James Barry was MILLER’s engineer. The hulk was later resurrected and converted to a fishing tug, which operated out of Grand Haven for some years.

name: LAURA MILLER
place_of_loss: 3 mi NW of Toledo gas buoy
detail: Foundered in a gale while bound for port Huron in tow of the tug HIGGIE. Her crew was rescued by fellow tow-barge QUIMBY. Owned out of Harbor Beach, Mich., by F. M. Potter. Document surrendered 10/8/99. Stranded at Glencoe, IL, in 1889. Also sank in a storm off Grand Haven, Nov 2, 1894, and driven ashore near Mackinaw City Jun 24, 1896.

name: MARY MILLER
build_info: 1867, R. Bertholf, Marine City (also given as Riley, Port Huron)
detail: No detail. Document surrendered 12/31/1878 at Port Huron, annotated “total loss – 11-1-71.” Out of Lakeport, Mich

name: DAVID W. MILLS
other_names: built as steamer SPARTA, last name in 1910. Also seen as DAVID M. MILLS
detail: She was on a routine trip when she was enveloped by fog and smoke from forest firse in western Ontario. She struck the infamous Oswego reef and jammed fast. Great effort was expended to release her, but by October she had been torn to pieces by wave action. Owned by Mills Transportation Co., Mentor, Oh. Master: Capt Matthew Langan. Her career on the lakes spanned 45 years, a long life for a wooden boat. It is probably the remains of her boiler that are still visible near Ford Shoal. Out of Buffalo. Rebuilt in 1907

name: FRANCIS MILLS
carrying: 550 bbl flour, 150 kegs butter, 22 bbl meat, 2770 bu oats, 500 staves
detail: Downbound from Cleveland, she tried to put into Erie for shelter. She was driven ashore, broke her back and sank, a total loss. Owned by C. Leeland, Cleveland . Master: Capt. Evans. Heavily damaged near Pt. Abino in the great gale of Nov 18, 1842.

name: HAMILTON J. MILLS
detail: Caught in a NE gale, her spars and sails went by the board and MILLS capsized soon after. Tow of the tug DAVID PROCTOR with the barges WILLIAM WHEELER (qv) and JENNIE MATTHEWS. The which saved the survivors of the barges’ crews. Registered out of Ogdensburg, owned by Geo. Hall & Co.

name: M.I. MILLS
other_names: none Also seen as MERRILL I. MILLS and M.J. MILLS
detail: She steamed out to pick up the bark UNADILLA and tow her in to the St. Clair River, but as she approached, the bark luffed to and rammed the tug. In a sinking condition, MILLS made for shore and was taken in tow by the nearby tug QUAYLE. Breasting a lake full of floating ice made it a slow process, and the MILLS sank 3 or 4 miles from shore in 90-100 feet of water. Owned by M. B. Kean, Detroit. Unsuccessful efforts were made to raise her soon after, and another abortive attempt was made to find and raise her in 1895. Lytle list erroneously reports her sunk at Detroit, 12/30/1880 and total loss. *Completed at the shipyard of Stewart McDonald, Detroit. M. I. Mills was the mayor of Detroit at the time.

name: MARY MILLS
place_of_loss: in harbor at Sturgeon Bay, WI
detail: Destroyed by fire, along with the nearby dock, warehouse and 300 t. hay. Owned by her master, Capt. John Bolton & Bilty Lumber Co. of Milwaukee Received the machinery from burned steamer G.P. HEATH in 1887.

name: N. MILLS
build_info: 1870, P. Lester, Vicksburg [Marysville], MI as a barge
detail: She collided with the steel steamer MILWAUKEE and sank. MILWAUKEE and other nearby boats rescued the survivors. Later blown up to clear the channel. Owner: Mills Estate, Marysville, Mich. Master: Capt. Dan Warwick. Beached and heavily damaged near Manistique, Michigan, 1892. Built as a barge, but equipped for the installation of an engine, which was done the next year. Major repairs in 1880.

name: PHILENA MILLS
detail: She drove ashore and was wrecked in a storm . The two survivors were rescued with the aid of local people. She was broken up and given up for lost by the 27 th . Owned by Walbridge and Hoag, Toledo. Master: Capt. Hoosefield.

name: MILTON
detail: Bound Ellison’s Bay, WI, for Milwaukee, she foundered in a heavy gale. Three brothers were among the lost. Owned and commanded by Capt. Julius Matheson (d), Milwaukee Heavily damaged and sunk in a collision with the schooner LOTUS near Port Washington, Wis., in November, 1877. Driven ashore and thought to be a total loss, Oct 9, 1883, near Jacksonport, WI.

name: JOE MILTON
type_at_loss: propeller steam fish tug and passenger boat, wood
place_of_loss: at Papoose Isl. N of Midland, Ont., Geo. Bay
detail: Destroyed by fire after a lamp was accidentally overturned in the forecastle when she struck a reef. Her crew abandoned in her boats and made it to Killarney, Ont., where they were picked up by the passenger steamer MANITOU. Owned by Jas. V. Crawford, Wiarton, Ont. [Wolverine Fish Co.] Used for excursions, fishing and cargo hauling in various seasons.

name: MILWAUKEE
other_names: none seen on original enrollment as “MILWAUKIE”
place_of_loss: 5 mi W of Gray’s Reef, Straits of Mackinac
detail: Bound Milwaukee for Buffalo, she collided in the dark with the schooner J.H. TIFFANY(qv) and sank, as did the schooner. Her crew took to the boats and were picked up by the propeller FREE STATE. Machinery and part of freight recovered in 1874. Owned by American Transportation Co.

name: MILWAUKEE
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
place_of_loss: Grand Haven Bar, off Grand Haven, MI
detail: She ran aground on a bar and later broke up. A number of vessels answered her distress signals and took her passengers and crew to safety, and more were rescued from shore . She had been running this route for her entire career and had even stranded on this same bar a couple of times previously. Sister of prop DETROIT (qv,#6198) Mpl collection says she was recovered, stranded off Harrisville in 1872.

name: MILWAUKEE
detail: Tow of tug THOMSON with barge SENATOR, she broke away and disappeared into a storm, supposedly with all hands. Bound Charlotte, NY, for Kingston. Owned by Montreal Transportation Company, Montreal. Master: Capt. Langevin(d). Rebuilt, 1880

name: MILWAUKEE
build_info: 1868, A.C. Keating, Ogdensburg, NY as a passenger steamer
place_of_loss: a few miles off Grand Haven, MI
detail: Bound Chicago for Muskegon, she collided with the steambarge C. HICKOX(qv) in fog. HICKOX rescued her crew, but when they arrived aboard, one member was found to be missing and presumed gone down with the ship. She had sunk quickly, in deep water. Both masters lost their licenses over the matter. Master: Capt. Armstrong. Owners: Lyman Mason, Muskegon. According to Merchant Vessels of the U.S., she had only a 75 hp engine Started out as a passenger vessel from Ogdensburg to the upper lakes for Northern Transportation Co., but was made obsolete by the enlarging of the Welland Canal.

name: MILWAUKEE
other_names: built as MANISTIQUE, MARQUETTE & NORTHERN No. 1, renamed in 1909
place_of_loss: on a line between Milwaukee and Grand Haven
detail: Sailed off into a storm, bound Milwaukee for Grand Haven, and was lost. Theory is that rail cars came loose in gale and crashed through her seagate, allowing water to come in over the stern and sink her. Her skipper apparently turned back for Milwaukee, but never made it.Some of her lifeboats were launched by her crew, and two of their bodies were picked up two days later by the steamer STEEL CHEMIST. Another lifeboat containing bodies washed up near Holland, MI.. It was routine for her to challenge the storm, she was built for it. Master: Capt. John McKay(d). Owner: Grand Trunk Railway. Wreck was located in 1972.

name: MILWAUKEE BELLE
place_of_loss: off Brevort, MI, Straits of Mackinac
detail: She was dismasted by a SW gale north of the Beaver Isls., and blown almost 40 miles and sank. Her rigging and gear were salvaged, but the vessel was abandoned Nov 30. Out of Chicago, owned by Powers. She spent the winter ashore after stranding in a terrific gale near Cheboygan, MI, in Nov of 1860. Recovered in May, 1861. Rebuilt, 1871.

name: MILWAUKIE
build_info: 1836, B.F. Delano, Grand Isl., Niagara R.
place_of_loss: two miles north of the mouth of the Kalamazoo R.
detail: She stranded off the mouth of the river, where she had been anchored after leaving the harbor under tow to wait out a freezing gale. Eventually she drifted onto a bar and broke in two. Seven froze to death before they could be rescued. Two others were lost trying to swim to shore. The storm she was lost in is reported to have lasted on and off for 2 months! Master: Capt. Whittemore. Owned by Hunter, Palmer & Co., Buffalo. *Nov 2 also given. Date shown is from a letter by someone who had been on the scene, supported by a wrecklist published in February of the following year. She was the first full-rig ship launched on the lakes, an honor often given to the ship [later steamer] JULIA PALMER, launched exactly one week later. Much confusion exists between her and the sidewheeler MILWAUKIE, also built at Grand Island the following year by Hotaling.

name: ANNA C. MINCH
place_of_loss: 1.7 mi, 216 deg from Pentwater, MI pierhead
detail: She broke in two and foundered with all hands in the “Armistice Day Storm.” She probably did NOT collide with steamer WILLIAM B. DAVOCK(qv) as often speculated. Sold Canadian in 1926, Owned by Western Navigation Co., Port Colborn, Ont.

name: CHARLES P. MINCH
place_of_loss: Tecumseh Cove, Cove Island, Georgian Bay
detail: Bound for Chicago, she was driven ashore and wrecked in a storm. She later slid into deeper water. Owner given as J. C. Gilchrist, Vermilion, Oh or C. W. Elphicke. Master: Capt. William Kaufmann. The wreck is a popular dive site in Fathom Five Provincial Park. Major repairs in 1878 & 80

name: PHILIP MINCH
place_of_loss: 10 miles SW of Southeast Shoal, near Marblehead
detail: Bound Conneaut, Ohio, for Sandusky, she was destroyed by fire offshore and sank. Lifesavers from Marblehead made it to the ship, but the crew had already rowed to Sandusky in her boat. The wreck was dynamited as a hazard to navigation in 1906. Master: Capt. Benson.

name: SOPHIA MINCH
detail: Arrived off Cleveland with her sails blown out, dropped anchor and ran up distress signals. Two tugs responded, and got a line to her, but the schooner sank on the way into harbor. Her crew took to the rigging as she went down and were saved by the Lifesaving Service in a surfboat. Master: Capt. Bates. Declared a total loss, but later repaired and served until 1927. Ashore and declared a total loss near Ashtabula in 1899, but sold off the beach and recovered. Some sources say she was built on the hull of an 1840 vessel.
media_notes: Image of her on the beach in 1907, from GLMD

name: JOHN MINER
place_of_loss: 2 mi W of Pte Aux Barques light
detail: Bound Harbor Springs for Detroit, she was driven into boulder-ridden shallows and broke up quickly in a north’r. Crew was rescued by the Lifesaving Service. Master and owner: Capt. Norman McDonald. The wreck was sold to Capt. Barry off the beach – cheap. She went ashore in Hammond Bay in the great gale of 1869 and was supposed to be a total loss. Her mate was killed by some object which fell from aloft when she struck.. Major repairs in 1883

name: JULIA MINER
other_names: often seen as JULIA MINOR, shown in ’69 mv as JULIUS MINER.
place_of_loss: NE of Big Charity Isl., Saginaw Bay
detail: She struck a reef and wrecked.Her crew escaped and was cared for by the local lighthouse keeper. The vessel was declared a total loss, but was recovered and repaired in August, though there is no record of her after this. Owned by Frutchey, Detroit. She had reportedly been uninsurable for several years.

name: MINERAL
detail: Bound Kingston for Oswego, she went ashore in a storm and became a total loss. Out of Kingston, owned by James Doyle.

name: MINERAL STATE
place_of_loss: just east of Port Stanley, Ont.
detail: Inbound for Port Stanley, she was driven on a reef in a storm and broke up. Most of crew was rescued by the tug GORDON BROWN and one floated ashore on a hatchcover. Out of Alpena, owned by W. H. Sanborn and F. H. Riebenack. Master: Capt. William Zien.
media_notes: Image as a wreck from GLMD

name: MINGOE
place_of_loss: Huron Islands, E of Keweenaw Pen.
detail: Foundered in a storm. Some speculation was that she struck an ice floe. Owned by Mingoe Transportation Co.

name: MINNEAPOLIS
build_info: 1873, Morley & Hill, Marine City as a passenger vessel
place_of_loss: almost under the current site of the Mackinac Bridge.
detail: She was damaged by ice and then ran across a rocky bottom near McGulpin Pt., causing her to leak profusely. She sank quickly after her fires were put out by the rising water. Her crew was rescued by the schooner SAN DIEGO, part of her tow. One crew-member claimed that she was holed by ice where her iron plating had been removed to repair the wooden hull and was not replaced. Owned by A. A. Parker, Detroit. Master, H. W. Bogart. Converted to a bulk freighter in 1880, major repairs in 1881
media_notes: Image from GLMD Image as a package freighter, same source

name: MINNEDOSA
detail: She was torn away from her tow steamer WESTMOUNT and foundered in a huge gale that sunk many ships. In consort with schooner MELROSE. 8 crewmen and captain’s wife died. The wreck was located in 1993. Out of Kingston, Ont. Master: Capt. John Phillips(d). Wreck located in 2016 Largest lake sail vessel ever built in Canada.

name: MINNEHAHA
place_of_loss: just east of Port Stanley, Ont.
detail: She encountered a storm, sank in the shallows and became a total loss. Her outfit was salvaged near the end of October. She had been bound Toledo for Oswego, and was owned and sailed by Capt. Dibble of Detroit.

name: MINNEHAHA
place_of_loss: At Starke, MI, 10 mi N of Onekama
detail: Tow of steamer HENRY J. JOHNSON, she was caught in 90mph northerly gale, broke loose and put on beach to save her crew. She later broke up there. Bound Chicago for Port Edward.
