Out of Cleveland and owned by H. J. Johnson and others. Her master: Capt. William Packer, was the only survivor. Wrecked near Detour in 1891 and stayed on the rocks all winter after being abandoned. Declared a constructive total loss in July of the following year and auctioned off to the highest bidder.

name: MINNEHAHA
place_of_loss: 1/4 mi SW of One Tree Isl., Georgian Bay
detail: Foundered and became a total loss in the channel between Penetang & Parry Sound. *also given as 1909 Registered out of Collingwood. 1869 engine by G. Notter of Buffalo

name: MINNEHAHA
other_names: also seen as MINNE HA HA
build_info: 1877, Todd, Sheboygan, WI (Cheboygan, MI appears in some volumes of MV)
detail: Reportedly missed the piers and drove ashore, where she broke up. Out of Milwaukee. Wrecked near Northport, Mich, Lake Michigan, and document surrendered in Oct., 1883; shown in 1884 Merchant Vessels as “lost or otherwise out of service.” Still registered in 1902

name: MINNEHAHA
build_info: 1872, Earnst, Manitowoc [builder also given as M. Ornes]
detail: Foundered. Out of Chicago

name: MINNESETUNK
place_of_loss: Mouth of Thames R. or near Malden
detail: Collided with the steamer ERIE and sunk. Declared a total loss, but later recovered and rebuilt. Csv says she sunk near Malden in 1839 as GODERICH.

name: MINNESOTA
detail: She”broke her arches,” sprang a leak and was put on Summer Island to keep her from sinking. The prevailing gale soon tore her to pieces, a total loss. Downbound from Chicago.

name: MINNESOTA
detail: Driven ashore in a gale and became a total loss. Out of Chicago. Major repairs in 1862, 65

name: MINNESOTA
detail: While upbound, a fire was discovered in the engine room when the ship was off Walpole Isl. As the blaze expanded, the vessel was abandoned and drifted down to a point abreast the Porter White Farm, just below Grande Pointe, and sank. She was a serious menace to navigation for quite some time until flattened by the Reid Wrecking Co. of Sarnia. Member of Corrigan Fleet. Master: Capt. Harvey Peters.

name: MINNIE
place_of_loss: 1 mi E of Salt Point, near Presqu’ile, Ont.
detail: Foundered. No detail. Another Canadian schooner of this name was reported wrecked and total loss near Frederick Point, Lake Ont. Nov 10, 1880. Owner: Kingston & Montreal Forwarding Co. Hailed from Port Colborne. Frames were made of Tamarack

name: MINNIE
build_info: 1865, Tripp & Kirby, Saginaw, Mich as a sidewheeler
detail: She had been laying at the Fowles shipyard when she caught fire – probably from a bursting lamp – and burned to a total loss. A local fire company arrived on the scene ill-equipped, and then had to watch while she burned to the waterline. Owned by Hagen & English, Green Bay. Converted to a propeller at Green Bay in 1877.

name: MINOR
detail: Went to pieces in storm and sank, a total loss.

name: J.S. MINOR
other_names: sometimes seen as J.N. MINOR and JOHN S. MINER
detail: She struck the rocky bottom in a plunge during a quick-rising storm. Drifted ashore and broke up. Bound for Caseville to pick up lumber. Homeport: Detroit Rebuilt and enlarged in 1864. Ashore and declared total loss near Kincardine, Ont, Oct 15, 1871.

name: MINT
place_of_loss: S of harbor entrance at Calumet, IL
detail: Stranded and broke up. (Possibly recovered) Maybe MINK

name: MIRANDA
detail: Bound Bay City for Detroit, she was driven ashore in 8 feet of water and pounded to pieces. Homeport: Detroit. Owned by Capt. H.L. Brown. Major repairs in 1861, 63, 68. Rebuilt, 1866, at Detroit. A small schooner MIRANDA had an odd wreck a week after this one [Sep 24]. She was picked up awash off Michigan City. Her cabin door was bolted and lifeboat in place. When the door was forced, her elderly skipper and a 17 year old crewman were found drowned inside. It was thought they were drunk and were unable to free themselves when she sank. A third crewman was missing. The vessel was probably put back in service. Rebuilt in 1866.

name: MISCHIEF
detail: Torn loose from her moorings and ripped to pieces on a rocky shore. Main evidence of her existence and destruction is from contemporary photos of the time. Documents not surrendered until 1928.

name: C.F. MISCHLER
detail: Destroyed by fire. Out of Erie

name: MISS EAST TAWAS
detail: She was chained for the night to the Tawas Municipal Dock (owned by the State of Michigan) when a fire broke out near her berth. The blaze spread quickly along to the creosoted dock to a gas pump and the dock and boat soon became an inferno. She burned to a total loss. Owned by Laurin D Murphy, Port Huron and operated by Capt. Frost, who was also the harbor master. A nearby yacht was also destroyed by the fire and another damaged before the local volunteer fire department and the Coast Guard extinguished it. Carried groups of 20 or more for sport fishing in Saginaw Bay.

name: MISS STEPHANIE II
type_at_loss: commercial fishing boat, welded steel, Diesel
detail: She foundered 2.5 mi NE of Kettle Point when water entered the lazaret while the traps were being brought aboard and the bilge pumps failed to work. She sank stern first. A man and his two-year old son, guests on the boat, were trapped in the cabin as she went down. Vessel recovered Aug 17 from 45 feet of water. Rebuilt in 1990 at Wheatly, Ont., and lengthened 10 feet after capsizing and sinking Jun 16, 1988. Link to TSBC report on the loss

name: MISSOULA
detail: Rode up on a big wave and broke her shaft when she came back down. Helpless, she was overwhelmed after drifting for a full day. Her crew abandoned her for the open lake, and drifted 30 mi in the boat before landfall. Master: Capt. William A. Williams. Owner: Capt. Thos. Wilso, et. al, Cleveland.

name: MISSOURI
detail: Stranded and lost. *also given as Erie, PA Some sources shows her still in service as late as 1868.

name: MISSOURI
detail: Outbound from Erie, PA, for Buffalo she was struck by a gale and dismasted just off Barcelona, finally drifting in to a bar just off the harbor mouth. There she pounded heavily and finally broke in two. Much of her gear, rigging and cargo was eventually saved. Owner: Capt. Bone.

name: MISSOURI
build_info: 1857, VanSlyke & Notter, Buffalo as a sidewheeler
detail: Destroyed by fire on the Canadian side just above Sandwich (Windsor), Ont. Her engine was later salvaged and the remains dynamited. Registered out of Buffalo Ashore and wrecked near Port Burwell, Ont. 10/26/1870 Converted to a prop before 1864 Major repairs in 1876,80 & 84.

name: MISTRESS
place_of_loss: between Pentwater, Mich and Milwaukee, Wis
detail: The tug had just been sold by the Pentwater owners and was being taken to Milwaukee by her new owners when she disappeared. Despite a search by the USCG cutters HOLLYHOCK and ESCANABA, a Coast Guard plane and a host of other vessels, nothing was ever found of her. Men who knew the boat speculated that she was sturdy enough to survive the storm that was blowing and probably had a fire or explosion. Master: Charles Jensen (d).

name: ALEXANDER MITCHELL
other_names: also seen as A. MITCHELL, ALEX. MITCHELL
detail: She was driven ashore in heavy weather and within two weeks had pounded to pieces and was abandoned. Owned by Ludington, Chicago. Major repairs in 1864-5

name: BELL MITCHELL
other_names: none also seen as BELLE MITCHELL
detail: She went missing in a SW gale, and was probably totally overwhelmed by weather. Lots of wreckage washed ashore but none was ever positively identified. Bound Toledo for Buffalo. Given up for lost the 22nd. Out of Clayton, NY, owned by Barker Bros and her master, Capt. Rushe(d).

name: CLAUD MITCHELL
detail: No detail – sunk and abandoned, according to Canadian records. Sold Canadian in 1875.

name: JOHN MITCHELL
build_info: 1907, Great Lakes Engineering, St. Clair, MI hull #25
detail: Rammed broadside by coal-laden steel steamer WILLIAM HENRY MACK. MACK tried to keep her bow in the hole, but MITCHELL still sank in 7 minutes. Quick work saved most of her crew and all 7 passengers. MACK got most of the blame for the accident. Owned by C.W. Elphicke out of Chicago. Master: Capt. John M. Massey. The wreck discovered upside-down on the bottom in 1972.

name: MINNIE MITCHELL
detail: She was blown ashore in a gale, and went to pieces as soon as she struck the beach. Her crew were able to wade ashore. She had been bound Pelee Island for Leamington. Out of Goderich, St. Catharines or Sarnia, Ont.

name: C.G. MIXER
detail: She was driven ashore in huge storm. Her cargo battered her to pieces quickly, but the crew was rescued by S. Chicago Lifesaving Service. Master: Capt.Henry Abehas; out of Chicago. Major repair in 1881

name: MIZTEC
build_info: 1890, J. Ihnken & P. Lester, Marine City as 3-mast schooner
detail: Tow of steamer ZILLAH(qv), she broke her towline and foundered. She had stranded with heavy damage in nearly the same spot in 1919. After rebuilding, she stranded again near the same location in 1920. It got her at last in ‘21. Wreckage found in 1983.

name: MOCKING BIRD
detail: Bound Buffalo for Chicago, she broke some mainmast gear and, sprung a leak. She stuggled into the shelter of Long Point and there water slowly gained on her pumps until she sank .near shore The Long Point lightkeeper was able to rescue her crew, who were later picked up by the revenue cutter PERRY. She was reported to have gone to pieces by the 27 th . Her insurance had expired just two days prior to the accident. Valued at $18,500. Out of Chicago. Shown in some sources as a Canadian vessel, but it would have been illegal for a Canadian ship to carry a cargo between two American ports.

name: MOCKING BIRD
detail: Bound Cross Village, Michigan, for Chicago, she was driven ashore by a gale and became a total wreck. Owned by M. E. Swartz, Chicago or by Saveland et. al, Milwaukee. One paper reports that she was “one of the ‘flat iron’ kind of scows.” Rebuilt 1874, Manitowoc Also ashore and wrecked near Charlevoix, MI, in 1882.

name: MOCKING BIRD
detail: Destroyed by a fire which started in her coal bunkers. Her crew abandoned her by jumping overboard in their night clothes. Removed from registry, but recovered, rebuilt and reregistered in 1892. Abandoned in 1909. Built as a boom tug.

name: FRANK MOFFAT
detail: Bound Saginaw for Buffalo, her boiler exploded and she sank while rounding to with five barges in tow. She was a total loss. It was thought the boiler ran dry. Out of Port Huron, owned by Moffatt Tug Line. Master: Capt. Thomas Currie. Also wrecked in a collision in the same area in 1873 and again in 1883.

name: GEORGE M. MOFFAT
other_names: none also seen as just GEORGE MOFFAT
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freighter*
place_of_loss: on Raby Head, W. of Bowmanville, Ont.
detail: Having lost her stack and been disabled by astorm, she was driven ashore and wrecked. Passengers and crew were rescued the day after she went ashore. Her whistle pipe had broken when her stack blew off, causing her to lose steam. Went ashore on Long Point [Lake Ont.] in 1861 & declared total loss. After the steamer AKRON busted out the lock gates below her, she rapidly descended 15 feet, struck bottom and sprung her keel in the Welland Canal in Aug of ’64. She was declared total loss. *she was a pollywog type vessel, with large, narrow paddlewheels situated well aft.

name: KATE MOFFAT
other_names: none also seen as KATE MOFFATT
carrying: none, schooners METROPOLIS and HAVANA in tow
detail: She became a total loss to fire after beaching on Blue Point in a dense fog. Her captain and some crew put a boat over the side to assess the stranding situation and go ashore for help, and were gone when the tug caught fire around her stack. A smallboat from the METROPOLIS, also stranded, was sent to the rescue of those left aboard. The tug’s engine and boiler were salvaged in Jul, 1886, and went into the tug GLADIATOR. The wreck was struck by the lighthouse tender DAHLIA in May of 1887 and was probably removed shortly after that. Owned by the Detroit Tug and Transportation Co., Detroit. Master: Capt. B. Mosler. She was one of the prominent and profitable large tugs towing at Detroit in the late 60’s. *machinery from Cuyahoga works installed at Cleveland. **Original dimensions 114x21x10, 134g. major repairs in 1874, 1880, 1881

name: MOHAWK
detail: She was lost in a British naval engagement with French Great Lakes fleet – captured by Montcalm and later burned. Other British vessels reportedly lost along with her: OSWEGO, ONTARIO, GEORGE, VIGILANT, LONDON and HALIFAX.

name: MOHAWK
detail: Bound Chicago for Oswego on a late season run, she struck the east pier while trying to make the harbor in a full WNW gale. Her masts went by the board and she immediately began to go to pieces. A passenger and four crewmen jumped to the pier and lived. Skipper and a hand drowned. Little of her was left by the following March. She was a total loss of $11,000. Owned by J.B. Morris of Oswego. Master: Capt. John Carmichael(d). Rebuilt, 1848

name: MOHAWK
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, package and bulk freight
detail: As she came down the flats channel during the night she encountered another steamer anchored directly in the channel. In attempting to avoid her, she ran aground on a bar. Her crew worked all night to get her off, but near morning, perhaps from the extra effort of trying to pull herself off, one of her two boilers exploded, killing five and setting the vessel afire. The fire was quickly extinguished. Her stern was terrifically damaged, but she was later recovered. Owned by Western Transportation Co. Master: Capt. H. D. Pheatt. Heavily damaged in a fire at Buffalo in November, 1875. Rebuilt to a barge in 1879, to the steamer J.E. POTTS(qv) in 1883 and to a schooner in 1891 after a fire in 1890. Burned in 1895.

name: MOHAWK
other_names: built as ELLEN MURRAY, renamed before 1850
detail: She collided with a tug and sank in 9 fathoms [54 feet] of water. Registration closed 2/14/66.

name: MOHAWK
build_info: 1843, Kingston Dock Yard, Kingston, Ont., as a twin engine sidewheel Royal Canadian gunboat
detail: Foundered with all hands. She had been part of a six-barge tow of the tug ZOUAVE, which was later broken up into two smaller tows. She was behind the tug CLEMATIS when she broke loose and drifted away into the storm. Master: Capt. Nathaniel Kendall (d). Launched in February, 1843, making her the first iron vessel on the Great Lakes – she may have been fabricated in England, assembled here. Rebuilt and lengthened 30 feet in 1846, also narrowed to fit the Welland Canal by the replacement of her paddle wheels. Sold for a commercial vessel in 1852 and converted to a passenger/freight steamer to operate between Buffalo and Port Stanley, Ont. One newspaper says she was an iron-hulled former Detroit boat that once operated between Saginaw and Tonawanda. She plied Lake Michigan regularly in the 1860’s and was converted from a sidewheeler to a propeller in 1868. Sold U.S. in 1868. Converted to barge by 1870.

name: MOHEGAN
detail: Bound from the Loud & Gay docks, Au Sable, Mich, for Cleveland, she was driven ashore by a storm. Stripped a few days later, she was reported thoroughly broken up on Dec 1. Homeport: Detroit, owned by Reidy & Cuson. Major repairs in 1860

name: ANNIE MOILES
detail: She burned to a total loss after 62 years of service. Owned by Nicholson Universal Steamship Co., Detroit. Suffered a major collision and declared total loss off Detroit, May, 1922. She capsized with the loss of 7 lives in “the whirlpool” near Sarnia, Ont. [St Clair R], in 1904. Salvaged by Tom Reid, her owner. *Builder also given as Tripp. Sold Canadian, 1900, U.S. again, 1911, Canadian again 1911, U.S. once again 1923.

name: MOIRA
detail: Bound Oswego for Belleville, Ont., she sprung a leak and foundered in deep water. Her crew and passengers were picked up by a passing unnamed sail vessel. She was a loss of $18,000.

name: MOJAVE
place_of_loss: on line between Sheboygan, WI, and Big Sable Pt., MI, about midlake
detail: While in company of the Canadian bark MONARCH, a gale got the best of her and she began to wallow. She was trying to wear ship and run for the Michigan shore when she disappeared from view. Master: Capt. D. N. Malott(d). Her crew was comprised mainly of veterans of the schooner AUGUSTA [Capt. Malott of AUGUSTA brought MOJAVE out], the vessel which collided with and sank LADY ELGIN in 1860. The press and the public, which unjustly blamed the crew for the ELGIN disaster [though the crew had been cleared], called it justice. One source says she was raised from 250 feet of water in 1875. She went ashore on Skillagallee in June of the same year and was thought to be a total loss.

name: MOLLIE
build_info: 1867, Cook, Fairport, OH [also given as Hansen R., MI, rebuilt at Fairport, ‘74]
detail: after she went to pieces offshore and foundered, her crew was rescued by the big break-bulk carrier F.&P.M. #4, in the middle of the night. Out of Racine and owned by Williams. Master: Capt. Anderson. Sunk at Sarnia, 1881. Rebuilt, 1874, 1876, 1881.

name: GILBERT MOLLISON
detail: Left Chicago Oct. 24, bound for Oswego in company of several other vessels. She was sighted struggling through a storm off South Manitou on the 27th, but never seen again. Her yawl washed ashore Nov 3. Owned out of Oswego by Mitchell Bros. Master: Capt. Joel A. Turner(d).

name: MONA
detail: Bound Thompson’s Harbor, MI, for Detroit, she sprang a leak and waterlogged 10 miles off Pte Aux Barques, stranding 2 mi ESE of the USLS station. Her crew was unable to signal the station, but made it to shore on a makeshift raft. The vessel was a near-total loss, only her outfit being saved.

name: MONARCH
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: Lost her way in an easterly snow storm and went ashore while approaching the harbor. She was exposed to heavy wave action and soon broke up. Two large steam pumps that were placed aboard to empty her were later washed overboard. Both they and the vessel’s engines were later saved. 1st year of service on the Toronto-Montreal route. Owned by A. & D. Shaw & Co. Kingston, plus her master, Capt. A. Sinclair. The wreck was flattened with dynamite after the boiler was removed in 1895. *builder also given as D. &. G. McCarthy, Sorel, Que.
media_notes: Image as a wreck from GLMD

name: MONARCH
detail: Became unmanageable and went on the rocks near Detour, where she sank. $15,000 loss. Out of Picton, Ont. [owners: Parker & Bishop] or Erie, Pa. and , reported in Lloyds as Canadian vessel having American owners – shows in both registries.

name: MONARCH
detail: Lost in blizzard, she drove her prow right up into the woods on Blake Point. After her people escaped, she began to break up, then slipped backwards into deep water, where she still lies. Owned by Beatty Line [Northern Navigation Co.]. Master: Capt. Ed. Robertson, who brought her out.

name: MONARCH
place_of_loss: in harbor at Grand Marais, MI
detail: She was a total loss to fire. Vessel shown was registered out of Tampa, Fla., but is the only one in mv the right size.

name: MONARCH
other_names: built as W.H. SIMPSON, renamed in 1933
place_of_loss: Port Huron, just S of the Blue Water Bridge
detail: She capsized and sank in a towing accident. Owned by the Nicholson Transportation Co and homeported at Detroit.

name: MONARCH OF THE GLEN*
type_at_loss: schooner, wood [also reported as a sloop]**
place_of_loss: off Marblehead, N of Sandusky, OH
detail: Capsized and foundered while bound Kelley’s Island for Sandusky. Master: Capt. Henry Magle(d). Rebuilt at Sandusky on 1860. *Most newspaper accounts call her just MONARCH, but local historians say MONARCH oF THE GLEN. If she was M of the G, she was recovered and was still operating years later. **may have been a yacht at time of loss

name: MONGUAGON
detail: Opened her seams while unloading coal at a Detroit dock and sank from age. Owner: Timothy Hurley, Detroit. Master: Capt. Moore. Sunk at Milwaukee by collision with prop CLARION, May 13 1888. She was a major repair job afterwards.

name: MONITOR
place_of_loss: Seul Choix Pte., Garden Peninsula, MI
detail: Bound Escanaba for Cleveland, she went on rocks and was wrecked. Wrecking tugs LEVIATHAN and later KATE WILLIAMS tried into December to release her. Owned out of Buffalo by Riordan, et. al. Another MONITOR reportedly went ashore at S Manitou Isl., some miles to the south, only 2 weeks later, causing a good amount of confusion. The second MONITOR was recovered the following June [see 1898 wreck, below]. In the late 1860’s there were more than a dozen vessels of the name operating on the lakes. Had a mural of the MONITOR/MERRIMAC battle painted on her transom, at least when built. Rebuilt, 1881

name: MONITOR
build_info: 1870, Campbell & Owen, Detroit as a schooner
loss_of_life_original: none of 12 [9 crew plus the captain’s wife & two children]
detail: She foundered in a powerful NW gale when bound Pierport, Michigan for Milwaukee. Her large crew abandoned in her six-foot[!] lifeboat and was picked up by a passing schooner. Out of Chicago. Master: Capt. A. J. Rice. Rebuilt from a schooner to a wrecker [dimensions 114x23x8] in 1875, rebuilt by 1884.

name: MONITOR
build_info: 1862, J. G. Shaler, Conneaut, Oh, as a bark
place_of_loss: Waiskai [“Whiskey”] Bay, near Pt. Iroquois, Whitefish Bay
detail: She foundered suddenly with the loss of five lives. On the 26 th she lay with her two spars above water, 1 mi W of Round Island on the N side of the channel. Owned by Soo River Lighter and Wrecking Co. Rebuilt to a 3-mast schooner in 1879, to a lighter in 1894. Driven ashore by a NE gale on S end of N. Mantiou Island, L. Michigan, Nov 15, 1883. Her crew and a small amount of gear was saved by local fisherman and the revenue cutter ANDREW JOHNSON. The N. Manitou Lifesaving station was censured and its keeper dismissed for neglect of duty in the failure to discover the schooner in distress within their venue when it was clearly visible from the station.

name: MONKSHAVEN
build_info: 1882, J. Readhead & Co., S. Shields, Eng.
detail: She was driven ashore by a terrific gale and wrecked. Her thinly-clad crew survived on the ice covered island for 96 hours until found. The vessel was pulled off by wreckers in the spring of 1906, but was immediately snatched back to the rocks by a storm and totally wrecked. Master: Capt. McIntyre. Wreck still lies at the site, though explosives were used on it during a scrapping effort during WWII.
media_notes: Image as a wreck from GLMD

name: MONOHANSETT
other_names: built as propeller IRA H. OWEN, renamed, 1882
place_of_loss: .5 mi W of Thunder Bay Isl. light
detail: She caught fire near the north side of Thunder Bay, and sank off the island. The fire was clearly visible from Alpena, 8 miles away. Inbound for Alpena at time of loss.

name: MONROVIA
other_names: Built as EMPIRE FALSTAFF, sold French 1945, renamed COMMANDANT MANTELET(1945) renamed COMMANDANT LeBIBOUL(1951), sold Liberian, 1954 and given last name
type_at_loss: propeller, steel, package freight “Empire Class” liberty ship
detail: Bound Antwerp for Chicago and Duluth, she collided with the lake freighter ROYALTON in heavy fog and sank. Part of her cargo was later salvaged. She was Liberian-flagged with a Greek crew, and had no pilot aboard. The investigation that followed blamed the wreck mainly on the MONROVIA’s captain being unfamiliar with the lakes “rules of the road.” Owned by Eastern Shipping Corp. Monrovia, Liberia. Wreck lies 13.6 mi, 106 deg from Thunder Bay I. light with 43 feet of water over her. Official U.S. Coast Guard Report (.pdf file, courtesy Kimm Satbelfeldt)

name: MONSON
detail: no detail

name: MONSOON
place_of_loss: along route between Chicago and Grand Haven
detail: She went missing in a gale while bound Muskegon for Chicago, having left Muskegon on the 2nd. At the time that her loss was first suspected, she was reported in one newspaper as “a worthless, rotten vessel.” On the 28 th a Detroit paper said “She was so rotten and water-soaked that there is hardly any reason to hope that a plank of her will ever be seen again. ” Owner: Burnestein, Chicago. Ashore at Milwaukee in April, 1864. Ashore and reported a total loss at Grand Haven, Mich in Nov, 1873. Rebuilt in 1857, major repairs in 1862 *builder also given as W. S. Lyon, J. P. Gay. Oades was probably the master carpenter.

name: MONT BLANC
detail: Bound Toledo for Port Huron in tow of the tug GLADIATOR, she was torn loose by a storm and sank in 20 feet of water. Her crew remained in the rigging until rescued by Bar Pt. lightkeepers in a rowboat the next day [9 hours later]. Owned by J. Little of Port Huron. Master: Capt. Littleton. Rebuilt 1878, 1883.

name: MONTANA
detail: She sprang a leak while in tow of the prop FAIRMOUNT and the extra weight broke her towline. Taken in tow by the steamer VENEZUELA, she foundered before she could be beached. Collided at midnight May 26, 1902 with an unknown steamer at Limekiln Crossing, Detroit R. Beached at Elliot’s Point, Amherstburg, with heavy damage. She was driven ashore Nov 2, 1890 north of Middle Island, Mich., Lake Huron, and wrecked in a gale after drifting helplessly for several hours. Her crew was rescued by Thunder Bay Lifesaving Service Bound Escanaba for Sandusky with iron ore. Out of Toledo but owned by F. A. Hubbard, Sandusky at that time. Master: Capt. James S. Ellis. Declared a total loss, but recovered in 1891. Major repairs, 1880, 1881 Sold Canadian at Port Huron in June, 1910.

name: MONTANA
build_info: 1872, Arch Muir, Port Huron, MI [A. Smith master carpenter], as a package freighter
detail: She caught fire 5 miles out from the lighthouse and burned to waterline after being abandoned by her crew. The crew was picked up from the water by the prop ALPENA. Owned by Geo. Kotcher, Detroit. Collided with the bank and sunk near the mouth of the Portage Ship Canal in September, 1895. Changed from package to bulk freighter, 1902

name: MONTAUK
place_of_loss: north of Pickard’s Dock, N Manitou
detail: She went ashore in blizzard/gale and became a total loss. Her crew was rescued by the Lifesaving Service after a harrowing night. By December 10 she had gone to pieces. Bound Buffalo for Chicago. Owned by H. Esselstyne & Co of Detroit, along with other “Monts” built for or purchased by them: Montana, Mont Blanc, Montcalm, Monterey, Montgomery, Monticello, Montezuma & Montpelier

name: MONTCALM
place_of_loss: half mile east of Selkirk, Ont.*
detail: She was driven aground at night and wrecked in a violent SW gale. Her crew was taken off the next day. Master: Capt. J. McCullough, vessel owned by Rochester Transportaion Co., Toledo. *also given as Long Point and “Lapp Point” Went ashore on Long Point on Nov 16, 1871 and declared a total loss. See MONTAUK for note on name.

name: MONTEAGLE
other_names: none also seen as MONT EAGLE
detail: Went ashore and wrecked. Owned by W. L. Richards, Erie, Pa. Major repairs in 1864 after going ashore near Long Point Cut, Oct 5, 1863. *Builder also given as Edwards.

name: MONTEAGLE
detail: This steamer struck a submerged crib and caught fire while traversing the lake. The fire quickly went out of control and crew were forced to abandon her to sink. Owned by Robert Downey, Oswego, NY. Also burned on Pilot Isl, Lake Mich, in 1909.

name: WILLIAM MONTEATH
other_names: none also seen as WILLIAM MONTEITH and just MONTEITH
detail: Bound Erie, PA for Chicago, she was driven ashore and wrecked. Her crew were able to save some of her cargo before she was abandoned to break up. Owner: E. K. Bruce, Buffalo. Master: Capt. M. Dimmick.

name: MONTEZUMA
build_info: 1853, L. Goler, Cape Vincent, NY, as a brig
place_of_loss: 30 mi SSE of Thunder Bay Isl., MI
detail: She collided at night and in heavy weather with upbound schooner HATTIE HOWARD. She sank quickly in deep water, her crew transferring to HOWARD. Bound Chicago for Buffalo. Owned by H.S. Halstead of Chicago. Rebuilt and enlarged, winter of 1860-61.

name: MONTEZUMA
detail: She was driven ashore near Port Stanley and broke up, a total loss. The skipper/owner’s wife, who was carrying a large sum of money, was lost with the wreck and reportedly never found. Owned and sailed by Capt Francis McAvoy, Chicago. The two MONTEZUMAs that were on the lakes in the summer of 1871, both out of Chicago, were lost almost exactly one year apart (see above).

name: MONTGOMERY
detail: Bound Kenosha for Oswego with wheat, she was seeking safe harbor from a storm when she went ashore and was later broken up. The hull and cargo were a total loss of $13,000, and the loss of the vessel was cited in an appeal to the Governor-general of Canada to improve the Bay of Quinte to a Harbour of Refuge. Owned by E. A. Bruce, Chicago.

name: MONTGOMERY
other_names: built on hull of schooner NORTHERN LIGHT(qv), blt in 1866 at St. Catharines
build_info: 1866, S. Johnson, Clayton, NY as a bark
place_of_loss: Stoney Cr., 6 mi N of Sheboygan, WI
detail: She was driven ashore and stranded, where she remained until she broke up the following winter.

name: MONTGOMERY
build_info: 1856, John Bushnell, Newport, MI as a passenger/freight propeller
detail: Tow of the steamer LELAND, she began to leak in a gale and became waterlogged. She was cut loose from LELAND after her crew was taken off. It was expected that she would founder, but she went onto a bar near livesaving station number 10 [Crisp Point] and broke up in another storm two weeks later. Primarily owned by her skipper, Capt. Duff of Port Clinton, OH. Repaired in 1866 at Detroit. Rebuilt from propeller to schooner in 1879 at Algonac after a major fire off Point Edward [L Huron], Jun 9, 1878. Rebuilt from 3-mast schooner to barge in 1889. Ashore near Menominee in November, 1890 and thought to be broken in two and a total loss. Ashore on the Canadian side of Lake Huron in 1896 after being cut loose from the steamer J. B. KETCHAM in a storm. Sunk in a collision in Lake St. Clair, 1898.
media_notes: Image as a steamer from GLMD

name: MONTICELLO
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: She began to leak when midway between Ontonagon and Eagle Harbor, MI, and was run to shallow water, where she sank and later broke up. She had had a collision with the steamer MANHATTAN earlier in the year, but no damage was found. It is suspected that her hull was weakened by the collision and this caused it to fail in the storm. The boats were two of only four steamers on Lake Superior at the time of their collision. Her engine was probably recovered. Owned by Sheldon McKnight of Detroit Master: Capt. John Wilson. Hauled around the Soo rapids on skids the previous winter. *Also given: 160x24x11, 460g 364n

name: MONTICELLO
detail: She was driven ashore and destroyed by a gale. See MONTAUK for note on name.

name: MONTMORENCY
other_names: also seen as MONTMORENCI, built on hull of schooner SOVEREIGN OF THE LAKES(qv)*
build_info: 1866, Johnson, Clayton, NY as a schooner**
place_of_loss: off west side of Big Charity Isl., Saginaw Bay
detail: She was lost in fog and stranded in the shallows. Later broken up by storms. The charted wreck is a popular dive target. May have been under her own sail at the time of loss, though some say she was in tow. Owned by F. King of W. Bay City *Built as a new vessel on a burnt-out hull. **Some sources say this rebuild took place at St. Catharines. major repairs in 1880 & 83

name: MONTPELIER
detail: She was on a routine trip up the river in good weather when hull began to leak profusely. The crew abandoned her as she sank, a total loss. Also sank off Grand Haven, MI, Nov 1, 1878, and spent more than a week ashore in southern Lake Huron in fall, 1870. *W side of Stag Isl. also given. Belle Isle is from official record.

name: MOONLIGHT
detail: Tow of twin-stack steamer VOLUNTEER, she broke her hawser and began to sink in a terrific gale. VOLUNTEER took her crew off before she foundered. Member of Gilchrist Fleet, Cleveland [J. C. Gilchrist, prop.]. There are famous photos of her high on Chocolay Beach, L. Superior in 1886 when she went ashore with HENRY A. KENT. She was salvaged a year later. Also ashore, declared a total loss in 1889.
sources: gwgl,is,sol,sagl,ns1,nsp,polk,lss,wb —-——————————— ALEXANDER B. MOORE – See NORTHWEST
media_notes: Image as a schooner from GLMD Image as being salvaged, 1896. Same source

name: H. MOORE
detail: While rounding to with the tug D. N. RUNNELS, to enter the Port Huron Dry dock, her towline parted and she drifted down the river, finally coming to rest near the “middle ground.” She was subsequently struck and wrecked by several other vessels, as she sat right in the channnel, 200 feet out from the Flint & Pere Marquette Railroad dock. She was reported as being “blowed up with dynamite” by Capt Hemminger, Algonac, on Oct 14, 1891. Document surrendered at Port Huron 1/12/1892, annotated “Wrecked, total loss.”

name: H.B. MOORE
place_of_loss: 26 mi SE of Chicago, near Michigan City
detail: She drove hard ashore in a gale. She was so high up that her crew landed on solid ground when they went over the side. Though she was only lightly damaged, salvagers were unable to pull her back to deep water and she was stripped and abandoned. Master: Capt. Chas. Elson; out of Chicago. Major repairs in 1874,80

name: H.D. MOORE
place_of_loss: south side of harbor at S. Manitou Isl.
detail: She sought refuge from a storm in the harbor, but struck a rocky area, settled and was pounded to pieces by waves. She had been bound Harbor Springs, Mich., for Port Washington, Wis.

name: JOHN MOORE
detail: Foundered, no detail. Registered out of Midland, Ont., Canadian Dredging Co.

name: SMITH MOORE
place_of_loss: ENE of Grand Island, near Munising, MI
detail: She was rammed in a hit-and-run by steamer JAMES PICKANDS in dense fog. Dead in the water, she remained afloat for some time, until the fog lifted and the steamer M.M. DRAKE(qv) came to her assistance. She was taken in tow for Munising, but sank enroute. Some sources say she was built in 1881.

name: T. M. MOORE
other_names: also seen as THOMAS M. MOORE
detail: Stranded, then totally destroyed by fire. Owned by Frank Harmon. There was another, almost identical tug of this name, built at Buffalo in 1880 (#145222), and reportedly burned at Grand Island, Niagara River, the same month. Recovered and rebuilt as WARNICK (qv).

name: W.A. MOORE
other_names: none also seen as WM A. MOORE
detail: This large tug burned caught fire in her coal bunker while lying at her dock near the Willow Street bridge, Cleveland. Initial reports were that she was only moderately damaged, but she never sailed again. Owned by Grummond Tug Line, Detroit. Also sank in a squall in Saginaw Bay in June, 1871. When she was recovered a month later, the soggy vessel got up steam immediately and made it to Detroit on her own.
media_notes: Image of Grummond Tugs around 1890, from GLMD

name: JOHN V. MORAN
build_info: 1888, F.W. Wheeler, W. Bay City, MI hull #44
detail: The MORAN was cut by ice during a mid-winter run and foundered after being carried a considerable distance in the ice. The iron passenger and package freight steamer NAOMI, her fleetmate, rescued her crew before she drifted away. Chartered by Crosby Transportation Co. She was a combination bulk and package freighter, with hatches in her flanks and on her deck as well. She also had iron plating on her hull to help her break ice on winter Lake Michigan. Wreck located in 365 feet of water about 15 miles offshore in the summer of 2015 . Link to story.

name: MORANIA #130
detail: Tow of tug DAUNTLESS #12(qv), she was rammed by the freighter PENOBSCOT in a passing error, exploded and turned into an inferno as her 800,000 gallon cargo went up in flames. Owned by Penn No. 5, Inc., New York. Hulk later removed.

name: FRANCISCO MORAZAN
other_names: built as ARCADIA, renamed ELBING(’34), EMPIRE CONGRESS(’45), BRUNES(’46), SKULD(’47), RINGAS(’48), LOS MAYAS (’58), last name in 1959.
type_at_loss: propeller, steel, package and bulk freight
place_of_loss: SW corner of S. Manitou Isl.
detail: Bound from Chicago, she lost her way in a gale and blizzard and struck bottom. She filled and sank athwart the remains of steamer WALTER W. FROST(qv). The Coast Guard rescued her crew by cutter and helicopter. Her remains are visible from shore.
media_notes: Image of MORAZAN wreck today by Lars Jensen [GoogleEarth] Image as RINGAS from Wikipedia

name: WILLIAM C. MORELAND
other_names: later SIR TREVOR DAWSON(1916), CHARLES L. HUTCHINSON(1921), GENE C. HUTCHINSON(1951) and PARKDALE(1963)
place_of_loss: Sawtooth Reef, near Eagle Harbor, MI
detail: She stranded on the reef on what was only her fifth trip. While her stern was jammed fast, the bow, overhanging a dropoff, filled, broke off and sank in deep water. The brand new stern was salvaged the next year, towed back to Lorain, where a new bow portion was installed.

name: F. MORELL
other_names: none also seen as F. MORRELL
build_info: 1866, Lester & Luff, Marine City, MI* former bark
detail: Drove ashore and wrecked in fog and big seas. After being ashore a few days, she was reportedly broken up by a NE storm on the 9 th . Homeport: Buffalo, owned by Peter Wex and her skipper, Capt. John Mullins *Builder also given as Luff & Gilchrist

name: C.P. MOREY
other_names: also seen as CHARLES P. MOREY
detail: Thrown against the breakwater and wrecked as she was trying to assist disabled schooner WAYNE(qv). Declared a total loss, but recovered and rebuilt in 1879. Master: Capt. Pappa. Off registry in 1895. Stereo image from MHGL

name: T.T. MORFORD
detail: Towing the steamer IONIA with the tug O.B. GREEN, her boiler exploded without warning, killing two on the MORFORD and one on the GREEN and injuring two others. GREEN was raked with shrapnel and a large part of the boiler was thrown through a grain elevator on shore. Master: Capt. John Ferguson(d). MORFORD was almost completely destroyed, but rebuilt and renumbered as a new vessel the following year [US#145717].
sources: nsp,mv,mpl —-——————————— ARTHUR MORGAN – See D.P. RHODES

name: J.P. MORGAN, Jr
detail: Collided almost perfectly “bows-on” with steel prop CRETE. Neither sank, but both were heavily damaged and two crewmen were crushed to death and three others injured in the foc’sle of MORGAN. Pittsburg Fleet.
media_notes: Image from HCGL Image of wreck damage

name: GEORGE W. MORLEY
build_info: 1888, F. Wheeler, W Bay City, MI hull #37
detail: Bound Milwaukee for Chicago, she exploded and caught fire, sank 100 yds off Greenwood Ave., Evanston. Her engine was recovered in Oct., 1898.

name: MORNING LIGHT
detail: Bound Manistee for Chicago, she was driven ashore and wrecked in a storm. One crewman swam to shore, the rest were saved by a lifesaving crew, local fishermen and the tug B.W ALDRICH. Out of Buffalo, owned by her skipper, Capt Thomas Burch, and Lorain Smith, both of Buffalo. Ashore in a storm and given up for lost, fall of 1857. Heavily damaged in a collision with the schooner L.M. MASON near Bar Pt. in 1860. Sunk in the Straits in Jun or Jul of the same year as her loss.

name: MORNING STAR
detail: Capsized and sank. This may refer to a capsizing on Jun 17, 1834, while the vessel was bound down. After she went over, with no loss of life, the steamer SUPERIOR attempted to tow her to port, but lost her. She was expected to be a total loss. In April of 1826 she ran ashore near Pelee Isl. and broke her rudder, whereupon her crew abandoned her. Later she floated off on her own, and her skipper and a crewman were lost trying to catch up with her. She was later towed in to Amherstburg (Malden), where she was found to have shipped little water in the mishap.

name: MORNING STAR
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
carrying: iron bars, glass, farm equipment, general merchandise
detail: Bound Cleveland for Detroit, the steamer MORNING STAR had been late leaving her dock because she was loading some last-minute freight. On the dark and rainy night she collided with the heavy-laden bark COURTLAND(qv) and sank quickly. Most of the survivors were providentially picked up by the passing steamer R. N. RICE. In Sep she was raised towed to Lorain and resunk in 55 feet of water, for possible future rebuilding. Attempts were made to raise her again several times, but in the summer of 1872 she was abandoned because it was determined that the previous attempts had reduced her to rubble. Registered out of Detroit, owned by the D & C line [Detroit & Cleveland Steam Navigation Co.]. One source claims she was in tow at the time.

name: MORNING STAR
detail: Bound Toledo for Buffalo, she foundered in gale in 75 feet of water. The tug SPRAGUE had droppeed her when she became unmanageable and ran for shelter, but was unable to find her again when she returned. Owned by J Kelderhouse of Buffalo. Master: Capt. John W. Thompson(d). First reports identified her as “Morning Light” She & her sister EVENING STAR were dubbed “Chang” and “Eng” (after the “Siamese Twins”) during building. The names stuck for awhile.

name: MORNING STAR
place_of_loss: E side of S. Fox I.
detail: Bound Charlevoix, Mich, for Chicago, she stranded in a storm and and later broke up. The local lightkeeper brought them to Northport, Mich., a few days later. Other sources say this accident was at Michigan City, Ind., in error. Owned by F. X. Ulrich, Milwaukee. Major repair in 1880

name: DANIEL J. MORRELL
build_info: 1906, W. Bay City Shipbuilding, W. Bay City, MI hull# 619
place_of_loss: 25 mi N of Port Austin, MI
detail: This big steamer broke in two and foundered in 25-foot waves. The stern portion plowed on past the only three survivors before she sank. They had made it to a life raft, but two of them died of exposure before their craft came ashore. Both parts of the ship lie in the Thumb Area Preserve diving area – the stern was found in 1967, bow in 1979. Master: Capt. Arthur L. Crawley(d). Official USCG Wreck Report (.pdf file, courtesy Kimm Satbelfeldt)

name: DAVID MORRIS
build_info: 1857, W. Jones, Black R., OH as a bark
place_of_loss: Pigeon Bay, near Pt. Pelee, Ont.
detail: Tow of prop T.U. BRADBURY, she broke away, was driven ashore and wrecked beside her fellow barge GLOBE (qv). Worth about $4,000. Owned by Capt. “Phin” Pomeroy of Saginaw. Master: Capt. Gains.

name: FRANK MORRIS
detail: She was blown ashore and wrecked while bound for Detroit. Her crew of 5 drifted 300 feet to shore on a broken spar. Owned by Capt. Geo. McClure, Detroit, her skipper

name: ANNIE F. MORSE
place_of_loss: between South Haven and Whitehall, MI
detail: Bound South Haven for Whitehall, Mich, she was driven ashore 3 miles north of South Haven. She may have been sailed with only one or two hands. *May 19 also given, in error

name: FRED A. MORSE
place_of_loss: 12 mi SE of Thunder Bay Isl.
detail: She collided with the prop JOHN C. PRINGLE and sank in 200 feet of water while in tow of the steamer HORACE A. TUTTLE with two other schooners. Her crew abandoned in her boat and were picked up by the TUTTLE, as was the skipper of the PRINGLE, who had been knocked overboard by a falling spar. Registered out of Cleveland. Master: Capt. Bonnah. Ashore and abandoned in Little Traverse Bay, MI, Nov., 1873, but recovered by the tug GLADIATOR in the summer of ’74. Major repair in 1882

name: JAY C. MORSE
other_names: none seen in early mvus’s and nsps as J.C. MORSE
detail: Struck boulders offshore and sank. Recovered, possibly much later. Struck a rock and foundered on a sightseeing trip with the loss of one life, Jul 17, 1867, after an unsuccessful run to shore. Shown in ’69 mvus as sidewheeler

name: HAMILTON MORTON
build_info: 1854, S. L. Collyer, Buffalo as a tug
detail: She was wrecked by fire and declared a total loss. Lay in the river for four years until her hull was recovered and rebuilt as W.R. MUIR (qv).

name: JULIUS D. MORTON
build_info: 1848, A.C. Keating, Monroe, Mich as a sidewheel passenger steamer, later a tug
detail: Tow of the propeller SUN with five others, she broke loose and went to pieces soon after. The captain, his wife and their little daughter, plus a sailor, escaped in the tiny yawl, while two other sailors who later drowned, elected to remain aboard. MORTON had been bound Saginaw for Erie, Pa. Master: Capt. P.H. Fick. She caught fire near Pte Aux Chenes [near Algonac] and burned to the waterline Apr 8, 1863. The blaze was thought to be the work of an arsonist. Raised and rebuilt to this barge. Also wrecked in a storm on Thunder Bay, MI, in 1853.

name: MARY MORTON
detail: Bound for Buffalo, she went ashore and broke in two amidships. By the end of the month she had been abandoned. Owned by William Rankin & Capt. George Wilson, her master, (Detroit?). Rebuilt in 1867.

name: MINNIE MORTON
place_of_loss: Bois Blanc Isl., near Bar Pt.
detail: She was working on raising the sunken tug MARTIN SWAIN and was tied to the wreck for the night when she was struck by an out-of-control raft of lumber in tow of the tug JOHN OWEN. The MORTON was forced up on the raft and dragged away, while most of her crew scrambled onto the SWAIN. The tug was pulled out into the lake off Bar Point, where the logs parted and she sank in about 25 feet of water, while her skipper, who had remained aboard, jumped onto the raft. Owned by Odette & Wherry, Windsor, Ont.

name: SYLVIA MORTON
place_of_loss: Sandy Beach, Michaels Bay, Manitoulin Isl.
detail: She was cast adrift by the steamer EMPIRE near White Shoals, Lake Michigan[!] and drifted across Lake Huron and onto this beach. As of Nov 1, salvagers were still trying to release her – she was not heavily damaged, but was far up the beach. Reg out of Detroit, owned by Murphy , Capt. Dearing, master.

name: RICHARD MORWOOD
other_names: built as schooner LENNIE or LINNIE POWELL, renamed in 1875
detail: Separated from her tow vessel and with sails torn apart, she struck bottom and sank after drifting for some time. Homeport: St. Catharines. Owner: Morwood . Master: Capt. McPherson. Declared a total loss, but recovered by Americans in 1888. Changed to an American bottom and rebuilt as E.B. PALMER. Wrecked near Buffalo in 1859. Rebuilt 1874 by Bald, Port Dover and in 1889

name: MOSES AND ELIAS
detail: Stranded and wrecked.

name: ALFRED MOSHER
detail: She was laid up for the night at the ‘”mill slip” when she caught fire. She was towed out by the tug NELSON and water was poured on her until she sank in the bay. Her boiler was recovered in 1912 and the engine in 1933, while the hull was left to rot away. Owned by Walker, Dumann and Helmholtz of Sturgeon and chartered to lumberman Capt. Peter Peterson, Menominee.

name: AMARETTA MOSHER
place_of_loss: Starve Isl. Reef, near Port Clinton, OH
detail: Lost from the tow of steamer D.F. ROSE, she was blown on the reef and wrecked. Bound Huron, OH for Port Huron in company with barges WAWANOSH(qv) and LYMAN CASEY. Owner: Capt. David Hutchinson. *one source says she was changed to a 3-master in 1885

name: A.H. MOSS
detail: In tow of the steamer ELFIN-MERE when she broke loose and went ashore. Her crew was rescued by the tug ANNIE MOILES. Homeport: Detroit Owner: Hurley. Also wrecked in a gale near Buffalo, Nov 24, 1882. Rebuilt, 1883

name: J.O. MOSS
place_of_loss: near Big Sable Point, Michigan side
detail: after being battered by a gale, she was lying at anchor and doing repairs in a moderate blow when she was hit with the renewed fury of the storm. Her anchors dragged and she was blown into the shallows, where she went to pieces. 5 crew were rescued from rigging by Lifesaving Service, but the sixth tried to save himself on his own and was lost. The vessel was reportedly recovered, but doesn’t show on later official lists, perhaps a constructive total loss..

name: JOHN T. MOTT
detail: No detail. She was sunk by a collision with the schooner MONTICELLO in 1884 near Colchester Reef, Lake Erie. Her coal cargo was salvaged in July, 1889 and sold to Canadians, and the vessel was brought up in August of the same year. Sunk on Colchester shoal in Sep, 1884 and declared a total loss. Also reported sunk at Fairport, OH, L. Erie, in an 1893 in a gale reported to have pushed 70-mph winds. Sold Canadian, 1903

name: THOMAS S. MOTT
detail: Bound Chicago for Buffalo, she struck bottom and sprung a leak in a gale. Her crew fought the rising water with her pumps for more than 12 hours until she finally drifted ashore. After several attempts a boat from the steamer BERTSCHY was able to rescue her crew. She went to pieces quickly. Her gear and outfit was salvaged the following summer. Out of Chicago. Master: Capt. McGowell.

name: MOUNT VERNON
place_of_loss: 3 mi ESE of “the Dummy,” Pt. Pelee
detail: Bound Detroit for Buffalo, she exploded her boiler and became a total loss. Her stern was blown to pieces and she sank in 20 minutes, a total loss. The survivors of her crew climbed her rigging as she went down and were rescued by the schooners FOX and LOOKOUT. Her final enrollment document of Sep 16, 1859 is annotated “Lost, burst her boiler, 1860.” Owned by Western Transportation Co. Master: Capt. Newman. Her wreckage was struck by several vessels in the months that followed, causing the loss of at least the scow OTTOCA of Saginaw.

name: MOUNT VERNON
detail: Blasted by a storm while loading at Dean’s Pier, she set sail to avoid beeing destroyed against the dock, but did not answer the helm and drove ashore north of the pier. Only sunk to her decks, but probably abandoned as a total loss. Major repair in 1861

name: MOUNTAIN MAID
detail: Bound for Cleveland with a heavy load, capsized NW of the west pier and sank. Her crew was taken off by the tug N. B. GATES. Her sunken hull was later reported as a dangerous obstruction 400 ft. NW of the Black River lighthouse. Owned by Russel, out of Cleveland. Master: Capt. Will Faragher. Rebuilt in 1864

name: MOUNTAINEER
place_of_loss: near Port Glasgow, Ont. [Pte Aux Pins], also given as Tyrconnell
detail: She was driven into shallows by a SE gale and severely damaged a Tyrconnel lumber dock by colliding with it. Initially she was only lightly damaged, but the building gale finally pounded her to pieces, a total loss. Stranded with heavy damage on Mohawk Isl., near Port Maitland, Ont., in 1857. Sunk at Sandusky, OH, in Oct., 1863. Ashore on the north side of Georgian Bay and declared a total loss in November, 1864. Heavily damaged in a grounding on Georgian Bay in 1869. Reported a total loss on Lake Huron near Meaford, Ont, in October, 1873, but recovered in 1875. Rebuilt 1870 & 1873, Owen Sound. Major repair in 1861 Maybe the vessel built at Port Stanley and launched in June, 1882. [mmgl (N.A.C.) shows this register closed 2/11/1881]

name: OLIVER MOWAT
other_names: none also seen as OLIVER MOWATT
place_of_loss: near Pennicon Shoal off Main Duck Isl.
detail: Rammed and sunk by the 1700 ton steel steamer KEYWEST, whose Capt & Mate were jailed for keeping such a poor lookout. Bound Oswego for Picton and owned out of Bowmanville; known as a fast sailer, she was a total loss of $10,000. Master: Capt. Thomas VanDeusen(d). Ashore & heavily damaged by storm near Oshawa, Ont. Nov 28, 1905. *Sep 21 also given in error as date.

name: JAMES MOWATT
detail: Rammed a stone jetty and sank, a total loss. Out of Port Huron, Mich Ashore near Eagle River, Mich, L. Superior, and thoroughly wrecked in September, 1893. She was hauled into drydock at Milwaukee but abandoned to her underwriters as a constructive total loss.

name: GEORGE M. MOWBRAY
other_names: also seen as G. W. MOWBRAY
detail: Bound Montreal for Fort William, she was caught in a NW gale and snowstorm and fled to the harbor for safety. She struck a pier and sank, where she became a total loss. Salvagers later removed her dangerous cargo [shipped by Hamilton Glycerine of Montreal to Canadian Pacific RR] before it caused more trouble. Master: Capt. Mellwain.

name: MARGARET A. MUIR
detail: Bound Bay City for South Chicago, she foundered in a gale. Came to rest standing on her stern with bow emergent, but was later pounded to pieces. Her crew abandoned in her yawl. Out of Chicago, owned by Capt. David Clow and David Muir of Chicago and Detroit. Major repair in 1884

name: W.R. MUIR
other_names: also seen as W.K. MUIR*. Built as HAMILTON MORTON(qv), renamed in 1863
place_of_loss: Near the mouth of the Black River.
carrying: none [bark CITY OF BUFFALO and another vessel in tow]
detail: Her engineer, noticing the water was low in her boiler, stopped her engine and began to douse her fires. The captain of her tow, CITY OF BUFFALO, coasting up behind, frantically signalled her to get out of the way. When she restarted her engine, a terrific boiler explosion resulted. Canadian registry, but owned out of Detroit by J. Pridgeon. Two of his brothers died in the explosion. Master: Capt. Robert Pridgeon(d). Burned at Detroit and sunk in Nov, 1858. Raised in 1863, rebuilt and enlarged, becoming a Canadian bottom in 1864. Raised and returned to U.S. bottom after this accident, US#26249 *There was a tug W.K. MUIR built at Milwaukee, 1862 [US#26367]. She had a major fire at Milwaukee, July 4, 1867, dismantled in 1881.

name: ANNIE MULVEY
detail: She was lying at the Rogers & Co. dock when she was caught up in the “Esplanade Fire” of August 3-4. She was scuttled to try to save her and her cargo, but was declared a total loss soon after and the hull removed. Owned by Wm. Hall, Toronto.

name: C. J. G. MUNRO
other_names: also seen as C.J.G. MONROE and CHARLES JOHN GEMLEY MUNRO
detail: Caught fire from a lamp explosion and destroyed. Her engine and boiler were later salvaged and the hull abandoned. Document surrendered in April, 1893. Owned out of Port Colborne

name: COLIN MUNRO
build_info: 1873, J. Allison or Ellison, Port Stanley, Ont.
detail: Burned to the water’s edge. Owned out of Port Stanley by T. Mentor.

name: MUNSON
detail: She had just finished dredging a channel for the launch of the big schooner MINNEDOSA[qv], and was on the return trip to Belleville in charge of the tug EMMA MUNSON, along with two small scows, when a minor leak turned into a major one. From the discovery of the leak to the loss of the vessel was only a matter of four minutes. The vessel’s cook was dragged down an estimated 35 feet by the suction of the sinking boat, but was able to make it back to the surface. The vessel went down in 100-130 feet of water. Owned by E. A. Munson of Cobourg, Ont, sailing out of Belleville.

name: MUNSON
detail: Bound for Belleville, Ont. In tow of the tug EMMA MUNSON, she sprang a leak and sank quickly in 130 ft of water. Owned by K. A. Munson, Cobourg, Ont. hgl gives location as “Loosemore’s Point.” Not in Polk.

name: MUNSTER
detail: Foundered well offshore. Owned by Dunbar & Sullivan Dredging, Buffalo.

name: MURIEL W.
other_names: built as propeller VERONICA, renamed in 1919
detail: Struck a sunken crib and sank. Sold Canadian in 1919.

name: PADDY MURPHY
place_of_loss: off Dover Bay, 12 mi W of Cleveland
detail: Bound for Lorain with the barge REPUBLIC in tow, she caught fire and was turned to shore to save her and her crew. The crew survived, but MURPHY grounded in 8 feet of water and burned to the waters edge, a total loss. Recently purchased by Thos. Maytham of Buffalo, but not turned over to him yet. Owned by Thomas Axworthy, Cleveland.

name: GRACE MURRAY
detail: This ancient schooner or schooner-barge had lain against a bar near shore for some years when a fire tripped off by a railroad switch engine set the nearby Raupp & Co. Lumber yard ablaze. The veteran craft was set on fire by flying sparks and quicky burned to the waterline. Out of Detroit Had many minor mishaps in her career: collisions in 1866 and 78; strandings in 1860, 69, 73 and 84; a sinking in 1871 and a fire in 1869.

name: ELLA MURTON
type_at_loss: schooner-barge, wood, bulk freight, crane equipped
place_of_loss: South Bay, S end of Manitoulin Isl.
detail: Stranded near the mouth of the Bay and wrecked by waves. *mmgl – file closes 11/1/1900, registration closes 4/27/03.

name: MUSCALLONGE
place_of_loss: near Port Weller, Ont. or Brockville
detail: Towing the fuel barge BRUCE HUDSON (qv), she caught fire in her galley and was run to the shallows. The next morning her 1,000 gallons of bunker fuel caught fire and the tug exploded, burned to the waterline and sank. Master: Capt. Ahern. Owner: Sincennes-McNaughten, Montreal, chartered to Lloyd Tankers, Toronto. Sold Canadian, 1913.

name: MUSIC
build_info: 1892, McMillan, South Haven, MI as an excursion boat
detail: Burned to a total loss at Onekama, Mich., along with the Onekama Lumber Co’s dock and warehouse. Local folklore – probably true – has it that she was cut loose and drifted down the lake, burning like a torch, though newspaper accounts don’t mention it. Owned by H. Slingerland, Manistee.
media_notes: Image as passenger vessel from GLMD

name: MUSKEGON
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, wood, passenger & package freight
detail: She fell a few feet off her blocks in drydock at the Milwaukee Dry Dock Co. The hull broke in two and was damaged beyond repair, even though some experts felt she could be repaired. She was auctioned off in December for $250 and remained in Milwaukee until she was dismantled in 1905. The Goodrich Line steamer was worth $25,000 at the time of the mishap.

name: MUSKEGON
other_names: built as pass/pkg freighter PEERLESS, renamed in 1907
place_of_loss: at dock at Michigan City, IN
detail: Unloading at the dock when fire broke out in her after end. She burned to total loss. Later towed to deep water and cast off to sink, but refused to go down and drifted ashore. Owned by Independent Sand & Gravel Co., Chicago. In September, 1877 she jettisoned a cargo to keep her from sinking after a collision. The cargo consisted of flour, feed and sundries, plus26 head of cattle and about 70 sheep. Had a short career in 1907 as a floating casino. Converted to a bulk freighter in 1908 after more than 35 years as a crack passenger steamer. Converted to a sandsucker in 1910
media_notes: Image as a wreck from GLMD

name: MUSKEGON
other_names: built as CITY OF MILWAUKEE, renamed HOLLAND(1905), last name in 1919. Some reports erroneously call her “City of Muskegon”
type_at_loss: sidewheel steamer, iron, passenger & package freight
build_info: 1881, Detroit Dry Dock, Wyandotte, MI hull# 44
detail: She went out of control in a westerly gale due to a problem with the engines and rammed the pierhead while docking. She sank quickly, trapping most of the lost below decks. Owned by Holland Steamship Co. (Crosby Transportation Co.) of Chicago. Built for the famous Goodrich Line and designed by Frank Kirby.
media_notes: Image as HOLLAND from Hudson Keenan Collection Image of her wreckage washing ashore from GLMD

name: MUSKINGUM
detail: Bound Cleveland for Chicago, she ran ashore after the the shifting of her anchor chain from forward to aft threw her compass off and she went ashore in the storm. She was reportedly broken up beyonf saving by the next day. Owners: McCormick Bros. & Finefield. Master: Capt. McCormick.

name: MYLES
detail: She stranded on a shoal when she went off course due to a buoy blown out of place. Immediately declared a total loss, but later refoated after a long and difficult salvage effort, and went on to a career lasting until 1926. In that year she was named THERESA T & laid up. Abandoned in ’42. Shoal where she stranded is sometimes charted as “Myles Shoal.” Rebuilt to barge in 1916, re-engined in 1918, back to a barge in 26. Rebuilt 1896, 1907, 1918.
media_notes: Image as sunk in 1886, from GLMD

name: MYOSOTIS
build_info: 1874, Milwaukee Shipyard, Milwaukee [begun by Allen & McClelland, Milwaukee]
place_of_loss: near harbor mouth at St. Joseph, MI
detail: Bound Escanaba for St. Joseph, she struck a reef near the harbor entrance and sank to her decks. Her crew made it ashore in her boat, but the vessel was pounded to pieces by waves. Master: Capt. Maloney. This was the 4th vessel of Capt. A. P. Read’s fleet of Chicago to be wrecked within a month, almost wiping him out financially [CITY OF GREEN BAY, HAVANA, $12,000 damage to D.S. AUSTIN].

name: MYRA
detail: Left Toledo for Cleveland, following a track made in the ice by a tug, but got caught after 7 or 8 miles. A tug and tow which was behind her had to pass, pushing her further into the ice field. Though her crew tried to save her, she soon began to leak and sank in relatively shallow water. Out of Saybrook, OH

name: MYRA
detail: Stranded and broke up, a total loss. Out of Sand Beach

name: MYRON
other_names: built as steamer MARK HOPKINS, renamed in 1902
detail: Towing the schooner-barge MIZTEC(qv), she began to leak in a terrific NW gale. Her crew cut the barge loose and prepared to abandon, but seas were too high. Despite steamer ADRIATIC trying to block the weather and 3 attempts by the Lifesaving Service, MYRON foundered with all still on board. Only one survived. Out of Duluth. Wreck was located in 1972. Sunk by collision in Hay Lake, 1895, at Chicago, 1896

name: MYRTIE
detail: Stranded on reef the 2nd, then broke up in big gale the 15th. Owner: Lake Erie Dredging Co.

name: MYRTIE
detail: no detail. Her engine was removed and the hull was later recovered and rebuilt as TUG ELMER (C75634) and registered out of Saugeen 1894-1900. Built for use as a river tug.

name: MYRTLE
place_of_loss: Chicago, at the base of 34th st.
detail: Struggled into Chicago but went out of control and collided with the schooners EVENING STAR(qv) & CLIFFORD, then drifted down the beach and sank within sight of thousands of helpless bystanders on shore. All hands were lost. Master: Capt. Jerome Wilson(d); vessel out of Chicago. Rebuilt in 1871. *Also reported as a sharp-bowed scow **builder also given as Kelley

name: MYSTERY
place_of_loss: E side of Sawyer Bay, near Sibley Peninsula*
detail: Destroyed by fire. Her remains are still visible at the site. Owned by Mills & Barnes, Port Arthur, Ont. Out of Owen Sound [also given as Thunder Bay, Ont.] *location also given as Saugeen, Ont, L. Huron Burned at White Cloud Isl, Geo. Bay, Aug 31, 1911. Rebuilt, 1916 and shortened 5 feet.

name: MYSTIC
other_names: built as U.S. tug H.P. CLINTON, renamed, 1876
detail: No detail. Final document surrendered Nov 24, 1890, annotated “lost” in 1881. Rebuilt in Canada in 1865, again at Detroit the following year. Burned on the Saginaw River in June, 1868. Wrecked in the North Channel near Cockburn Isl. in early Sep, 1878. Recovered in May of the following year, and rebuilt..Sold Canadian in 1879 to L. P. Trempe, Soo, Ont.. One source says owned in Windsor, Ont. In 1884, but maybe as salvageable wreck.

name: MYSTIC
detail: Carrying a party of surveyors when she caught fire offshore and was run to shore at Ransom’s Landing to save her people. There she burned to the waterline. Owned out of Port Huron by D. N. Runnels. Major repairs in 1877.

name: MYSTIC
detail: A gale blew her very high on the rocks on the east side of the island. Her crew came ashore hand-over-hand on a line, while the schooner broke up. Her outfit was saved by local lightkeepers. She had been bound for Little Bay de Noc for a load of Christmas trees. Owned by Samuel Swinson of Chicago and chartered to Henry Schuenemann
sources: nsp,hgl,polk,wl,ec,wgts Share this: Share on X (Opens in new window) Share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Facebook Like Loading...

name: N. ELLEN M.
place_of_loss: 5 mi E of Cabot Head, Georgian Bay
detail: Burned to a total loss. Rebuilt at Wiarton, Ont in 1936 Originally named after the builder’s daughter, Molly.

name: N. 1
detail: Lying at her anchorage on Grassy Point when the area was swept by the tremendous Cloquet Forest Fire. Northern Dock & Dredge’s facilities were destroyed along with nine vessels. See dredge DULUTH, tugs ELLA G. STONE, MENTOR and JOHN H JEFFREY, JR, scows N. 2 thru N.4 and scows No.1 and No. 40.

name: N. 2
detail: Lying at her anchorage on Grassy Point when the area was swept by the tremendous Cloquet Forest Fire. Northern Dock & Dredge’s facilities were destroyed along with nine vessels. See dredge DULUTH, tugs ELLA G. STONE, MENTOR and JOHN H JEFFREY, JR, scows N. 1 thru N.4 and scows No.1 and No. 40.

name: N. 3
detail: Lying at her anchorage on Grassy Point when the area was swept by the tremendous Cloquet Forest Fire. Northern Dock & Dredge’s facilities were destroyed along with nine vessels. See dredge DULUTH, tugs ELLA G. STONE, MENTOR and JOHN H JEFFREY, JR, scows N. 1 thru N.4 and scows No.1 and No. 40.

name: N. 4
detail: Lying at her anchorage on Grassy Point when the area was swept by the tremendous Cloquet Forest Fire. Northern Dock & Dredge’s facilities were destroyed along with eight or nine vessels. See dredge DULUTH, tugs ELLA G. STONE, MENTOR and JOHN H JEFFREY, JR, scows N. 1 thru N. 3 and scows No.1 and No.40.

name: N. G.
detail: She had just been purchased for $800 and was enroute from Cleveland to her new homeport when she was driven ashore by a storm and wrecked. Out of Port Dover, owned by John Jennings, sailmaker. Rebuilt and enlarged at Port Burwell in 1852, originally 84x17x7, 97 t. Heavily damaged in a wreck near Avon Point in 1855.

name: NAGAHO
other_names: built as schooner-barge F.R. BUELL*, renamed in 1920
build_info: 1888, Dulac, Mt. Clemens, MI as a schooner-barge
detail: Bound Buffalo for Montreal, she was damaged by a westerly gale, limped in and sank near shore. In November she was declared a constructive total loss and was abandoned. *engine installed 1889 & became package freighter. Rebuilt to a bulker in 1904.
media_notes: Image as prop F. R. BUELL from GLMD

name: NAHANT
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, package and bulk freight
place_of_loss: Escanaba, MI, Little Bay de Noc
detail: She caught fire while loading at ore dock No. 4 and both dock and boat were destroyed. Firefighters were hampered by sub-zero temperatures and she burned to a total loss. Owned by C. E. Benham, Cleveland. Wreckage is reportedly still visible from the Escanaba lighthouse. Major repairs, 1880

name: NAIAD
build_info: 1863, M. Squires, Huron, OH as a bark [US#18100]
place_of_loss: off Au Sable Point, near Oscoda, MI
detail: She was waterlogged and heavily damaged in a storm. Later she was towed in to Harbor Beach, MI, by the steamer ISAAC L. ELLWOOD and abandoned there. Also heavily damaged and lost her rigging in a storm in July of 1895. Two men went over the side and were lost with the rigging. Rebuilt, 1876. major repairs in 1882 Sold Canadian in 1902

name: H.M.S.
type_at_loss: schooner, wood, built as a commercial fur-trading vessel, later a naval support vessel and gunboat
place_of_loss: site of Nancy Island, Georgian Bay
detail: She was burned and sunk by U.S. warships TIGRESS, NIAGARA and SCORPION after being bottled up in Nottawasaga Bay. Nancy Island eventually built up around her remains. Parts of the vessel, raised by the Canadian government in 1927, reside in a museum at Collingwood, Ont. Some sources say she was a commercial vessel at the time of her loss.
media_notes: Image of sail plan from GLMD

name: NAOMI
place_of_loss: Near Manistee, MI on Big Sable Point
detail: Bound Erie, PA for Milwaukee, she was caught in a storm, drove ashore and was wrecked. Her skipper, his wife and at least one crewman drowned. Out of Ashtabula, owned by Willard and Capt. Carpenter. Master: Capt. J. M. Carpenter(d). Rebuilt in 1865. One report says she was lost on Lake Ontario. *Shows on her initial enrollment documents as a “2-mast barque!”

name: NAOMI
other_names: built as steamer WISCONSIN; renamed NAOMI,1899; renamed E.G. CROSBY(1909), GEN. ROBERT M. O’REILLY(1918), PILGRIM(1920), WISCONSIN(qv) again in 1924.
detail: She caught fire offshore and produced a very dramatic conflagration in the night. A number of vessels stood by to help, but she still burned to a shell with loss of life. Owner: Crosby Transportation Co. Master: Capt. Thos. Triall. She was rebuilt the next year and operated until 1929.
media_notes: Image from GLMD Image as wreck, same source

name: NAPANEE
detail: Destroyed by fire. Maybe same vessel as BYTOWN (qv)

name: NAPOLEAN
place_of_loss: Western edge of Burlington Bay, near Hamilton, Ont.
detail: She was driven ashore by a gale, perhaps later burned to total loss. Out of Dundas, Ont.

name: NAPOLEON
type_at_loss: propeller, wood, built as a schooner [also shown as a sidewheeler]
detail: She had gone to the assistance of the schooner DREADNAUGHT, but went ashore and bent her rudder shoe, disabling her engine. Helpless, she went on a reef and pounded to pieces. Her engine, boilers and gear were salvaged in the fall of 1858 and sold at Detroit. Also reported as lost on Lake Erie in 1855. Converted to a passenger steamer about 1850.

name: NAPOLEON
place_of_loss: at Pierport, 15 mi S of Frankfort, Mich.
detail: Caught in a storm off Pierport, she was leaking heavily, out of control and in danger of foundering when the lifesaving service crew in Frankfort was called. By the time the crew had towed their boat through the storm the fifteen miles behind the tug HALL, NAPOLEON had gone ashore and her crew was safe. She was thought to be lost in another storm earlier in the same month and at that time was said to be “crumbling to pieces with rot, she would be fortunate if she had safely made the trip in fair weather.” On the return trip, the lifesaving crew lost their lifeboat when the bow was jerked out of it, and the tug was almost wrecked as well. Master: Capt. Knudson. NAPOLEON was out of Milwaukee and owned by Hanson. She had changed hands at least 20 times in her career. Rebuilt in 1869

name: NARRAGANSETT
detail: Her old hull sprang a leak and the vessel waterlogged. She was abandoned by her crew, but the steamer ADVANCE later picked her up and towed her for 3 hours before she sank 11 mi off Goderich, Ont. She had been bound Detour for Port Huron for repairs. Crew landed at Harbor Beach the 14th. Owner: John Leisch, Milwaukee. Also reported wrecked near Hammond Bay, L. Huron, in the great gale of 1872. She was on her first lakes trip after a five-year venture on the Atlantic seacoast. major repair in 1880

name: NASHUA
build_info: 1868, Lafrinier, Cleveland, as a passenger/package freighter
place_of_loss: near Bayfield, Ont., S end of the lake
detail: Bound Byng Inlet for Toledo with barge C.C. RYAN in tow, she capsized in a huge gale after her engine failed. The RYAN stood by, but was helpless to assist. Soon after, the steam barge WM. H. GRATWICK arrived on the scene, but found no trace of the vessel. NASHUA drifted ashore near Bayfield 4 days later, upside-down with her stern and machinery gone. Speculation at the time was that her boiler came loose in heavy seas and tore the rear of her hull off. Owned by Sturdevant Lumber Co. Cleveland. Master: Capt. Richard Millen(d).

name: NASSAU
detail: She collided in a storm with the whaleback steamer JOSEPH L. COLBY and began to sink. The COLBY took most of her crew of and towed her into shallower water, where she sank. She had been bound Detroit for Buffalo and was coming to anchor against the gale when she was run down by the steamer. Owned and sailed by Capt. John Dorrington, Trenton, Mich.

name: MARY NAU
other_names: none often seen as MARY NAN
place_of_loss: S of Harbor entrance at Grand Haven, MI
detail: Bound Whitehall, MI, for Chicago, she drove ashore in a gale. Grand Haven Lifesaving Service rescued her crew, but she broke up the next day. Out of Chicago; Harmon Oleson, Owner

name: NAUTILUS
place_of_loss: Chicago Harbor, off the foot of Lake St.
detail: She touched a bar and was holed while trying to avoid 3 anchored schooners that were partially blocking the harbor entrance. She sank just off the harbor and was not much damaged, but tugs were unable to release her. A storm about ten days later tore her to pieces. Master: Capt. Hackett. Owned by Rounds, Hudson & Ranney, Chicago. One reliable source states that she was the old sloop shown in parentheses, but newspaper clippings of 1854 call her a schooner. One sailor died when she capsized in a storm 50 mi above Erie, PA, Sep 18, 1817.

name: NAUTILUS
detail: Heavily damaged by fire and abandoned, she was later recovered, back in service the next year. Listed in 1892 mv, hgl as a yacht. Abandoned in 1933.

name: JAMES NAVAGH
detail: Bound Milwaukee for Oswego, she struck north of Twin River Pt. and broke in two, a total loss. The survivors of her crew spent a miserable night on her bowsprit and were later rescued by local residents in two Mackinaw boats from shore, but her cook later died of exposure. Master: Capt. Griffin. NAVAGH’s rigging was later salvaged and used in the schooner LOUISA McDONALD (see LILY E.). She went ashore near Presque Islae, Mich [L. Huron] on her maiden voyage in late Nov, 1857. She wasn’t released until May of 1858. Fifteen days before this accident her mate was killed by a piece of gear breaking in the Welland Canal. Ashore and expected to break up on Middle Island, L. Huron, in October, 1864.

name: NAVAJO
other_names: built as the steamer KING BEN, renamed in 1904
place_of_loss: S side of Main Duck Isl.
detail: She was driven ashore and wrecked while trying to free the stranded steamer CEYLON. Owned out of Kingston. Major repair after a fire in 1896; rebuilt, 1905
media_notes: Image as a wreck from GLMD

name: NAVARINO
detail: This brand new vessel was lying at a dock when the city of Chicago was destroyed by its great fire. She tried to pull away, but the wind drawing into the fire held her against the dock. She burned to a total loss. Owned by Goodrich Line and valued at about $50,000. Her machinery was later salvaged and used in the new prop MENOMINEE (later IOWA), which replaced NAVARINO. The remains of the hull may also have been recovered and rebuilt to a barge or lighter. She was built with an “oscillating” engine, but it proved a failure and was removed in October of her first season, just after her trials.

name: NEBRASKA
detail: This brig was run into on a foggy night by the Western Transportation line propeller ORIENTAL and sunk “in the bay,” near the wrecks of the two steamers SEBASTOPOL (qv) and ALLEHENY (qv). A judge later decided the prop was at fault for not taking action to avoid the sail vessel. Owned by W. Hibbard & J. Stewart, Chicago.

name: NEEBING
other_names: built as JOHN B. KETCHAM, 2nd; named GREENLAND in 1922, COALHURST in 1927, NEEBING the same year
place_of_loss: 800 yds off Eagle’s Nest Pt, Nipigon Strait
detail: She filled and sank in a gale while towing the barge COTEAU. She had been bound Paradise Island for Red Rock, Ont. Lies in 100′ of water. 1st steel vessel built especially for the lumber trade. Sold Canadian, 1913. She had many wrecks and mishaps in her long and busy career.

name: G.M. NEELON
other_names: noted at launch as GEORGE MORTIMER NEELON
build_info: 1873, S. Andrews & Son, Port Dalhousie, Ont.
detail: She broke from the tow of the steamer S.L. TILLEY and struck the rocks in heavy seas. She was abandoned to her underwriters, but was recovered by American wrecker J.H. Gillett in November of 1893. Canadian National Archives shows her wrecked and total loss on Lake Superior [no specifics], on Nov 29, 1897, but that may just be the date her document was surrendererd. By 1898 she was the American hull MANITOU (US#92568, qv)

name: HARVEY NEELON
detail: She burned to a total loss while her crew escaped in yawl. The cause of the fire was unknown. Reportedly partially iron framed. Owned by Michael Lemoux of Midland, Ont.

name: M.C. NEFF
detail: She caught fire while unloading and burned to a total loss. Owned by Thompson & Lavaque Lumber Co. Lies about 500 ft S of the Interstate Transfer RR bridge, 50 ft from E shore.

name: S. NEFF
other_names: sometimes seen as SAMUEL NEFF and SAM NEFF
build_info: 1881, Samuel J. Neff or James Ryan, Oshkosh, WI [on Lake Winnebago]
detail: The NEFF burned to a total loss at her dock. She may have been Canadian at time of loss, but owner is reported as John Corrigan, Cleveland. Driven on breakwater at Cleveland by storm, Aug 11, 1892. Heavily damaged & declared total loss, but later rebuilt. Built as a rafting tug and packet, her engine and boiler came from the large rafting tug AJAX.

name: SIDNEY O. NEFF
other_names: named M.C. & M.C. No. 2 from 1920 to 24, then returned to her original name
build_info: 1890, Burger, Manitowoc, WI as a schooner-barge
place_of_loss: mouth of Menominee R., Menominee, MI
detail: She sprang a leak at the mouth of the river and sank, then was abandoned in place. The wreck is located .35 mi SE of Menominee S. pierhead light. Some sources say she was abandoned at that spot. Converted from schooner-barge to bulk freighter in 1896.

name: NEGAUNEE
build_info: 1867, I. Nicholas, Vermilion, OH as 3-mast schooner
detail: Tow of steamer CITY oF CONCORD (which sank as well), she went on reef in a gale and was heavily damaged. Before she could be released, another storm on the 30th pushed her off the reef and into deep water. Owned out of Cleveland by Bradley fleet. Sunk in a collision with the schooner E. M. PORTCH near Kewaunee, Wis., in 1880.

name: NEKICK
other_names: also seen as NAKICK, later renamed FLORA
place_of_loss: just W of Cape Vincent, NY
detail: Bound Oswego and Sackett’s Harbor for St. Vincent, she capsized in a squall with the loss of all hands. She was found floating keel up in the St. Lawrence the next day and was towed into St. Vincent. Out of Sacket’s Harbor or Oswego. Master: Capt. Sias(d).

name: ELSIE NELL
detail: Burned to a total loss in Pigeon Cove. Owned by William Miller, Bayfield, Wis. Out of Duluth.

name: NELLIE
detail: No detail. Document surrendered at Port Huron Dec 12, 1881, annotated “wrecked in September, 1878.” Out of Banks, MI.

name: NELLIE
place_of_loss: 1/4 mile W of Port Sherman, MI
detail: Bound Holland for Charlevoix, MI., she capsized in Muskegon harbor entrance while trying to enter in a gale. Her two-man crew* was swept away and drowned quickly, even though the Lifesaving Service and several tugs rushed to her assistance. *Newspaper articles also say the crew consisted of a man and two boys.

name: NELLIE MAY
detail: Stranded and abandoned at Harbor Beach. Abandoned off Lexington, Mich, in Sep, 1899, when her crew thought she would sink. She was found later, floating merrily at anchor.

name: NELLIE TERESA
detail: She was caught in a gale and wrecked at a pier. She had been bound Oswego for Hamilton and was a total loss of $5,000. Hailed from Napanee, Ont. A steamer (tug?) called ELIZA BONAR shows in Canadian records with the same official number, builder information and port of hail. She was burned Nov 15, 1891, near Belleville, Ont. Her remains were then hauled out at Belleville and dismantled.

name: NELSON
build_info: 1866, Ellsworth & Davidson, Milwaukee as a 3-mast schooner or bark
detail: Tow of steamer A. FOLSOM(qv), she broke her towline in a gale, went to pieces and sank offshore in 300 feet of water. The vessels had been unable to manage the harbor entrance at Grand Marais, and tugs would not risk the storm to bring them in. The captain was the only survivor; his wife and child perished along with the crew when the schooner went to the bottom with the yawl and its occupants still in its davits. Floating on the roof of the cabin he came ashore at precisely the same spot that the sole survivor ot the sunken steamer WESTERN RESERVE (qv) had several years before. Owned by Mitchell Transportation Co. of Bay City. Master: Capt. Andrew Haghney was the only survivor. Ashore behind Grand Island, Munising, Mich. and almost wrecked in October of 1895. Rebuilt, enlarged [36 ft. longer, 2 ft wider, 2 ft deeper] in 1887.
